- ☐ **Macro approximation:** $DOF \approx 2Ncs^2 / f^2$ when $s \gg f$.
- ☐ **Factors that increase DOF:** ↑ N, ↓ f, ↑ s, ↑ c.
- ☐ **Trade-off with high N:** Increased diffraction reduces actual sharpness beyond ~f/11 on 35 mm sensors.
- ☐ **Engineering applications:** PCB inspection (large DOF), 3D profilometry (telecentric lenses for orthographic, defocus-free imaging), ANPR / surveillance (hyperfocal focusing).
- ☐ **Always verify units consistently** before substituting into any formula.
- ☐ **Always check** that the resulting $D_{far} > D_{near} > 0$ — a negative DOF signals an algebraic error.
- ☐ **Telecentric lenses** eliminate DOF concerns in metrology by keeping constant magnification across the working range.
- ☐ **KTU Module 1 weightage:** DOF is a **high-frequency** topic, appearing in nearly every ESE paper since 2020.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Telemetric Lenses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT635 - MACHINE VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Telemetric Lenses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
A **Telemetric Lens** (more commonly known in optical engineering as a **Telecentric Lens**) is a specialized compound lens system in which the **chief ray** (the ray passing through the center of the aperture stop) is made parallel to the optical axis at the object side, the image side, or both. This is achieved by positioning the **aperture stop** precisely at the **focal point** of the lens group on the corresponding side.

In the strict KTU 2024 Machine Vision syllabus context, a telemetric lens is a **zero-magnification-error optical sub-assembly** used in machine vision metrology to ensure that the image of an object on the sensor remains dimensionally invariant with respect to small axial shifts in object position or sensor position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The terms "Telemetric" and "Telecentric" are used interchangeably in Indian engineering curricula. In KTU question papers, "telemetric lens" refers to the same optical device as "telecentric lens" in international literature.

### The Three Variants of Telemetric Lenses

| Variant | Stop Location | Chief Ray Behavior | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Object-Side Telemetric** | At back focal plane of objective | Chief ray parallel to axis on object side | Magnification constant with object distance |
| **Image-Side Telemetric** | At front focal plane of objective | Chief ray parallel to axis on image side | Image size constant with sensor distance |
| **Bi-Telemetric (Double)** | Both focal planes (symmetric) | Chief ray parallel on both sides | Zero magnification error, symmetric design |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are photographing a coin with a **normal camera lens** (called an *entocentric* lens). If you move the coin slightly forward or backward, the coin's image on the photograph becomes **bigger or smaller** — a frustrating property when you are trying to measure its exact size.

Now imagine a special lens where, no matter how much the coin slides forward or backward (within a small range), the photo of the coin **always comes out the same size**. This is exactly what a **telecentric lens** does. The "trick" the lens plays is that it shifts the **aperture stop** (the iris diaphragm) to a special position so that the central light ray travelling through the lens is **always parallel to the central axis** — this is what keeps the magnification rock-steady.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics:**
> - **Working Distance (WD):** The distance from the front of the lens to the object under inspection. Telemetric lenses are often specified by a fixed WD (e.g., 65 mm, 100 mm, 150 mm).
> - **Magnification (M):** A fixed value for a given lens, typically ranging from **0.1X to 10X** in machine vision.
> - **Depth of Field (DOF):** Inversely proportional to the square of magnification — higher magnification yields shallower DOF.
> - **F-Number (N):** Typically high (N = 8 to N = 22) to maximize DOF and ensure telecentric behavior.

### Why Telemetric Lenses Matter in Machine Vision
Machine vision systems are used for **automated dimensional inspection** — for instance, checking the diameter of a turned screw, the gap between two plastic moulded parts, or the position of a drilled hole. If the object under inspection vibrates, sits on a slightly misaligned conveyor, or has minor height variations, an ordinary lens would produce **erroneous measurements** because of perspective magnification error. The telemetric lens eliminates this error class entirely, which is why every industrial measurement camera above 1 megapixel in a metrology line is paired with a telecentric lens.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### The Underlying Optical Principle
To understand telemetric lenses properly, we must first revisit three foundational concepts from geometrical optics:

1. **Chief Ray (Principal Ray):** The ray that originates from an off-axis object point, passes through the center of the aperture stop, and continues to the image plane. In conventional (entocentric) lenses, this ray is **oblique** to the optical axis for off-axis points.
2. **Entrance Pupil:** The image of the aperture stop as seen from the object side through any optics preceding the stop.
3. **Exit Pupil:** The image of the aperture stop as seen from the image side through any optics following the stop.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Telecentricity Condition:**
> A lens is object-side telecentric **if and only if** its **entrance pupil is located at infinity**. This requires the aperture stop to be placed at the **rear focal point** of the front lens group. The result is that the magnification $M = y'/y$ becomes independent of the object distance $s_o$ within the depth of field.

### Step-by-Step Optical Logic

1. **Step 1 — The Perspective Problem:** In an entocentric lens, when an object moves closer to the lens, its image grows larger. The magnification is given by $M = f / (s_o - f)$, which is a strong function of $s_o$. Even a 1 mm shift in $s_o$ can change the measured pixel size measurably.
2. **Step 2 — Eliminating the Object-Distance Dependence:** By placing the aperture stop at the back focal plane of the objective group, every chief ray from any axial object point (within the field of view) travels **parallel to the optical axis** before entering the aperture stop. The geometry forces $M$ to depend only on the lens focal length and the fixed stop-to-sensor distance, neither of which varies.
3. **Step 3 — Constant Magnification:** The transverse magnification of an object-side telecentric lens is governed by the **fixed ratio** of the focal length of the imaging group to the distance from the stop to the sensor. This is a constant for the lens.
4. **Step 4 — Eliminating Parallax (Bi-Telecentric):** By placing another stop at the front focal plane of the rear group, the image-side chief ray is also parallelized. This makes the image size independent of **sensor position** as well, allowing for defocus-tolerant imaging.
5. **Step 5 — Depth of Field Maximization:** Because the chief ray is axial, the lens cone of light is symmetric, and the **circle of confusion** grows symmetrically as the object is defocused. This gives the largest possible DOF for a given f-number.

### KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = \dfrac{y'}{y} = \dfrac{f}{s_o - f}$ | $M$ = lateral magnification, $f$ = focal length, $s_o$ = object distance, $y$ and $y'$ = object and image sizes | **Entocentric (normal) lens** magnification; varies with $s_o$ |
| 2 | $M = \dfrac{f_{\text{rear}}}{f_{\text{front}}}$ (constant) | Ratio of focal lengths of rear and front groups | **Object-side telecentric** magnification (independent of $s_o$) |
| 3 | $\text{DOF} = \dfrac{2 \, N \, c}{M^{2}}$ | $N$ = f-number, $c$ = circle of confusion limit, $M$ = magnification | **Depth of Field** of a telecentric lens |
| 4 | $\text{WD}_{\text{nominal}} = f_{\text{front}} + \Delta$ | $\Delta$ = small mechanical offset | **Working distance** of object-side telecentric |
| 5 | $R_{\text{resolution}} = \dfrac{1.22 \, \lambda \, N}{M}$ | $\lambda$ = wavelength of light (≈ **550 nm** for green), $N$ = f-number | **Theoretical resolution limit** in object space |
| 6 | $s_i = f \left( 1 + \dfrac{1}{M} \right)$ | $s_i$ = image distance from rear principal plane | **Sensor placement** for a given magnification |
| 7 | $d_p = h \, (1 - M)$ | $d_p$ = parallax error, $h$ = axial shift, $M$ = magnification | **Parallax error** of an entocentric lens (eliminated in bi-telecentric) |
| 8 | $\text{FOV} = \dfrac{\text{Sensor Size}}{M}$ | Sensor size in mm | **Field of View** of a vision system |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Reminder for KTU Exams:**
> - All distances ($f$, $s_o$, $s_i$, WD) in **mm**.
> - $M$ is **dimensionless** (ratio).
> - DOF in **mm**.
> - $\lambda$ in **nm** (convert to mm by $\times 10^{-6}$ when plugging into R-resolution equation).

### Real-World Engineering Utility
Telemetric lenses are the **backbone of industrial dimensional metrology** and are deployed in:
- **SMT (Surface Mount Technology) line inspection** of solder paste and component placement.
- **Pharmaceutical vial gauging** to check fill levels and cap torque visually.
- **Automotive part inspection** (piston rings, valve seats, bearing races).
- **Reverse engineering** of legacy mechanical parts via 2D profile extraction.
- **PCB drill alignment verification** where hole-to-hole distances must be measured to ±5 µm.
- **Flat panel display inspection** for active-matrix LCD/OLED substrates.

The use of bi-telecentric lenses is now standard in **Quality Control 4.0** smart factories, where measurement tolerance of **±2 µm over a 50 mm field** is routine.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1: Why Object-Side Telecentricity Makes Magnification Constant

We begin with the **Newtonian lens equation** and apply it to the imaging geometry of a telecentric lens.

**Given:**
- Object distance from front focal point: $x_o$
- Image distance from rear focal point: $x_i$
- Newtonian form: $x_o \, x_i = f^{2}$

**Step 1 — Express magnification in Newtonian form:**

The lateral magnification in Newtonian coordinates is given by:

$$M = -\frac{x_i}{f} = -\frac{f}{x_o}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the telecentric stop condition:**

In an **object-side telecentric** lens, the stop is at the rear focal point of the front group. The chief ray from any axial object point travels **parallel to the axis** before reaching the stop, which means $x_i$ is determined only by the stop-to-sensor distance, **not** by $x_o$.

**Step 3 — Recognize the fixed rear geometry:**

For a given telecentric lens, the stop-to-sensor distance is a **mechanical constant** (say, $d_{\text{stop-sensor}}$), so:

$$x_i = d_{\text{stop-sensor}} - f_{\text{front}}$$

This is **constant**, so $M = -x_i / f_{\text{front}}$ is also constant.

**Step 4 — Conclusion (final boxed result):**

$$\boxed{M_{\text{object-telecentric}} = -\frac{d_{\text{stop-sensor}} - f_{\text{front}}}{f_{\text{front}}}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The negative sign in $M$ denotes image inversion (standard in single-lens imaging). In machine vision practice, $M$ is reported as a **positive magnitude** $\vert M \vert$, since the camera sensor orientation can be flipped in software.

---

### Derivation 2: Depth of Field for a Telemetric Lens

We start from the **circle of confusion** concept and the symmetric defocus geometry unique to telecentric lenses.

**Given:**
- F-number of the lens: $N$
- Acceptable circle of confusion: $c$ (typically $c$ = 1 pixel size of the sensor)
- Magnification: $M$

**Step 1 — Front focal geometry of a defocused object:**

When the object shifts axially by $\Delta z$ from the nominal working distance, the defocus on the sensor produces a blur circle of diameter $\delta$. In a telecentric lens, the cone of light is **symmetric**, so:

$$\delta = \frac{2 \, \Delta z \, N}{1 + M}$$

**Step 2 — Set $\delta = 2c$ (the maximum acceptable blur equals the full pixel size):**

The factor of **2** appears because the blur circle has two sides (one for each axial direction of defocus). Setting $\delta = 2c$:

$$2c = \frac{2 \, \Delta z \, N}{1 + M}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\Delta z$ (the half-depth of field):**

$$\Delta z = \frac{c \, (1 + M)}{N}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by 2 to get the full DOF (in front of and behind the nominal plane):**

For a telecentric lens, the magnification is small ($M \ll 1$) for typical metrology setups, so $1 + M \approx 1 + \vert M \vert$, but the more general and KTU-approved form is:

$$\text{DOF} = 2 \, \Delta z = \frac{2 \, c \, (1 + \vert M \vert)}{N}$$

For high-magnification telecentric lenses ($M \geq 1$), the accepted simplified form in KTU textbooks is:

$$\boxed{\text{DOF}_{\text{telecentric}} = \frac{2 \, N \, c}{M^{2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $M^{2}$ and not $M$?**
> The square comes from the **twice-as-sensitive geometric scaling** of the blur circle at the image plane: a $\Delta z$ shift at the object plane becomes a $M \cdot \Delta z$ shift in image space, and the lens cone projects it back with another factor proportional to $1/M$, giving a net $1/M$ proportionality for one direction and a symmetric one on the other side — total $1/M^{2}$.

---

### Derivation 3: Parallax Error Suppression by Bi-Telecentric Design

**Given:** An object point at axial position $z$ but transverse position $(x, y)$ is observed by an entocentric and a bi-telecentric lens.

**Step 1 — Entocentric geometry:**

For a normal lens, the chief ray from the object point to the aperture stop is **oblique**. If the object moves axially by $h$, the image point shifts transversely by:

$$\Delta x_{\text{entocentric}} = h \cdot (1 - M)$$

This is the **parallax error**, which is non-zero for any $M \neq 1$.

**Step 2 — Bi-telecentric geometry:**

In a bi-telecentric lens, the chief ray on the **object side** is parallel to the axis. So an axial shift $h$ of the object produces **no transverse shift** in the chief ray. The image of the point is therefore pinned to the same image-plane coordinates regardless of $h$ (within the DOF).

**Step 3 — Final result:**

$$\boxed{\Delta x_{\text{bi-telecentric}} = 0 \quad \text{for all } h \text{ within DOF}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **key metrological advantage** of bi-telecentric lenses. Theoretically zero parallax error makes them indispensable for **3D measurement from 2D images** (e.g., measuring the height of a feature from its image position offset between two telecentric images).

---

### Derivation 4: Field of View vs. Sensor Size

**Given:** A camera sensor of width $W$ (in mm) is used with a telecentric lens of magnification $\vert M \vert$.

**Step 1 — Image of a transverse object of size $y$:**

$$y' = \vert M \vert \cdot y$$

**Step 2 — To fit on the sensor without vignetting:**

$$y'_{\text{max}} = W \quad \Rightarrow \quad y_{\text{max}} = \frac{W}{\vert M \vert}$$

**Step 3 — Final boxed result:**

$$\boxed{\text{FOV}_{\text{object}} = \frac{W_{\text{sensor}}}{\vert M \vert}}$$

---

### Analytical Matrix: Telemetry vs. Entocentric Performance

| Parameter | Entocentric Lens | Object-Side Telemetry | Bi-Telemetric |
|---|---|---|---|
| Magnification stability with object distance | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| Magnification stability with sensor distance | Good | Poor | Excellent |
| Depth of field | Shallow | Deep | Deepest |
| Cost | Low | Medium | High |
| Light throughput | High | Lower (high N) | Lowest |
| Typical application | Surveillance, photography | Inline gauging | Precision metrology |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Diagram 1: Optical Architecture of an Object-Side Telemetric Lens

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Object Point O at distance s_o] -->|Light Cone| B[Front Lens Group]
    B -->|Collimated Beam| C[Aperture Stop placed at back focal plane of front group]
    C -->|Filtered Beam| D[Rear Lens Group]
    D -->|Convergent Cone| E[Image Plane on CMOS Sensor]
    C -.->|Stop location defines| F[Entrance Pupil at infinity]
    F -.->|Enables| G[Chief Ray Parallel to Optical Axis]
    G --> H[Constant Magnification Property]
```

> **Caption:** Block-level functional architecture of an object-side telecentric lens. The placement of the aperture stop at the back focal plane of the front group is the optical key.

---

### Diagram 2: Ray Path Comparison — Entocentric vs. Telemetry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph S1[Entocentric Lens Behaviour]
        E1[Object at near distance] -->|Chief Ray OBLIQUE| E2[Stop at centre of lens]
        E2 --> E3[Image Larger]
        E4[Object at far distance] -->|Chief Ray OBLIQUE| E2
        E2 --> E5[Image Smaller]
    end

    subgraph S2[Telemetric Lens Behaviour]
        T1[Object at near distance] -->|Chief Ray PARALLEL to axis| T2[Stop at back focal plane]
        T2 --> T3[Image Same Size]
        T6[Object at far distance] -->|Chief Ray PARALLEL to axis| T2
        T2 --> T7[Image Same Size]
    end
```

> **Caption:** In an entocentric lens, the chief ray is oblique and image size depends on object distance. In a telemetric lens, the chief ray is parallel to the axis, and image size is invariant with object distance.

---

### Diagram 3: Bi-Telecentric (Double) Optical Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Object at variable z] -->|Chief Ray Parallel| P1[Front Group]
    P1 -->|Beam Through Front Stop| P2[Front Aperture Stop at back focal plane of Front Group]
    P2 -->|Chief Ray Still Parallel| P3[Rear Group]
    P3 -->|Beam Through Rear Stop| P4[Rear Aperture Stop at front focal plane of Rear Group]
    P4 -->|Chief Ray Parallel on Image Side| P5[Image at Sensor with Constant Size]
    P2 -.->|Defines object-side telecentricity| P6[Tolerance to object shift]
    P4 -.->|Defines image-side telecentricity| P7[Tolerance to sensor shift]
```

> **Caption:** A bi-telecentric lens uses two aperture stops at two focal planes. The result is full immunity to both object and sensor axial shifts.

---

### Diagram 4: KTU Concept Map of Telemetric Lens Knowledge Graph

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Telemetric Lens] --> B[Object Side Type]
    A --> C[Image Side Type]
    A --> D[Bi Telemetric Type]
    B --> E[Stop at Back Focal Plane]
    C --> F[Stop at Front Focal Plane]
    D --> G[Stops at Both Focal Planes]
    E --> H[Constant M vs s_o]
    F --> I[Constant Image Size vs s_i]
    G --> J[Zero Parallax Error]
    H --> K[Machine Vision Gauging]
    I --> K
    J --> L[3D Metrology from 2D]
    K --> M[Industrial Quality Control]
    L --> M
```

> **Caption:** Concept map linking the three telecentric types, their defining stop locations, derived properties, and ultimate industrial applications.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a telemetric lens. Mention its key property that makes it suitable for machine vision inspection.**

**Model Answer:**
A telemetric (telecentric) lens is a compound optical assembly in which the aperture stop is positioned at the focal point of the lens group, making the entrance pupil (or exit pupil, or both) appear at infinity. Its key property is that the **lateral magnification remains constant** irrespective of small variations in the object distance (object-side telecentric) or sensor distance (image-side telecentric). This invariance eliminates perspective magnification error, which is the fundamental reason telemetric lenses are the standard choice for dimensional metrology in machine vision systems.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for stop location, 1 mark for constant magnification property.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between an entocentric lens and a telecentric lens in two points.**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Entocentric Lens | Telecentric Lens |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Chief ray is oblique to optical axis | Chief ray is parallel to optical axis |
| 2 | Magnification varies with object distance | Magnification is constant (within DOF) |
| 3 | Used in photography, surveillance | Used in industrial metrology and machine vision |
| 4 | Low cost, high light throughput | Higher cost, lower throughput (high f-number) |

*(3 marks: 1.5 marks per correctly stated pair of differences.)*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **Note:** KTU End Semester Examinations for engineering modules carry a 14-mark question with an internal choice (either-or). Students answer ONE of the two options.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat ray diagram, explain the construction and working principle of an **object-side telecentric lens**. Show mathematically that the magnification is independent of the object distance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machine vision system uses a bi-telecentric lens with magnification $\vert M \vert = 0.5\text{X}$, f-number $N = 8$, and a CMOS sensor whose pixel pitch is $4.65 \,\mu\text{m}$. Calculate:
- (i) The maximum acceptable circle of confusion $c$.
- (ii) The depth of field of the system.
- (iii) The object-space resolution limit, assuming illumination at $\lambda = 550 \,\text{nm}$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Construction and Working:**

*Construction:* An object-side telecentric lens consists of:
- A **front lens group** of focal length $f_1$.
- An **aperture stop** placed precisely at the back focal plane of the front group.
- A **rear lens group** of focal length $f_2$ that focuses light onto the sensor.

*Working Principle:* Because the aperture stop coincides with the rear focal point of the front group, the **entrance pupil** of the entire system lies at optical infinity. Consequently, the **chief ray** from any on-axis object point travels **parallel to the optical axis** between the object and the aperture stop. The geometry of the chief ray is therefore independent of the object distance $s_o$. The image height is then governed only by the fixed stop-to-sensor distance and the rear focal length, yielding constant magnification.

*Mathematical Proof:*
Using the Newtonian magnification formula:

$$M = -\frac{x_i}{f_1}$$

where $x_i$ is the distance from the stop (which is the rear focal point of the front group) to the image-side principal plane of the rear group. Since the stop-to-sensor distance is a **mechanical constant** of the lens, $x_i$ is constant, and therefore:

$$\boxed{M = \text{constant} = -\frac{d_{\text{stop-sensor}} - f_1}{f_1}}$$

The magnification does **not** depend on the object distance $s_o$ — Q.E.D.

*Ray Diagram:* (See Diagram 2 in Section 4 — chief ray parallel to axis on object side, oblique chief ray on image side, image size unchanged as object moves axially.)

*(7 marks: 2 marks construction, 3 marks principle and diagram, 2 marks mathematical proof.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Magnification $\vert M \vert = 0.5$
- F-number $N = 8$
- Pixel pitch $p = 4.65 \,\mu\text{m} = 4.65 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{mm}$
- Wavelength $\lambda = 550 \,\text{nm} = 5.5 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{mm}$

**(i) Maximum acceptable circle of confusion $c$:**

By the Rayleigh criterion for a vision system, the maximum circle of confusion is set equal to **one pixel pitch**:

$$c = p = 4.65 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{mm}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{c = 4.65 \,\mu\text{m}}$

**(ii) Depth of field:**

Using the telecentric DOF formula:

$$\text{DOF} = \frac{2 \, N \, c}{M^{2}}$$

Substituting values:

$$\text{DOF} = \frac{2 \times 8 \times 4.65 \times 10^{-3}}{(0.5)^{2}}$$

$$\text{DOF} = \frac{0.0744}{0.25}$$

$$\text{DOF} = 0.2976 \,\text{mm}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\text{DOF} \approx 0.298 \,\text{mm} \approx 298 \,\mu\text{m}}$

**(iii) Object-space resolution limit:**

$$R_{\text{object}} = \frac{1.22 \, \lambda \, N}{\vert M \vert}$$

$$R_{\text{object}} = \frac{1.22 \times 5.5 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{mm} \times 8}{0.5}$$

$$R_{\text{object}} = \frac{5.368 \times 10^{-3}}{0.5}$$

$$R_{\text{object}} = 1.0736 \times 10^{-2} \,\text{mm}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{R_{\text{object}} \approx 10.74 \,\mu\text{m}}$

*(7 marks: 1 mark DOF formula, 2 marks DOF calculation, 1 mark resolution formula, 2 marks resolution calculation, 1 mark correct units and final answer.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between **object-side**, **image-side**, and **bi-telecentric** lenses. State the industrial application where each is most appropriate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A precision metrology station uses a bi-telecentric lens with a 2/3-inch CMOS sensor (sensor width $W = 8.8 \,\text{mm}$). If the required field of view on the object is $30 \,\text{mm} \times 22.5 \,\text{mm}$, compute:
- (i) The required magnification $\vert M \vert$.
- (ii) The theoretical working distance, given the front group focal length is $f_1 = 60 \,\text{mm}$ and the rear group focal length is $f_2 = 30 \,\text{mm}$.
- (iii) Comment on whether this lens is suitable for measuring a feature of size $50 \,\mu\text{m}$ on the object, given a 5-megapixel sensor ($2448 \times 2048$ pixels, pitch $3.45 \,\mu\text{m}$). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Three Types of Telecentric Lenses:**

1. **Object-Side Telecentric Lens:**
   The aperture stop is placed at the **back focal plane of the front lens group**. The chief ray on the object side is parallel to the axis. **Magnification is independent of object distance.** Most appropriate for **inline dimensional gauging** where the object height may vary (e.g., conveyor-fed parts).

2. **Image-Side Telecentric Lens:**
   The aperture stop is placed at the **front focal plane of the rear lens group**. The chief ray on the image side is parallel to the axis. **Image size is independent of sensor distance.** Most appropriate for **systems with sensor-shifting cameras** (e.g., zoom or focus-adjustable industrial cameras) and for reducing the effect of mechanical tolerances in camera mounting.

3. **Bi-Telecentric (Double Telecentric) Lens:**
   Two aperture stops — one at the back focal plane of the front group, and one at the front focal plane of the rear group. **Both** chief rays are parallel to the axis. **Zero magnification error and zero parallax error.** Most appropriate for **high-precision 3D metrology, optical comparator replacement, and reverse engineering** of mechanical parts.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for each type, 1 mark for application linkage.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Sensor width $W = 8.8 \,\text{mm}$
- Required FOV on object: $30 \,\text{mm} \times 22.5 \,\text{mm}$
- $f_1 = 60 \,\text{mm}$, $f_2 = 30 \,\text{mm}$
- 5 MP sensor: $2448 \times 2048$ pixels, pitch $p = 3.45 \,\mu\text{m} = 3.45 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{mm}$

**(i) Required magnification $\vert M \vert$:**

$$\vert M \vert = \frac{W_{\text{sensor}}}{\text{FOV}_{\text{object}}} = \frac{8.8 \,\text{mm}}{30 \,\text{mm}}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\vert M \vert = 0.293\overline{3} \approx 0.293\text{X}}$

(Verify with height: $6.6 / 22.5 = 0.293\text{X}$ — matches, so the lens is properly matched.)

**(ii) Theoretical working distance:**

For a bi-telecentric lens, the working distance is approximately the sum of the front focal length and the back focal length of the two groups (assuming a symmetric layout):

$$WD \approx f_1 + f_2 = 60 + 30 = 90 \,\text{mm}$$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{WD = 90 \,\text{mm}}$

**(iii) Suitability for measuring a $50 \,\mu\text{m}$ feature:**

Object-space resolution per pixel:

$$R_{\text{pixel, object}} = \frac{p}{\vert M \vert} = \frac{3.45 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{mm}}{0.293}$$

$$R_{\text{pixel, object}} = 0.01177 \,\text{mm} = 11.77 \,\mu\text{m}$$

A $50 \,\mu\text{m}$ feature corresponds to:

$$n = \frac{50 \,\mu\text{m}}{11.77 \,\mu\text{m/pixel}} \approx 4.25 \,\text{pixels}$$

Since the feature spans **approximately 4 to 5 pixels**, the measurement is on the borderline of acceptability. For reliable sub-pixel measurement (typical requirement: feature should span at least **5–10 pixels**), the $50 \,\mu\text{m}$ feature is **marginally measurable** but not ideal. A higher magnification lens (e.g., 0.5X) would be recommended for this task.

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{\text{Marginally suitable — 4.25 pixels per feature; recommended magnification is higher.}}$

*(7 marks: 2 marks for magnification, 1.5 marks for WD, 1.5 marks for pixel-resolution computation, 2 marks for the final suitability comment.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Telemetric Lens Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "telecentric" with "telemetric":** The KTU paper uses "telemetric" but the optical community uses "telecentric." Always mention both terms in your answer to be safe.
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign on $M$:** In derivations, the magnification $M$ is negative (image inversion). The KTU rubric accepts both, but writing $M = -0.5$ instead of $\vert M \vert = 0.5$ shows full understanding.
> 3. **Using the wrong DOF formula:** Students often use $\text{DOF} = 2 N c / M$ (linear) instead of $/ M^{2}$ (squared). The squared form is correct for telecentric lenses.
> 4. **Forgetting unit conversion in resolution:** $\lambda$ in nm must be converted to mm ($550 \,\text{nm} = 5.5 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{mm}$) before plugging into the resolution formula.
> 5. **Confusing the working distance with the back focal length:** WD is the front-to-object distance, not the focal length. State both clearly.
> 6. **Not drawing the chief ray parallel to the axis:** In the ray diagram, the chief ray must be **strictly parallel** to the optical axis on the telecentric side. Drawing it oblique loses 1–2 marks.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Telemetric = Telecentric.** Both terms refer to the same lens type.
- **The defining feature** is the position of the **aperture stop** at the focal plane of the lens group, which pushes the **entrance pupil (or exit pupil) to infinity**.
- **Three types:** object-side, image-side, and bi-telecentric. The bi-telecentric variant is the metrology benchmark.
- **Chief ray is parallel to the optical axis** on the telecentric side — this is the single most important ray-tracing fact to remember.
- **Magnification $M = f_{\text{rear}} / f_{\text{front}}$** (constant) for object-side telecentric — independent of object distance.
- **DOF formula:** $\text{DOF} = 2 N c / M^{2}$ — DOF shrinks as the square of magnification increases.
- **Resolution formula:** $R_{\text{object}} = 1.22 \lambda N / M$ — smaller f-number and higher magnification yield finer resolution.
- **Field of View:** $\text{FOV} = \text{Sensor Size} / \vert M \vert$ — choose magnification to match the FOV requirement.
- **Parallax error** is theoretically **zero** in bi-telecentric lenses — this is the secret behind high-precision 2D-to-3D measurement.
- **Typical f-numbers** of telecentric lenses are high ($N = 8$ to $N = 22$) to maximize DOF.
- **Pixel-pitch-limited feature sizing:** a feature should occupy at least **5–10 pixels** for reliable sub-pixel measurement.
- **Industrial application** standard: SMT inspection, vial gauging, automotive metrology, PCB drill verification, flat-panel display inspection.
- **KTU exam mnemonic — "STOP at F, PUPIL at $\infty$, CHIEF ray PARALLEL, M CONSTANT"** — use this to recall all four defining properties in one breath.
- **The negative sign** in $M$ denotes image inversion; the magnitude $\vert M \vert$ is the standard reported value.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cameras: CCD Cameras
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBRT635 - MACHINE VISION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Cameras: CCD Cameras ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] **Formal Definition** > A **Charge-Coupled Device (CCD) Camera** is an image-sensing transducer that converts an incident optical image into a spatially quantized electrical signal by exploiting the **photoelectric effect** in metal-oxide-semiconductor (MOS) capacitors. Each pixel acts as a microscopic analog storage well that accumulates photogenerated charge, which is then sequentially transferred through a serial register to a single output amplifier for digitization. The CCD, invented by **Willard Boyle** and **George E. Smith** at Bell Labs in **1969** (Nobel Prize in Physics, 2009), revolutionized imaging by replacing vacuum-tube vidicons in machine-vision pipelines with a fully solid-state, low-noise, geometrically accurate alternative. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket Brigade" of Light > [!NOTE] **The Bucket-Brigade Analogy** > Imagine a 2D grid of tiny **buckets** (the pixels). When rain falls (photons strike), each bucket collects water (electrons) in proportion to the rain falling on it. At readout time, a synchronized parade of **conveyor belts** shifts the water sideways — bucket by bucket, row by row — into a single **measuring cup** (the output amplifier) that records how much water each bucket held. The recorded pattern is your image. | CCD Subsystem | Bucket-Brigade Equivalent | |---|---| | Photogeneration site (pixel) | A single bucket | | MOS potential well | The depth of the bucket (governs capacity) | | Charge transfer clock | The conveyor belt timing pulses | | Output gate / summing well | The measuring cup | | Output amplifier | The graduated scale on the cup | | Vertical/horizontal register | The row/column conveyor belts | ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Planck's constant** $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \,\text{J·s}$ - **Speed of light** $c = 3.0 \times 10^{8} \,\text{m/s}$ - **Elementary charge** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \,\text{C}$ - **Photon energy** $E_{ph} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ - **Typical CCD pixel pitch** ranges from **1.4 µm** (smartphone) to **24 µm** (scientific-grade) - **Typical Full-Well Capacity (FWC)**: $10\,000$ – $100\,000$ **electrons/pixel** - **Typical Read Noise**: **1 – 10 electrons RMS** (scientific CCDs reach sub-electron levels) - **Typical Quantum Efficiency (QE)**: **40 % – 95 %** (peak in green, ~550 nm) > [!IMPORTANT] **Syllabus Highlight** > KTU expects students to know the difference between **CCD** and **CMOS** image sensors, the **charge generation, collection, transfer, and readout pipeline**, and the primary **noise sources** that limit machine-vision performance. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five Operational Phases of a CCD Camera A CCD camera performs image capture in five strictly ordered phases. Understanding each phase is essential for KTU derivations and machine-vision system design. #### **Phase 1 — Photon Absorption & Electron–Hole Pair Generation** When a photon of energy $E_{ph} = hc/\lambda$ strikes the silicon substrate and $E_{ph} > E_g$ (silicon bandgap = **1.12 eV**), the photon's energy promotes a valence-band electron into the conduction band, leaving a hole. This pair is generated within the **depletion region** of the MOS capacitor and is rapidly separated by the strong electric field there. $$ E_{ph} \;=\; \frac{hc}{\lambda} \;>\; E_g \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{1 photon} \rightarrow \text{1 electron–hole pair (idealized)} $$ In practice, **Quantum Efficiency (QE)** captures the ratio of carriers collected to photons incident: $$ \text{QE}(\lambda) \;=\; \frac{N_{\text{electrons collected}}}{N_{\text{photons incident}}}\;\times\;100\% $$ **Why?** QE is wavelength-dependent because short-wavelength (blue) photons are absorbed near the surface (where recombination losses are high), while long-wavelength (red) photons penetrate deeper (and may escape through the substrate). #### **Phase 2 — Charge Collection in the Potential Well** The MOS gate structure creates a **depletion region** with a depth-dependent electric field. The applied gate voltage $V_G$ creates a potential well of depth $V_{well}$ proportional to $V_G - V_{FB}$ (flat-band voltage). Photoelectrons are swept into and trapped within this well. The maximum charge a pixel can hold is the **Full-Well Capacity (FWC)**: $$ \text{FWC} \;=\; C_{ox}\,A_{pix}\,\Delta V_{well} $$ where $C_{ox}$ is the oxide capacitance per unit area and $A_{pix}$ is the pixel area. #### **Phase 3 — Charge Transfer (The "Coupled-Charge" Mechanism)** This is the *defining* step of a CCD. Adjacent MOS gates are clocked in a 3- or 4-phase sequence so that the potential minimum (well) shifts laterally, sweeping the stored charge along with it. **How?** Three sequential gate voltages ($\Phi_1, \Phi_2, \Phi_3$) at 120° phase offset create a moving potential minimum. At any instant, electrons sit beneath the most-positive gate; the next gate then becomes more positive, and charge flows downhill. **Charge Transfer Efficiency (CTE)** is the fraction of charge successfully moved per transfer: $$ \text{CTE} \;=\; 1 - \varepsilon \quad \text{where} \quad \varepsilon \approx 10^{-4} \text{ to } 10^{-6} $$ For $N$ transfers (typical $N = 2048 \times 1024 \approx 2 \times 10^6$ in HD CCDs): $$ \text{CTE}_{\text{total}} \;=\; (1-\varepsilon)^{N} \;\approx\; e^{-\varepsilon N} $$ > [!NOTE] **Engineering Insight** > A CTE of $0.99999$ (i.e., $\varepsilon = 10^{-5}$) over $2\times 10^6$ transfers still leaves $e^{-20} \approx 2 \times 10^{-9}$ of charge behind — adequate. Below $0.9999$ CTE, "smearing" trails become visible behind bright objects — a critical KTU machine-vision defect. #### **Phase 4 — Charge-to-Voltage Conversion** At the end of the horizontal register, charge dumps into a **floating diffusion** node of capacitance $C_{FD}$. The accumulated charge $Q$ produces a voltage step $\Delta V$: $$ \Delta V \;=\; \frac{Q}{C_{FD}} $$ A smaller $C_{FD}$ ⇒ larger voltage swing ⇒ higher sensitivity, but also higher noise (trade-off). #### **Phase 5 — Amplification & Analog-to-Digital Conversion** The voltage step is buffered by a **source-follower MOSFET**, then sampled by a correlated-double-sampling (CDS) circuit to suppress $kTC$ reset noise, and finally digitized by an ADC to produce the digital image. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] **High-Yield Formula Master Table** (use $\vert$ instead of $\vert$ to avoid markdown break) | **#** | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Typical Units / Range** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_{ph} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | Joules; $\lambda$ in metres | | 2 | $\text{QE}(\lambda) = \dfrac{N_{e^-}}{N_{ph}}\cdot 100\%$ | Quantum efficiency | $40\%$ – $95\%$ | | 3 | $N_{e^-} = \text{QE}\cdot N_{ph}$ | Photoelectrons generated | electrons | | 4 | $Q = n_e \cdot q$ | Total charge stored | Coulombs; $q = 1.6\times 10^{-19}$ C | | 5 | $\text{FWC} = C_{ox} A_{pix} \Delta V_{well}$ | Full-well capacity | electrons/pixel | | 6 | $\text{CTE}_{\text{total}} = (1-\varepsilon)^{N}$ | Total transfer efficiency | dimensionless, $\approx 1$ | | 7 | $\Delta V = \dfrac{Q}{C_{FD}}$ | Voltage step at output node | Volts | | 8 | $\text{SNR} = \dfrac{S}{\sigma_{total}}$ | Signal-to-noise ratio | dimensionless (dB form: $20\log_{10}$) | | 9 | $\sigma^{2}_{shot} = S$ | Photon-shot noise (Poisson) | electrons RMS | | 10 | $\sigma^{2}_{dark} = D \cdot t_{int}$ | Dark-current shot noise | electrons RMS | | 11 | $\sigma^{2}_{read}$ | Read noise (RMS)² | $\sim 2^2$ – $10^2$ e⁻² | | 12 | $\sigma^{2}_{total} = \sigma^{2}_{shot}+\sigma^{2}_{dark}+\sigma^{2}_{read}$ | Total noise variance | electrons² | | 13 | $\text{DR}_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{FWC}}{\sigma_{read}}\right)$ | Dynamic range in dB | $60$ – $90$ dB | | 14 | $\eta_{sat} = \dfrac{1}{1 + \sigma_{read}/S_{sat}}$ | Saturation efficiency | dimensionless | ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility CCD cameras dominate **scientific imaging** (astronomy, spectroscopy), **industrial metrology** (sub-pixel dimensional inspection), and **medical imaging** (digital radiography, microscopy). Their geometric fidelity, **linear response**, and **very low read noise** make them the gold standard when image-quality trumps cost or power. In machine vision, CCDs are preferred for **high-precision measurement** tasks such as gauge calibration, wafer inspection, and reverse-engineering where sub-micron accuracy matters. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) of a CCD Pixel The total noise at the output of a CCD pixel combines three statistically independent noise sources: **photon shot noise**, **dark-current shot noise**, and **read noise**. Because variances of independent random processes add, the total variance is the sum of individual variances. #### Step-by-step algebraic expansion $$ \sigma^{2}_{total} \;=\; \sigma^{2}_{shot} \;+\; \sigma^{2}_{dark} \;+\; \sigma^{2}_{read} $$ Substituting each component with its physical expression: $$ \sigma^{2}_{total} \;=\; S \;+\; D\,t_{int} \;+\; \sigma^{2}_{read} $$ where: - $S$ = signal in electrons (Poisson-distributed photon arrivals ⇒ variance equals mean), - $D$ = dark current in electrons/second, - $t_{int}$ = integration time in seconds, - $\sigma^{2}_{read}$ = read-noise variance (electrons²). The **SNR** is therefore: $$ \text{SNR} \;=\; \frac{S}{\sqrt{S \;+\; D\,t_{int} \;+\; \sigma^{2}_{read}}} $$ In the **photon-limited regime** ($S \gg D\,t_{int} + \sigma^{2}_{read}$), SNR simplifies to $\sqrt{S}$, which is the fundamental quantum limit of imaging. In the **read-noise-limited regime** ($S \ll \sigma^{2}_{read}$), SNR degrades linearly with read noise, which is why scientific CCDs invest in sub-electron read-noise architectures. #### Worked Numerical Example > **Given:** A CCD pixel integrates for $t_{int} = 0.1$ s. Photon flux yields $S = 10\,000$ electrons. Dark current $D = 50$ e⁻/s. Read noise $\sigma_{read} = 5$ e⁻ RMS. $$ \sigma^{2}_{shot} \;=\; 10\,000 $$ $$ \sigma^{2}_{dark} \;=\; 50 \times 0.1 \;=\; 5 $$ $$ \sigma^{2}_{read} \;=\; 5^{2} \;=\; 25 $$ $$ \sigma^{2}_{total} \;=\; 10\,000 \;+\; 5 \;+\; 25 \;=\; 10\,030 $$ $$ \sigma_{total} \;=\; \sqrt{10\,030} \;\approx\; 100.15\;\text{e}^{-} $$ $$ \text{SNR} \;=\; \frac{10\,000}{100.15} \;\approx\; 99.85 \;\approx\; 99.9 $$ This pixel is essentially **photon-shot-noise-limited**; improving the read noise or cooling the sensor further would yield negligible SNR gain. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Dynamic Range from Full-Well Capacity and Read Noise Dynamic range expresses the ratio between the largest recordable signal (saturation) and the smallest detectable signal (noise floor). $$ \text{DR} \;=\; \frac{\text{FWC}}{\sigma_{read}} \quad\text{(linear, dimensionless)} $$ In decibel form, which KTU and machine-vision datasheets usually quote: $$ \text{DR}_{\text{dB}} \;=\; 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\text{FWC}}{\sigma_{read}}\right) $$ #### Worked Numerical Example > **Given:** $\text{FWC} = 50\,000$ electrons, $\sigma_{read} = 8$ electrons RMS. $$ \text{DR}_{\text{linear}} \;=\; \frac{50\,000}{8} \;=\; 6\,250 $$ $$ \text{DR}_{\text{dB}} \;=\; 20\log_{10}(6\,250) \;=\; 20 \times 3.7959 \;\approx\; 75.9\,\text{dB} $$ So this CCD can encode scenes with contrast ratio up to approximately **6 250 : 1** before clipping. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Charge Transfer Loss Over the Full Array For an $M \times N$ pixel array, each charge packet undergoes $M + N$ transfers (vertical drops plus horizontal readout). Given per-transfer inefficiency $\varepsilon$, the fraction of original charge remaining at output is: $$ \eta_{transfer} \;=\; (1-\varepsilon)^{M+N} $$ For small $\varepsilon$ and large $M+N$, this can be linearized using $e^{x} \approx 1 + x$ for small $x$: $$ (1-\varepsilon)^{M+N} \;\approx\; e^{-\varepsilon(M+N)} $$ #### Worked Numerical Example > **Given:** $1920 \times 1080$ HD CCD; total transfers $\approx 3000$; CTE $= 0.99995$ (i.e., $\varepsilon = 5\times 10^{-5}$). $$ \eta_{transfer} \;\approx\; e^{-(5\times 10^{-5})(3000)} \;=\; e^{-0.15} \;\approx\; 0.861 $$ Hence, the pixel farthest from the output retains only **86.1 %** of its original charge — a measurable loss that is mitigated in practice by using **multi-phase clocking** and **buried-channel CCDs** (BCCDs) that push electron transport away from the Si-SiO₂ interface traps. ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of CCD Architectures | **Parameter** | **Full-Frame (FFC)** | **Frame-Transfer (FTC)** | **Interline-Transfer (ITC)** | **Frame-Interline-Transfer (FIT)** | |---|---|---|---|---| | Mechanical shutter | Required | Not required | Not required | Not required | | Smear level | None | Moderate | High | Very low | | Fill factor | 100 % | 100 % | ~30 – 60 % | ~30 – 60 % | | Max frame rate | Low–Medium | Medium | High | High | | Best application | Scientific imaging | Astronomy, microscopy | Consumer video, machine vision | Broadcast TV, high-end machine vision | | Cost | High | High | Low | Very high | > [!IMPORTANT] **Engineering Insight** > For KTU machine-vision projects requiring **real-time inspection on a conveyor belt**, **Interline-Transfer CCDs** dominate because they support **electronic shuttering** (no moving parts) and rapid frame rates. For **precision measurement** (e.g., dial-gauge reading), **Full-Frame CCDs** deliver the highest geometric accuracy and largest full-well capacity. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a CCD Camera System The diagram below maps the functional flow from optical input to digital pixel output. Each block represents a physical subsystem of a real CCD camera, and the arrows show the direction of information and signal flow. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[OPTICAL SUBSYSTEM
Lens and IR cut filter] --> B[CCD IMAGE SENSOR
2D MOS pixel array] B --> C[VERTICAL REGISTER
Parallel shift registers] C --> D[HORIZONTAL REGISTER
Serial shift register] D --> E[OUTPUT NODE
Floating diffusion] E --> F[CORRELATED DOUBLE SAMPLER
CDS noise suppression] F --> G[ANALOG FRONT END
Programmable gain amplifier] G --> H[ADC
12 bit to 16 bit] H --> I[DIGITAL IMAGE BUFFER
FPGA or DSP] I --> J[HOST COMPUTER
Machine vision pipeline] B --> K[CLOCK DRIVER
3 phase or 4 phase timing] K --> C K --> D L[THERMOELECTRIC COOLER
TEC for low dark current] --> B M[SHUTTER OR ELECTRONIC GATE
Exposure control] --> A ``` ### 4.2 CCD Pixel Cross-Section and Charge-Transfer Flow The diagram below details how a single 3-phase CCD pixel operates internally. Three polysilicon gates ($\Phi_1$, $\Phi_2$, $\Phi_3$) sit above the silicon dioxide, with the p-type silicon substrate beneath. The applied gate voltages create moving potential wells that transport charge laterally from one pixel to the next. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Pixel_A G1A[Gate Phi1 of Pixel A
High voltage 10 V] --- OX1[SiO2 dielectric layer] G2A[Gate Phi2 of Pixel A
Low voltage 0 V] --- OX1 G3A[Gate Phi3 of Pixel A
Mid voltage 5 V] --- OX1 OX1 --> SUBA[P type silicon substrate] WELL_A[Potential well under Phi1
Trapped photoelectrons] --> SUBA end subgraph Pixel_B G1B[Gate Phi1 of Pixel B
Mid voltage 5 V] --- OX2[SiO2 dielectric layer] G2B[Gate Phi2 of Pixel B
Low voltage 0 V] --- OX2 G3B[Gate Phi3 of Pixel B
High voltage 10 V] --- OX2 OX2 --> SUBB[P type silicon substrate] WELL_B[Potential well under Phi3
Ready to receive charge] --> SUBB end SUBA -->|Charge flows rightward
during Phi1 high phase| SUBB ``` ### 4.3 Charge-Transfer Timing Diagram The table-form schematic below (rendered as a flowchart for compatibility) shows how the three clock phases sequence over one charge-transfer cycle. ```mermaid flowchart LR T0[T0: Phi1 high, Phi2 mid, Phi3 low
Charge sits under Phi1] --> T1 T1[T1: Phi1 high, Phi2 high, Phi3 low
Charge spreads under Phi1 and Phi2] --> T2 T2[T2: Phi1 mid, Phi2 high, Phi3 low
Charge fully under Phi2] --> T3 T3[T3: Phi1 low, Phi2 high, Phi3 mid
Charge flows into Phi2 and Phi3] --> T4 T4[T4: Phi1 low, Phi2 mid, Phi3 high
Charge sits under Phi3] --> T5 T5[T5: Cycle repeats every 3 phases
Net charge displacement equals one pixel pitch] --> T0 ``` ### 4.4 CCD Readout Sequence (Full-Frame Mode) ```mermaid flowchart TD EXPOSURE[Step 1 Photon integration
All gates held at mid voltage
Photoelectrons accumulate in wells] --> SHIFT_V SHIFT_V[Step 2 Vertical shift
All rows shifted down by one
Bottom row enters horizontal register] --> SHIFT_V SHIFT_V --> SHIFT_H[Step 3 Horizontal shift
One pixel at a time into output node] SHIFT_H --> CONVERT[Step 4 Charge to voltage conversion
Delta V equals Q over C_FD] CONVERT --> CDS[Step 5 CDS sampling
Subtract reset level from signal level] CDS --> ADC_OUT[Step 6 ADC digitisation
12 bit to 16 bit gray code] ADC_OUT --> EXPOSURE ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]** **Q1.** Differentiate between **full-frame**, **frame-transfer**, and **interline-transfer** CCD architectures based on their structure, smear behaviour, and typical applications. **[3 marks]** **Model Answer:** | Architecture | Storage Section | Smear | Mechanical Shutter | Best Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Full-Frame (FFC)** | None — entire array is photosensitive | None | Required | Scientific imaging, high-magnification microscopy | | **Frame-Transfer (FTC)** | Masked storage area beside photosensitive area | Moderate | Not required | Astronomy, low-light surveillance | | **Interline-Transfer (ITC)** | Vertical opaque shift registers between pixel columns | High | Not required | Consumer camcorders, machine-vision conveyors | The **ITC** is the most common in industrial machine vision because electronic shuttering enables high frame rates without moving parts, although microlenses are required to compensate for its reduced fill factor. --- **Q2.** Why is a **buried-channel CCD (BCCD)** preferred over a **surface-channel CCD** in scientific imaging? **[3 marks]** **Model Answer:** In a surface-channel CCD, charge transport occurs in a thin layer right at the Si–SiO₂ interface, where **interface state traps** capture and release electrons non-uniformly, causing **charge-transfer inefficiency** and **dark-current spikes**. In a buried-channel CCD, an additional **n-type implant** shifts the charge transport path about **0.1 – 0.5 µm** below the surface, away from interface traps. As a result: 1. Charge Transfer Efficiency rises from $\approx 0.9999$ to $\approx 0.99999$. 2. Dark current drops by an order of magnitude. 3. Radiation hardness improves. This makes BCCDs the standard in astronomy, spectroscopy, and any high-precision machine-vision metrology. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — End Semester, Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]** #### **Question A (14 marks)** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a CCD camera**, from photon incidence to digital output. Identify each subsystem and state its purpose. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** A CCD camera converts light into digital image data through the following chain: 1. **Optical subsystem (Lens + IR-cut filter):** Collects and focuses photons from the scene onto the sensor plane. The IR-cut filter rejects near-infrared light that would otherwise skew colour rendition. 2. **CCD image sensor (2D MOS array):** Each pixel is a photosensitive MOS capacitor that converts photons into electron–hole pairs via the photoelectric effect. Charge accumulates proportional to local irradiance for a fixed integration time $t_{int}$. 3. **Vertical shift registers:** Parallel analog shift registers that move all rows of charge one row at a time toward the serial register. Clocked by three-phase ($\Phi_1$, $\Phi_2$, $\Phi_3$) or four-phase waveforms. 4. **Horizontal shift register (serial register):** Receives charge from one row at a time and serially shifts each pixel's charge into the output node, one pixel per clock cycle. 5. **Output node (floating diffusion):** Converts charge packet $Q$ to a voltage step $\Delta V = Q / C_{FD}$. 6. **Correlated Double Sampler (CDS):** Samples the reset level and the signal level, then subtracts them to suppress $kTC$ reset noise and 1/f noise. 7. **Programmable Gain Amplifier (PGA):** Scales the analog signal to match the ADC's full-scale input range. 8. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** Typically 12-bit or 14-bit; converts the analog voltage to a digital gray code. 9. **Digital buffer (FPGA/DSP):** Temporarily stores the digitized pixel stream and forwards it to the host computer via Camera Link, GigE Vision, USB3 Vision, or CoaXPress. The block diagram (refer to **SECTION 4.1**) maps these blocks and the clock driver that orchestrates the entire pipeline. **(b)** A CCD pixel has a **full-well capacity of 60 000 electrons** and a **read noise of 6 electrons RMS**. Calculate: (i) The dynamic range in linear and decibel forms. **[3 marks]** (ii) The minimum detectable signal in electrons. **[2 marks]** (iii) Comment on the practical implications for machine-vision contrast detection. **[2 marks]** **Model Solution:** **(i) Dynamic Range:** $$ \text{DR}_{linear} = \frac{\text{FWC}}{\sigma_{read}} = \frac{60\,000}{6} = 10\,000 $$ $$ \text{DR}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(10\,000) = 20 \times 4 = 80\;\text{dB} $$ **(ii) Minimum Detectable Signal:** The minimum signal discernible above noise floor is conventionally set to $3\sigma_{read}$ (3-sigma criterion for 99.7 % confidence): $$ S_{min} = 3 \times \sigma_{read} = 3 \times 6 = 18\;\text{electrons} $$ **(iii) Practical Implications:** An 80 dB dynamic range is **excellent** for machine vision. The camera can simultaneously capture bright highlights (e.g., specular reflections on metal) and dim shadows (e.g., engraved text) without saturation or loss. A minimum detectable signal of 18 electrons means even very dim features (such as laser-etched marks under ambient factory lighting) can be resolved. However, the 3-sigma floor of 18 e⁻ also implies that contrast ratios below ~5 500 : 1 (= 60 000 / 18) cannot be reliably distinguished — a critical limit when imaging glossy vs. matte surfaces in the same frame. --- #### **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain the **principle of charge generation, collection, and transfer** in a CCD with reference to the **MOS capacitor structure**. Show the **potential well diagram** and describe the **three-phase clocking** that moves charge from one pixel to the next. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **MOS Capacitor Structure:** A CCD pixel consists of an **n-type polysilicon gate** (or p-type, depending on design), a thin **SiO₂ dielectric** (~100 nm), and a **p-type silicon substrate**. Applying a positive voltage $V_G$ to the gate repels majority-carrier holes, creating a **depletion region** beneath the oxide. The depletion region acts as a **potential well** whose depth increases with $V_G$. **Charge Generation:** Incoming photons with $E_{ph} = hc/\lambda > E_g$ generate electron–hole pairs inside the depletion region. The built-in electric field sweeps the electrons into the potential well and pushes holes into the substrate. The number of electrons collected per pixel after integration time $t_{int}$ is: $$ N_{e^-} = \int_{0}^{t_{int}} \Phi_{ph}\cdot \text{QE}(\lambda)\cdot A_{pix}\;dt $$ where $\Phi_{ph}$ is the photon flux (photons/m²/s). **Three-Phase Clocking:** Three gate electrodes ($\Phi_1, \Phi_2, \Phi_3$) per pixel are clocked with 120°-offset square waves. At any instant, exactly one gate is at high voltage (e.g., +10 V) creating the deepest well, while the others are at lower voltages (e.g., 0 V or +5 V). Charge always sits beneath the most-positive gate. The transfer sequence over one clock period $T_{clk}$: $$ \begin{aligned} \Phi_1(t) &= 10\,\text{V}\cdot \Pi\!\left(\tfrac{t}{T_{clk}}\right) \\ \Phi_2(t) &= 10\,\text{V}\cdot \Pi\!\left(\tfrac{t-T_{clk}/3}{T_{clk}}\right) \\ \Phi_3(t) &= 10\,\text{V}\cdot \Pi\!\left(\tfrac{t-2T_{clk}/3}{T_{clk}}\right) \end{aligned} $$ During the overlap when both $\Phi_1$ and $\Phi_2$ are high, charge redistributes freely between the two wells. Then $\Phi_1$ returns low while $\Phi_2$ remains high, forcing all charge into the well under $\Phi_2$. Repeating this sequence shifts charge by exactly one pixel pitch per cycle. Because the gates form a chain, charge is transferred sequentially through the entire pixel column toward the output amplifier. **(b)** A CCD camera is used to inspect **printed circuit boards** under a **100 mm/f2.8 lens** at an exposure time of **20 ms**. The peak photon flux at the sensor is $5\times 10^{15}$ photons/m²/s, pixel area is $A_{pix} = (10\,\mu\text{m})^2 = 10^{-10}\,\text{m}^2$, and the peak QE at 550 nm is **70 %**. (i) Compute the **number of photoelectrons** generated per pixel per frame. **[4 marks]** (ii) The dark current is **100 electrons/s** at operating temperature. Compute the **total noise** in electrons RMS. **[2 marks]** (iii) Compute the **SNR**. **[1 mark]** **Model Solution:** **(i) Photoelectrons per pixel:** $$ N_{ph} = \Phi_{ph}\cdot t_{int}\cdot A_{pix} = 5\times 10^{15} \times 0.020 \times 10^{-10} = 10\,000\;\text{photons} $$ $$ N_{e^-} = \text{QE}\cdot N_{ph} = 0.70 \times 10\,000 = 7\,000\;\text{electrons} $$ **(ii) Total noise:** Assume read noise $\sigma_{read} = 5$ e⁻ RMS (typical for industrial CCD). $$ \sigma^{2}_{shot} = S = 7\,000 $$ $$ \sigma^{2}_{dark} = D \cdot t_{int} = 100 \times 0.020 = 2 $$ $$ \sigma^{2}_{read} = 5^{2} = 25 $$ $$ \sigma^{2}_{total} = 7\,000 + 2 + 25 = 7\,027 $$ $$ \sigma_{total} = \sqrt{7\,027} \approx 83.8\;\text{e}^{-} $$ **(iii) Signal-to-Noise Ratio:** $$ \text{SNR} = \frac{7\,000}{83.8} \approx 83.5 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 38.4\;\text{dB} $$ This SNR is sufficient for detecting subtle solder-pad defects (typically requires $\text{SNR} \geq 40$ for high-confidence automated optical inspection). --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks** > 1. **Confusing CCD with CMOS:** Students frequently swap the architectures. Remember: CCD = **charge-coupled** (sequential readout, single output amplifier) vs. CMOS = **active-pixel** (per-pixel amplifier). KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks per occurrence. > 2. **Forgetting $\sigma_{shot}^{2} = S$ (not $\sqrt{S}$):** Shot-noise variance equals the mean signal for Poisson statistics; the RMS noise is $\sqrt{S}$. Mixing up these two forms is a recurring KTU pitfall. > 3. **Skipping units in numerical answers:** Always quote the result with units (electrons, dB, photons). Marks are routinely docked for unit omission. > 4. **Using bandwidth-limited read-noise formulas without justification:** If the question specifies "correlated double sampling," you must subtract $kTC$ noise; otherwise include $\sqrt{kTC/C_{FD}}$ in your noise budget. > 5. **Skipping the 3-sigma explanation for minimum detectable signal:** Simply stating "$S_{min} = \sigma_{read}$" is incomplete — KTU expects the 3-sigma confidence basis. > 6. **Drawing the potential-well diagram incorrectly:** The well depth must scale with gate voltage, and arrows must clearly show charge moving from a high-potential to a low-potential region. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist — CCD Cameras** - [x] **CCD = Charge-Coupled Device.** Invented by **Boyle & Smith, 1969**, Bell Labs. Nobel Prize 2009. - [x] **Operating principle:** Photons → electrons in MOS potential wells → sequential charge transfer → single output amplifier → ADC → digital image. - [x] **Photon energy:** $E_{ph} = hc/\lambda$, must exceed silicon bandgap $E_g = 1.12$ eV. - [x] **Quantum Efficiency (QE):** 40–95 % depending on wavelength; peak typically near 550 nm (green). - [x] **Full-Well Capacity (FWC):** $10^{4}$ – $10^{5}$ electrons/pixel. Sets the maximum recordable signal. - [x] **Charge Transfer Efficiency (CTE):** $\approx 0.99999$ per transfer for scientific-grade BCCD; total $\text{CTE} \approx (1-\varepsilon)^N$. - [x] **Noise sources:** Photon shot noise ($\sqrt{S}$), dark-current shot noise ($\sqrt{D t_{int}}$), read noise ($\sigma_{read}$, constant), reset $kTC$ noise (suppressed by CDS). - [x] **Dynamic Range:** $\text{DR}_{dB} = 20\log_{10}(\text{FWC}/\sigma_{read})$, typically 60–90 dB. - [x] **Three-phase clocking:** Gates $\Phi_1, \Phi_2, \Phi_3$ with 120° phase offset move charge one pixel pitch per cycle. - [x] **Architecture variants:** Full-Frame (no shutter, scientific), Frame-Transfer (storage area, astronomy), Interline-Transfer (electronic shutter, video), Frame-Interline (best of both, broadcast). - [x] **Buried-Channel CCD (BCCD):** Higher CTE and lower dark current by transporting charge below the Si–SiO₂ interface. - [x] **Minimum Detectable Signal:** $3 \sigma_{read}$ (3-sigma rule). - [x] **Advantage over CMOS:** Higher uniformity, lower read noise, better linearity, superior for metrology. - [x] **Disadvantage vs. CMOS:** Higher power, slower readout, requires multiple supply voltages, more expensive. > [!NOTE] **One-Line Mnemonic** > **"Photo-Electrons in a Well, Coupled by Clocking to a Capacitor, Read as Voltage, Digitised as Code."**

topics.title: CMOS Camera
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT635 - MACHINE VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** CMOS Camera

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of CMOS Camera

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CMOS Camera (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Image Sensor Camera)** is an active-pixel image capture device in which each pixel contains its own **photosensor** (typically a photodiode) along with **in-pixel amplification and readout circuitry** fabricated directly using standard CMOS semiconductor processes.

In the context of **KTU Machine Vision (PEBRT635, Module 1)**, the CMOS camera is treated as the **default electronic image acquisition front-end** of any modern vision system. Unlike legacy CCD (Charge-Coupled Device) sensors that shift charge across the array to a single output amplifier, a CMOS sensor performs **per-pixel charge-to-voltage conversion** and reads out rows/columns through an integrated multiplexer.

**Key standardized terminology aligned with the KTU 2024 syllabus:**

| Term | KTU Definition |
|------|----------------|
| **APS** | Active Pixel Sensor — a pixel that contains at least one amplifier transistor inside the pixel |
| **PPS** | Passive Pixel Sensor — a pixel with only a photodiode and a select transistor |
| **ROI** | Region of Interest — a sub-window that can be read out at higher frame rate |
| **CFA** | Color Filter Array — the mosaic (typically Bayer) overlaid on the sensor |
| **QE** | Quantum Efficiency — fraction of incident photons converted to electrons |

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU syllabus specifically treats the **CMOS camera** as the primary digital transducer that converts **photons → electrons → voltage → digital bits** (the P-E-V-D conversion chain).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Building With a Cashier on Every Floor"

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Imagine a large office building (the sensor array) with thousands of rooms (pixels), and every room has one photo (electron packet) stored inside.

- A **CCD sensor** is like a building where all rooms must be **emptied in sequence through a single corridor** leading to one cashier on the ground floor. If any room is slow, the entire building waits.
- A **CMOS sensor** is like a building where **every room has its own cashier (in-pixel amplifier)**. Each room reports its count independently and simultaneously through a parallel address bus.

This is *exactly* why CMOS cameras are:
- **Faster** (parallel readout)
- **Lower power** (no high-voltage charge transfer clocks)
- **Cheaper** (fabricated in standard CMOS fabs — the same fabs that make your phone processor)

## 1.3 Photon-to-Pixel Conversion Chain

The fundamental pipeline executed at every pixel of a CMOS camera:

$$\text{Photon} \;\xrightarrow{\text{absorption}}\; \text{Photo-electron} \;\xrightarrow{\text{integration}}\; Q_{e} \;\xrightarrow{\text{CFW}}\; V_{pix} \;\xrightarrow{\text{ADC}}\; \text{DN (Digital Number)}$$

where:
- **CFW** = Charge-to-Floating-diffusion-conversion + capacitive weighting
- **DN** = Digital Number (grayscale intensity, 0 to $2^{N}-1$ for an N-bit ADC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 scheme expects every student to remember the **four-stage pipeline**: Photon → Electron → Voltage → Digital number.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Block Decomposition

The CMOS camera is decomposed into **six functional subsystems**. KTU examiners frequently test these as a 7-mark sub-question.

1. **Optical Interface (Lens + IR cut filter)**
   - Focuses incoming light onto the sensor plane.
   - The focal spot diameter must satisfy the **Nyquist sampling criterion**:
     $$d_{spot} \ge 2 \cdot p_{pixel}$$
   - where $p_{pixel}$ is the pixel pitch (typically **3 µm to 10 µm** in modern machine vision CMOS sensors).

2. **Pixel Array (Photodiode + In-pixel transistors)**
   - Standard **4T (four-transistor) pinned-photodiode pixel** is the industry default.
   - Transistors: **Reset (Rst), Source Follower (SF), Row Select (RS), Transfer Gate (TG)**.

3. **Column-Parallel Readout & CDS**
   - **Correlated Double Sampling (CDS)** removes kTC reset noise and pixel FPN.
   - Performed column-wise to enable high frame rates.

4. **On-chip ADC**
   - Modern CMOS cameras use **column-parallel single-slope** or **SAR ADCs**.
   - Resolution is typically **10-bit to 14-bit** in machine vision applications.

5. **Digital Interface (MIPI CSI-2, GigE Vision, USB3 Vision, CoaXPress)**
   - Defines the protocol between camera and host PC / embedded board.

6. **Image Signal Processor (ISP)**
   - Performs **demosaicing, white balance, gamma correction, denoising, color correction matrix (CCM), tone mapping**.

## 2.2 The 4T Pinned Photodiode Pixel — Detailed Operation

The **4T pixel** is the heart of nearly every high-performance CMOS image sensor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "4T"?** Four MOSFETs per pixel: **RST, SF, RS, TG**.

### Step-by-step operating phases:

1. **Reset Phase**
   - RST transistor ON → floating diffusion (FD) node is charged to $V_{rst}$ (typically 2.8 V to 3.3 V).
   - TG is OFF, so photodiode (PD) is isolated.

2. **Exposure (Integration) Phase**
   - RST OFF, TG OFF.
   - Photons strike the pinned photodiode, generating electrons via the photoelectric effect.
   - Electrons accumulate in the PD's intrinsic capacitance $C_{PD}$:
     $$V_{PD}(t) = V_{rst} - \frac{Q_{e}(t)}{C_{PD}}$$

3. **Transfer Phase**
   - TG turns ON → all accumulated charge is transferred to the floating diffusion $C_{FD}$.
   - The voltage swing on FD:
     $$\Delta V_{FD} = \frac{Q_{e}}{C_{FD}}$$
   - This is the **Charge-to-Voltage Conversion (CFW)** — the cornerstone formula of CMOS pixel design.

4. **Readout (Source Follower) Phase**
   - RS transistor ON → SF buffers $V_{FD}$ to the column bus.
   - **Correlated Double Sampling** computes:
     $$V_{signal} = V_{reset} - V_{signal\_level}$$
     This cancels kTC reset noise and 1/f noise partially.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **All formulas below are KTU high-yield — memorize and practice.**

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | $I_{ph} = R \cdot P_{opt}$ | Photocurrent proportional to optical power; $R$ = responsivity (A/W) |
| 2 | $Q_{e} = \eta \cdot N_{ph} \cdot q$ | Total electrons collected = Quantum efficiency × photons × electron charge |
| 3 | $V_{pix} = \dfrac{Q_{e}}{C_{FD}}$ | Charge-to-voltage conversion at floating diffusion |
| 4 | $\sigma_{kTC}^{2} = \dfrac{k_B T}{C_{FD}}$ | kTC (reset) noise variance on FD |
| 5 | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{S}{N}\right)\; \text{dB}$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio in decibels |
| 6 | $N_{e,sat} = V_{sat} \cdot C_{FD}/q$ | Full-well capacity (max electrons) |
| 7 | $\text{DR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{N_{e,sat}}{\sigma_{read}}\right)$ | Dynamic range in dB |
| 8 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{read} \cdot N_{rows}}$ | Max frame rate (global shutter ideal) |
| 9 | $V_{sf} = g_{sf} \cdot V_{FD}$ | Source follower gain ($g_{sf} \approx 0.85$ typical) |
| 10 | $\text{Well Capacity} \propto C_{PD} \cdot V_{PD,max}$ | Trade-off with pixel size |

> [!WARNING]
> In the markdown table above, every absolute-value / magnitude notation uses `\vert` instead of a literal pipe character to **prevent breaking KTU markdown rendering**. Do not write `|x|` inside any row — write `\vert x \vert`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

CMOS cameras dominate the following **Machine Vision** application spaces (directly relevant to KTU PEBRT635):

- **Industrial inspection** (PCB, semiconductor wafer AOI — Automated Optical Inspection)
- **Robotics and autonomous mobile robots (AMR)** — high frame rate, low latency
- **Traffic and surveillance ANPR/ITMS** systems — high dynamic range (HDR)
- **Medical endoscopy and microscopy** — small footprint, low power
- **Automotive ADAS** — global shutter CMOS required for distortion-free imaging
- **AR/VR headsets** — global shutter, sub-3 ms latency
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Charge-to-Voltage Conversion Sensitivity

**Given:** A photodiode capacitance $C_{PD} = 10\;\text{fF}$ receives $N_{ph} = 1{,}000{,}000$ photons during an exposure. Quantum efficiency $\eta = 0.6$, electron charge $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\;\text{C}$.

**Step 1:** Compute the number of photo-electrons generated:

$$
N_{e} \;=\; \eta \cdot N_{ph}
$$

$$
N_{e} \;=\; 0.6 \times 1{,}000{,}000 \;=\; 600{,}000\;\text{electrons}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert electron count to charge:

$$
Q_{e} \;=\; N_{e} \cdot q
$$

$$
Q_{e} \;=\; 600{,}000 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \;=\; 9.612 \times 10^{-14}\;\text{C}
$$

**Step 3:** Apply the charge-to-voltage conversion law at the floating diffusion (FD):

$$
V_{signal} \;=\; \frac{Q_{e}}{C_{FD}}
$$

Assuming $C_{FD} = 5\;\text{fF} = 5 \times 10^{-15}\;\text{F}$:

$$
V_{signal} \;=\; \frac{9.612 \times 10^{-14}}{5 \times 10^{-15}} \;=\; 19.22\;\text{V}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Wait — this exceeds the supply rail (typically 3.3 V). **Conclusion:** Either the photodiode is overflowing or the assumption is unrealistic. This illustrates the **full-well capacity** limit.

**Step 4 (corrected):** Full-well capacity at 3.3 V swing:

$$
N_{e,sat} \;=\; \frac{V_{sat} \cdot C_{FD}}{q}
$$

$$
N_{e,sat} \;=\; \frac{3.3 \times 5 \times 10^{-15}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \;\approx\; 103{,}000\;\text{electrons}
$$

So the realistic maximum signal is **~19.22 mV** if we instead assume $C_{FD} = 5\;\text{fF}$ and $Q_{e} = 9.6 \times 10^{-17}\;\text{C}$ (i.e., $N_e = 600{,}000$ **only valid if full-well is much larger — usually not**).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — kTC Reset Noise and SNR

**Given:** $C_{FD} = 5\;\text{fF}$, $T = 300\;\text{K}$, Boltzmann constant $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\;\text{J/K}$.

**Step 1:** Compute the kTC noise standard deviation:

$$
\sigma_{kTC} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{k_B T}{C_{FD}}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{kTC} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 300}{5 \times 10^{-15}}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{kTC} \;=\; \sqrt{8.28 \times 10^{-7}} \;\approx\; 9.10 \times 10^{-4}\;\text{V} \;=\; 0.91\;\text{mV (rms)}
$$

**Step 2:** Convert to electron-equivalent noise:

$$
\sigma_{e,noise} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{kTC} \cdot C_{FD}}{q}
$$

$$
\sigma_{e,noise} \;=\; \frac{9.10 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times 10^{-15}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \;\approx\; 28.4\;\text{electrons (rms)}
$$

**Step 3:** SNR for a signal of $N_e = 50{,}000$ electrons:

$$
\text{SNR} \;=\; \frac{N_e}{\sigma_{total}}
$$

For shot-noise-limited case, $\sigma_{shot} = \sqrt{N_e} = \sqrt{50{,}000} \approx 224\;e^-$.

With CDS cancelling kTC, the dominant noise is shot + read:

$$
\sigma_{total} \;\approx\; \sqrt{N_e + \sigma_{read}^{2}} \;\approx\; \sqrt{50{,}000 + 28.4^{2}} \;\approx\; 225\;e^-
$$

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} \;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{50{,}000}{225}\right) \;\approx\; 46.9\;\text{dB}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Dynamic Range of a Machine Vision CMOS Camera

**Given:** Full-well capacity $N_{e,sat} = 30{,}000\;e^-$, read noise $\sigma_{read} = 5\;e^-$, 12-bit ADC.

**Step 1:** Dynamic Range (linear):

$$
\text{DR}_{linear} \;=\; \frac{N_{e,sat}}{\sigma_{read}} \;=\; \frac{30{,}000}{5} \;=\; 6{,}000
$$

**Step 2:** Dynamic Range in dB:

$$
\text{DR}_{dB} \;=\; 20 \log_{10}(6{,}000) \;\approx\; 75.6\;\text{dB}
$$

**Step 3:** Equivalent in stops (photographic DR):

$$
\text{DR}_{stops} \;=\; \log_{2}(6{,}000) \;\approx\; 12.55\;\text{stops}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — CMOS vs CCD

| Parameter | CMOS APS | CCD |
|-----------|----------|-----|
| Pixel-level amplifier | ✅ Yes (in-pixel SF) | ❌ No (off-chip) |
| Power consumption | **Low (10–100 mW)** | High (1–5 W) |
| Fabrication process | Standard CMOS | Specialized process |
| Random access / ROI readout | ✅ Yes | ❌ Sequential only |
| Global shutter | Achievable (with extra transistor) | Native |
| Rolling shutter | Native | ❌ No |
| Windowing / multi-region | ✅ Native | Limited |
| Fill factor (without micro-lens) | 30–60 % | ~100 % |
| Blooming / smear | Mitigated (anti-bloom drain) | Severe |
| Cost | **Low** | High |
| Suitability for machine vision | **Excellent** | Declining |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners often pose a **3-mark question** asking for any three differences between CMOS and CCD. Memorize the matrix above.

## 3.5 Shutter Modes — Analytical Treatment

**Rolling Shutter (RS):**
- Rows are reset and read sequentially, with a small time offset $\Delta t$ between consecutive rows.
- Frame readout time $t_{frame} = N_{rows} \cdot t_{row}$.
- **Distortion** appears if the object moves faster than $\Delta t$ produces smearing.

**Global Shutter (GS):**
- All pixels begin and end exposure simultaneously via an external storage node.
- Requires an additional **storage gate** or **storage diffusion** inside every pixel (5T or 6T pixel).
- Eliminates motion artifacts but reduces fill factor.

**Frame Rate Equation (GS, ideal):**

$$
f_{fps} \;=\; \frac{1}{t_{readout} + t_{exposure}}
$$

**Frame Rate Equation (RS, $N_{rows}$ rows, $t_{row}$ per row):**

$$
f_{fps} \;\le\; \frac{1}{N_{rows} \cdot t_{row}}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of a CMOS Camera (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Optics["OPTICAL SUBSYSTEM"]
        LENS["Lens Stack and IR Cut Filter"]
    end

    subgraph Sensor["CMOS SENSOR DIE"]
        PIXEL["4T Pixel Array Photodiode plus 4 Transistors"]
        COLAMP["Column Amplifier and CDS Circuit"]
        ADC["Column Parallel ADC 12-bit or 14-bit"]
        BIAS["Bias Generator and Timing Controller"]
    end

    subgraph ISP["ON CHIP IMAGE SIGNAL PROCESSOR"]
        DEMO["Demosaicing Bayer to RGB"]
        WB["White Balance Gain"]
        CCM["Color Correction Matrix"]
        GAM["Gamma and Tone Mapping"]
        NR["Noise Reduction 2D plus 3D"]
    end

    subgraph IFACE["DIGITAL INTERFACE"]
        MIPI["MIPI CSI-2 or GigE Vision or USB3 Vision"]
        HOST["Host PC or Embedded SoC"]
    end

    LENS --> PIXEL
    PIXEL --> COLAMP
    COLAMP --> ADC
    BIAS --> PIXEL
    BIAS --> COLAMP
    BIAS --> ADC
    ADC --> DEMO
    DEMO --> WB
    WB --> CCM
    CCM --> GAM
    GAM --> NR
    NR --> MIPI
    MIPI --> HOST
```

## 4.2 4T Pinned Photodiode Pixel — Schematic Equivalent

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PD[("Photodiode Pinned")] -->|"Charge Qe"| TG["Transfer Gate TG"]
    TG -->|"Transfer"| FD(("Floating Diffusion C_FD"))
    RST["Reset Transistor"] -->|"Resets to Vrst"| FD
    FD --> SF["Source Follower SF"]
    SF --> RS["Row Select RS"]
    RS --> COL["Column Bus to ADC"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This diagram traces the **charge path** (PD → TG → FD → SF → RS → column). The KTU 2024 examiner often asks students to **label the four transistors** and explain the **transfer phase**.

## 4.3 Rolling Shutter vs Global Shutter — Timing Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ROLLING["ROLLING SHUTTER TIMING"]
        R1["Row 1 Exposure Reset Read"] --> R2["Row 2 Exposure Reset Read"]
        R2 --> R3["Row 3 Exposure Reset Read"]
        R3 --> RN["Row N Exposure Reset Read"]
    end

    subgraph GLOBAL["GLOBAL SHUTTER TIMING"]
        GALL1["ALL Rows Reset Simultaneously"]
        GALL1 --> GALL2["ALL Rows Expose Simultaneously"]
        GALL2 --> GALL3["ALL Rows Read Out Sequentially After Exposure"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Bayer Color Filter Array Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1["R Gr R Gr"] --> R2["Gb B Gb B"] --> R3["R Gr R Gr"] --> R4["Gb B Gb B"]
```

> [!TIP]
> The **Bayer pattern** uses 50% green, 25% red, 25% blue — mimicking the human eye's higher green (luminance) sensitivity. Always state this in KTU answers.

## 4.5 System-Level Machine Vision Block — Where the CMOS Camera Fits

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SCENE["Physical Scene"] --> OPT["Optics Lens"]
    OPT --> CMOS["CMOS Camera Sensor"]
    CMOS --> FRAME["Image Frame Buffer"]
    FRAME --> PROC["Image Processing OpenCV Halcon"]
    PROC --> DEC["Decision Actuator PLC Robot"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEBRT635]`
**Define CMOS image sensor. List any two advantages over CCD.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** A CMOS (Complementary Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) image sensor is a solid-state imaging device in which each pixel contains its own photosensitive element (photodiode) and active amplification/readout circuitry, all fabricated in a standard CMOS process.
>
> **Two advantages over CCD (2 × 0.75 = 1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Lower power consumption** because charge is converted to voltage locally (no high-voltage charge-transfer clocks).
> 2. **On-chip integration** of ADC, timing logic, and ISP, enabling smaller, cheaper camera modules.
> 3. (Bonus) Random-access readout, ROI windowing, and native global/rolling shutter selection.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEBRT635]`
**What is the role of the floating diffusion in a 4T CMOS pixel?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **floating diffusion (FD)** is a small parasitic capacitance ($C_{FD}$, typically a few femtofarads) located inside each pixel. Its role is to **convert the photo-electron charge packet into a measurable voltage** via the relation $V_{pix} = Q_{e}/C_{FD}$. The FD is connected to the gate of the in-pixel source-follower transistor, which buffers this voltage onto the column bus for ADC conversion.
