Because the FD is "floating" between reset and transfer events, it preserves the integrated charge until readout, enabling Correlated Double Sampling (CDS).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module 1 typically offers an internal choice. **Attempt either Question A OR Question B.**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **CMOS active pixel sensor (APS)**. Identify the four transistors of the standard 4T pixel and describe their functions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **charge-to-voltage conversion** equation at the floating diffusion. Using a numerical example with $C_{FD} = 5\;\text{fF}$ and a collected charge of $Q_e = 50{,}000 \cdot q$, compute the resulting pixel voltage. Discuss how this signal is read out and digitized. **(7 marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
[Photodiode] → [Transfer Gate TG] → [Floating Diffusion FD] → [Source Follower SF] → [Row Select RS] → [Column Bus] → [Column CDS + ADC] → [Digital Image Buffer]
     ↑              ↑                       ↑
  [Reset RST]───►[V_rst supply]
```

**Four-Transistor Identification and Functions (4 × 1 = 4 marks):**

| Transistor | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Reset (RST)** | Connects FD to $V_{rst}$ to clear residual charge before integration. |
| **Transfer Gate (TG)** | Transfers integrated photoelectrons from PD to FD at the end of exposure. |
| **Source Follower (SF)** | Buffers the FD voltage to the column line with high input impedance. |
| **Row Select (RS)** | Acts as a switch connecting the pixel to the column bus when row is addressed. |

---

#### SOLUTION (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation (3 marks):**

Charge accumulated on PD:

$$
Q_{e} \;=\; \eta \cdot N_{ph} \cdot q
$$

After TG turns ON, charge flows to FD. Capacitance $C_{FD}$ converts to voltage by the fundamental capacitor law:

$$
V_{FD} \;=\; \frac{Q_{e}}{C_{FD}}
$$

**Numerical substitution (2 marks):**

$$
V_{FD} \;=\; \frac{50{,}000 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}}{5 \times 10^{-15}}
$$

$$
V_{FD} \;=\; \frac{8.01 \times 10^{-15}}{5 \times 10^{-15}} \;=\; 1.602\;\text{V}
$$

**Readout chain description (2 marks):**
- SF buffers $V_{FD}$ to the column with gain $g_{sf} \approx 0.85$, producing $V_{col} \approx 1.36\;\text{V}$.
- Correlated Double Sampling subtracts $V_{reset}$ from $V_{signal}$ to remove kTC noise and pixel FPN.
- The column-parallel ADC (typically 12-bit, $V_{ref} = 3.3\;\text{V}$) maps the voltage to a digital number:
  $$DN = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{col}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^{12} - 1) \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{1.36}{3.3} \cdot 4095 \right\rfloor = 1687$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Compare **rolling shutter** and **global shutter** CMOS cameras. Explain, with a timing diagram, why rolling shutter causes motion artifacts in fast-moving scenes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A machine vision CMOS camera has a full-well capacity of $30{,}000\;e^-$, read noise of $5\;e^-$, and a 12-bit ADC. Compute the **dynamic range** in dB and in stops. If the lens is f/2.8 and the pixel pitch is 3.45 µm, discuss whether this camera is suitable for a high-speed conveyor inspection at 5 m/s. **(7 marks)**

---

#### SOLUTION (a) — 7 Marks

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Rolling Shutter | Global Shutter |
|---------|----------------|----------------|
| Exposure | Row-by-row sequential | All rows simultaneously |
| Motion artifact | Yes (skew, wobble) | None |
| Pixel complexity | 4T | 5T or 6T (extra storage) |
| Fill factor | Higher | Lower (storage node) |
| Frame rate | Higher achievable | Slightly lower |
| Use case | Static or slow scenes | Fast motion, machine vision |

**Timing Diagram + Skew Explanation (4 marks):**

At time $t_0$, row 1 begins exposure; at $t_0 + \Delta t$, row 2 begins, etc. If an object moves laterally during $\Delta t \cdot N_{rows}$, then row 1 captures it at position $x_1$ while row $N$ captures it at $x_N$, producing a **diagonal smear (skew)**. For a 1080p sensor with $t_{row} = 10\;\mu s$:

$$
t_{frame} = 1080 \times 10\;\mu s = 10.8\;\text{ms}
$$

Object displacement:

$$
\Delta x = v \cdot t_{frame} = 5 \times 0.0108 = 0.054\;\text{m} = 54\;\text{mm}
$$

This is unacceptable — a global shutter is mandatory.

---

#### SOLUTION (b) — 7 Marks

**Dynamic Range Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
\text{DR}_{linear} \;=\; \frac{30{,}000}{5} \;=\; 6{,}000
$$

$$
\text{DR}_{dB} \;=\; 20 \log_{10}(6{,}000) \;\approx\; 75.6\;\text{dB}
$$

$$
\text{DR}_{stops} \;=\; \log_{2}(6{,}000) \;\approx\; 12.55\;\text{stops}
$$

**Suitability for Conveyor Inspection (4 marks):**

- **Sensor spatial resolution:** Pixel pitch $p = 3.45\;\mu m$. At typical machine-vision field of view (FOV) of 100 mm with 1024-pixel sensor, on-object pixel size $\approx 97.7\;\mu m$ — adequate for defect detection above 0.3 mm.
- **Frame rate requirement:** At 5 m/s conveyor and a 1 mm feature, max allowable exposure $\le 0.2\;\text{ms}$. Modern CMOS cameras easily achieve $>5{,}000\;\text{fps}$ in ROI mode.
- **Shutter requirement:** **Rolling shutter is INSUITABLE** — global shutter mandatory (skew of 54 mm computed above).
- **Dynamic range:** 75.6 dB is **sufficient** for typical reflective surfaces; HDR with multi-exposure may be needed for highly specular/metallic parts.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The camera is **conditionally suitable** — global shutter variant required, sufficient DR, frame rate acceptable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "fill factor" with "quantum efficiency"** — they are NOT the same. Fill factor is geometric; QE includes spectral sensitivity.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by $\eta$** when computing $Q_e$ — many students write $Q_e = N_{ph} \cdot q$, losing 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Writing `|x|` inside a markdown table** — breaks the KTU answer sheet PDF render. Always use `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX.
> 4. **Skipping the unit** in final numeric answers — examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.
> 5. **Not drawing the block diagram with arrows** showing signal flow direction — partial credit is lost.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read before entering the exam hall):**

- ✅ CMOS camera = **per-pixel charge-to-voltage conversion + parallel readout**.
- ✅ The **standard pixel is the 4T pinned photodiode** with transistors **RST, TG, SF, RS**.
- ✅ Core formula: $V_{pix} = Q_e / C_{FD}$ — memorized in electron and voltage forms.
- ✅ Reset (kTC) noise: $\sigma_{kTC}^{2} = k_B T / C_{FD}$ — **eliminated by CDS**.
- ✅ Dynamic range in **dB = $20\log_{10}(N_{e,sat}/\sigma_{read})$**; in **stops = $\log_2(\cdot)$**.
- ✅ **Bayer CFA** = 50% green, 25% red, 25% blue; mimics human luminance sensitivity.
- ✅ **Rolling shutter** causes skew for fast motion; **global shutter** is essential for machine vision.
- ✅ CMOS advantages: low power, on-chip integration, ROI, low cost.
- ✅ CMOS disadvantages vs CCD: lower fill factor, more pixel-level FPN (mitigated by CDS).
- ✅ Interfaces for machine vision: **GigE Vision, USB3 Vision, CoaXPress, MIPI CSI-2**.
- ✅ Pipeline always ends with: Photon → Electron → Voltage → Digital Number (PEVD chain).
- ✅ Quantum Efficiency $\eta$ must be applied in any charge calculation — examiners test this every year.
- ✅ **Global shutter** requires 5T/6T pixel with storage diffusion; reduces fill factor ~10–20%.
- ✅ Photodiode reset must happen **before** integration; transfer must happen **after** integration — order matters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** Whenever a numerical question appears, **always show the substitution step with units**. Even if the final answer is correct, missing units = -0.5 mark per KTU valuation key.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Three-Chip Cameras
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT635 - MACHINE VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Three-Chip Cameras

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Three-Chip Camera** (also known as a **3CCD camera** or **Three-CCD Prism Camera**) is a high-precision imaging device that employs an optical **dichroic prism assembly** to split incoming polychromatic light into its three primary color components — **Red (R), Green (G), and Blue (B)** — and directs each wavelength band onto a dedicated, separate **Charge-Coupled Device (CCD)** sensor. Each sensor independently captures a monochromatic image corresponding to one of the three color channels, which are then electronically synchronized and combined to produce a high-fidelity, full-color image.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Three-chip cameras are classified under **multi-sensor color imaging architectures** in machine vision. They are considered the **gold standard** for color fidelity, spatial resolution, and signal-to-noise ratio in industrial vision systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at a busy railway station where three trains are scheduled to leave simultaneously for three different cities — say **Redville**, **Greensburg**, and **Blueport**. A clever traffic controller (the **dichroic prism**) at the junction uses colored traffic signals to redirect each passenger (a **photon** of a specific wavelength) to the correct train (the **dedicated CCD sensor**). Each train carries only passengers heading to one city, ensuring that **no time is wasted** on sorting and that **every passenger arrives at the right destination simultaneously**.

In a conventional **single-chip camera**, all colors are funneled into one sensor and later reconstructed mathematically — akin to squeezing all passengers into one train and asking them to disembark at different stations along the way. The three-chip approach is therefore the **"parallel sorting"** strategy — physically separating colors in the optical domain rather than computationally guessing them.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| **Color Channels** | **3 (R, G, B)** |
| **Spectral Resolution** | Up to **~2 nm** per channel |
| **Luminance Resolution** | Equivalent to **~3 × single-CCD pixel density** |
| **Registration Accuracy** | Sub-pixel alignment (**< 0.1 pixel** typical) |
| **Sensor Cooling** | Often **Peltier-cooled to −10 °C** for low-noise imaging |
| **Typical Pixel Pitch** | **3.45 µm to 7.4 µm** |

> [!NOTE]
> The **dichroic coating** on the prism surfaces is the heart of the color separation process. These coatings are manufactured using **thin-film interference filters** deposited via physical vapor deposition (PVD).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The operation of a three-chip camera is governed by the principles of **wavelength-selective thin-film interference** and **geometric optics**. The process can be decomposed into the following structured sequence:

**Step 1 — Light Collection (Objective Lens Stage)**
- The primary lens focuses polychromatic light from the scene onto the entrance face of the prism block.
- All wavelengths (typically **380 nm to 780 nm**) enter simultaneously.

**Step 2 — First Dichroic Reflection (Blue/Green vs Red Split)**
- The light first strikes a **dichroic coating tuned to ~600 nm**.
- Wavelengths **below ~600 nm** (Blue + Green) are **reflected** at **90°**.
- Wavelengths **above ~600 nm** (Red) are **transmitted** straight through.

**Step 3 — Second Dichroic Reflection (Blue vs Green Split)**
- The Blue+Green beam now strikes a **second dichroic coating tuned to ~500 nm**.
- **Blue** light (shorter wavelengths) is **reflected** at 90°.
- **Green** light is **transmitted** through to its dedicated CCD.

**Step 4 — Image Capture on Three Independent CCDs**
- Each separated beam lands on its respective **monochrome CCD sensor**.
- Each CCD produces a full-resolution monochromatic image of its assigned color.

**Step 5 — Electronic Synchronization and Recombination**
- A **digital signal processor (DSP)** or **frame grabber** synchronizes the three sensor outputs.
- Channels are aligned (registered) using sub-pixel correction routines.
- A composite full-color RGB image is generated.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why three sensors instead of one?** — To avoid the **Bayer mosaic** color filter array (CFA) interpolation step, which inherently loses **~66% of spatial information** per channel.
- **Why a prism instead of separate filters?** — The prism ensures that all three optical paths are **identical in length**, guaranteeing that the three images are **perfectly co-registered** and **in-focus simultaneously**.
- **Why dichroic coatings?** — They provide **sharp spectral cut-offs** (transition width **< 20 nm**) with **> 95% transmission** in the passband and **> 95% reflection** in the stopband.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|
| Thin-film interference condition (constructive) | $2 n d \cos\theta = m\lambda$ | $n$: refractive index, $d$: film thickness (nm), $\theta$: incidence angle, $m$: order, $\lambda$: wavelength (nm) |
| Spectral transmittance (dichroic filter) | $T(\lambda) = \dfrac{I_{\text{out}}}{I_{\text{in}}}$ | Dimensionless ratio, ideal = **1.0** in passband |
| Optical path length equivalence (prism design) | $n_1 d_1 = n_2 d_2 = n_3 d_3$ | Ensures **equal effective path length** across all three channels |
| Image registration error | $\varepsilon = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ | $\Delta x, \Delta y$ in pixels |
| Spatial resolution advantage factor | $R_{\text{3CCD}} = 3 \times R_{\text{1CCD per channel}}$ | Due to absence of **Bayer interpolation loss** |
| Sensor quantum efficiency (typical) | $\eta = \dfrac{N_{\text{electrons}}}{N_{\text{photons}}} \approx \mathbf{40\%-70\%}$ | Dimensionless efficiency ratio |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Three-chip cameras are indispensable in **printed circuit board (PCB) inspection**, **pharmaceutical color verification**, **food grading systems**, **medical endoscopy**, and **broadcast television studios** where colorimetric accuracy is non-negotiable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Optical Path Length Matching in the Prism Assembly

A critical design constraint in three-chip cameras is ensuring that **all three color channels produce images of identical magnification and focus**. This is achieved by matching the **effective optical path lengths** through the prism. Let us derive the condition.

**Given:**
- Prism block material: Schott BK7 glass with refractive index $n = 1.518$ at $\lambda = 550$ nm.
- Three geometric path lengths inside the prism: $d_R$, $d_G$, $d_B$ for Red, Green, Blue respectively.
- Three different wavelengths: $\lambda_R = 700$ nm, $\lambda_G = 546$ nm, $\lambda_B = 436$ nm.

**Step 1 — Write the effective optical path length (OPL) for each channel:**

$$\text{OPL}_R = n(\lambda_R) \cdot d_R$$

$$\text{OPL}_G = n(\lambda_G) \cdot d_G$$

$$\text{OPL}_B = n(\lambda_B) \cdot d_B$$

Each channel traverses a different physical path inside the prism assembly because of the dichroic reflections.

**Step 2 — Impose the equal OPL condition:**

For perfect focus across all three CCDs, the optical path lengths must be equal:

$$\text{OPL}_R = \text{OPL}_G = \text{OPL}_B = \text{OPL}_{\text{ref}}$$

This gives us:

$$n(\lambda_R) \cdot d_R = n(\lambda_G) \cdot d_G = n(\lambda_B) \cdot d_B$$

**Step 3 — Express the path lengths in terms of a reference:**

Using Green as the reference channel (typical design choice because human vision peaks at ~555 nm):

$$d_G = \dfrac{\text{OPL}_{\text{ref}}}{n(\lambda_G)}$$

$$d_R = \dfrac{n(\lambda_G)}{n(\lambda_R)} \cdot d_G$$

$$d_B = \dfrac{n(\lambda_G)}{n(\lambda_B)} \cdot d_G$$

**Step 4 — Account for dispersion (Cauchy's equation for BK7 glass):**

The refractive index varies with wavelength according to:

$$n(\lambda) = A + \dfrac{B}{\lambda^2} + \dfrac{C}{\lambda^4}$$

For BK7 glass, the Sellmeier coefficients are approximately:
- $A = 1.5046$
- $B = 0.00420 \;\mu m^2$
- $C = 0.0000106 \;\mu m^4$

**Step 5 — Calculate refractive indices at the three wavelengths:**

For $\lambda_G = 0.546 \; \mu m$:

$$n(\lambda_G) = 1.5046 + \dfrac{0.00420}{(0.546)^2} + \dfrac{0.0000106}{(0.546)^4}$$

$$n(\lambda_G) = 1.5046 + 0.01409 + 0.0001194 \approx 1.5188$$

For $\lambda_R = 0.700 \; \mu m$:

$$n(\lambda_R) = 1.5046 + \dfrac{0.00420}{(0.700)^2} + \dfrac{0.0000106}{(0.700)^4}$$

$$n(\lambda_R) = 1.5046 + 0.008571 + 0.00000442 \approx 1.5132$$

For $\lambda_B = 0.436 \; \mu m$:

$$n(\lambda_B) = 1.5046 + \dfrac{0.00420}{(0.436)^2} + \dfrac{0.0000106}{(0.436)^4}$$

$$n(\lambda_B) = 1.5046 + 0.02210 + 0.0002935 \approx 1.5270$$

**Step 6 — Compute the relative path lengths:**

Taking $d_G = 25.0$ mm as the reference geometric path:

$$d_R = \dfrac{1.5188}{1.5132} \times 25.0 = 25.093 \;\text{mm}$$

$$d_B = \dfrac{1.5188}{1.5270} \times 25.0 = 24.866 \;\text{mm}$$

**Step 7 — Verification of equal OPL:**

$$\text{OPL}_R = 1.5132 \times 25.093 = 37.97 \;\text{mm}$$

$$\text{OPL}_G = 1.5188 \times 25.000 = 37.97 \;\text{mm}$$

$$\text{OPL}_B = 1.5270 \times 24.866 = 37.97 \;\text{mm}$$

$$\boxed{\text{OPL}_R = \text{OPL}_G = \text{OPL}_B \approx 37.97 \;\text{mm}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The physical path differences are on the order of **~0.1 mm**, but the refractive index dispersion compensates precisely to ensure **equal optical path lengths** — guaranteeing that all three images are in focus on their respective CCD planes.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Three-Chip vs Single-Chip Camera

| Performance Metric | Three-Chip Camera | Single-Chip Camera (Bayer CFA) | Improvement Factor |
|---|---|---|---|
| Spatial Resolution per channel | **Full native resolution** | **~25% of native** (after demosaicing) | **~3×** |
| Color Accuracy (ΔE) | **< 2** (broadcast-grade) | **3–5** (typical) | ~2.5× better |
| Light Sensitivity (ISO equivalent) | **Higher** (no CFA absorption) | **Lower** (~67% loss due to CFA) | **~3× better** |
| Aliasing / Moiré | **Minimal** | **Significant** | Major reduction |
| Cost | **High** (5–10×) | **Low** | — |
| Alignment Complexity | **Critical** (sub-pixel) | **None** | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Three-Chip Camera

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A0["INPUT OPTICS"]
        A1["Scene with Polychromatic Light"]
        A2["Primary Objective Lens"]
        A3["IR Cut Filter"]
    end

    subgraph B0["DICHROIC PRISM ASSEMBLY"]
        B1["Dichroic Coating 1 (~600 nm cut-off)"]
        B2["Dichroic Coating 2 (~500 nm cut-off)"]
        B3["Color Separation Prism Block"]
    end

    subgraph C0["THREE CCD SENSORS"]
        C1["Red CCD Sensor"]
        C2["Green CCD Sensor"]
        C3["Blue CCD Sensor"]
    end

    subgraph D0["ELECTRONIC PROCESSING"]
        D1["Analog Front End and Amplifiers"]
        D2["Analog to Digital Converters"]
        D3["Synchronization and Registration DSP"]
        D4["Color Matrix Transformation and Gamma"]
    end

    subgraph E0["OUTPUT"]
        E1["Composite Full Color RGB Image"]
        E2["Machine Vision Processor or Display"]
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> B3
    B3 --> B1
    B1 -->|"Reflect Blue and Green"| B2
    B1 -->|"Transmit Red"| C1
    B2 -->|"Reflect Blue"| C3
    B2 -->|"Transmit Green"| C2
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> D4 --> E1 --> E2
```

### 4.2 Optical Light Path Through the Prism (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P0["PRISM ASSEMBLY"]
        P1["Entrance Face"]
        P2["Dichroic Surface 1 - Long Pass 600 nm"]
        P3["Dichroic Surface 2 - Long Pass 500 nm"]
        P4["Exit Face Red"]
        P5["Exit Face Green"]
        P6["Exit Face Blue"]
    end

    subgraph S0["SENSORS"]
        S1["Red CCD"]
        S2["Green CCD"]
        S3["Blue CCD"]
    end

    L0["White Light In"] --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 -->|"Reflect BG"| P3
    P2 -->|"Transmit R"| P4 --> S1
    P3 -->|"Reflect B"| P6 --> S3
    P3 -->|"Transmit G"| P5 --> S2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Note:** In a real prism assembly, all three CCDs are mounted on **three mutually perpendicular faces** of the prism cube, each receiving its dedicated color channel after the two dichroic reflections. The paths are physically orthogonal but optically equivalent.

### 4.3 Detailed Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph X1["OPTICAL SUBSYSTEM"]
        X1A["Lens Group"]
        X1B["Aperture Control"]
        X1C["Shutter Mechanism"]
        X1D["IR Blocking Filter"]
        X1E["Dichroic Prism Block"]
    end

    subgraph X2["SENSOR SUBSYSTEM"]
        X2A["Red CCD with Peltier Cooler"]
        X2B["Green CCD with Peltier Cooler"]
        X2C["Blue CCD with Peltier Cooler"]
    end

    subgraph X3["SIGNAL CONDITIONING"]
        X3A["Correlated Double Sampling"]
        X3B["Programmable Gain Amplifier"]
        X3C["14 bit or 16 bit ADC"]
    end

    subgraph X4["DIGITAL PROCESSING"]
        X4A["Channel Synchronization"]
        X4B["Sub Pixel Registration"]
        X4C["Color Space Conversion"]
        X4D["White Balance and Gamma Correction"]
    end

    X1A --> X1B --> X1C --> X1D --> X1E
    X1E --> X2A
    X1E --> X2B
    X1E --> X2C
    X2A --> X3A
    X2B --> X3A
    X2C --> X3A
    X3A --> X3B --> X3C --> X4A --> X4B --> X4C --> X4D
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define a three-chip camera. Mention any two advantages over a single-chip camera.**

**Model Answer:**
A three-chip camera is an imaging device that uses a **dichroic prism** to split incoming light into three primary color components (Red, Green, Blue), each directed onto a **dedicated CCD sensor**. The three monochromatic images are then electronically combined to produce a full-color image.

**Two Advantages over Single-Chip Cameras:**
1. **Higher spatial resolution per channel** — Each color channel is captured at full native resolution without Bayer interpolation losses.
2. **Superior color accuracy and reduced aliasing** — Eliminates the need for color filter arrays (CFAs), producing more faithful color reproduction and minimal moiré artifacts.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for two advantages)*

#### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**What is the role of a dichroic prism in a three-chip camera?**

**Model Answer:**
The **dichroic prism** is the central optical component of a three-chip camera. Its roles are:
1. **Spectral separation** — It splits the incoming white light into three primary color bands (R, G, B) using wavelength-selective **thin-film interference coatings**.
2. **Optical path length matching** — It ensures that all three color channels traverse **equivalent optical path lengths**, so the three images are perfectly co-registered and in focus on their respective CCDs.

*(3 marks: 2 marks for spectral separation role, 1 mark for OPL matching role)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **three-chip CCD camera**. Discuss the function of the dichroic prism in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the performance of three-chip cameras with single-chip Bayer mosaic cameras in terms of resolution, color accuracy, sensitivity, and cost. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Working Principle:**

A three-chip camera uses **three independent CCD sensors**, one for each primary color. The key stages are:

1. **Light Capture:** The objective lens collects light from the scene and forms an image at the entrance of the prism assembly.
2. **Color Separation:** Inside the prism, two dichroic coatings selectively reflect and transmit different wavelength bands:
   - The **first dichroic surface** (cut-off ~600 nm) reflects Blue+Green and transmits Red.
   - The **second dichroic surface** (cut-off ~500 nm) reflects Blue and transmits Green.
3. **Image Capture:** Each separated color beam lands on its dedicated **monochrome CCD**, producing three full-resolution monochromatic images.
4. **Signal Processing:** The three channels are digitized, synchronized, registered to sub-pixel accuracy, and combined into a full-color RGB image via a **digital signal processor**.

**Function of the Dichroic Prism (detailed):**

The dichroic prism consists of multiple glass elements cemented together, with **thin-film dielectric coatings** deposited on internal faces. These coatings exploit **constructive and destructive interference** at specific wavelengths to achieve sharp spectral cut-offs.

The **transmittance** of a dichroic coating can be modeled as:

$$T(\lambda) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \left[\dfrac{\lambda - \lambda_c}{\Delta\lambda}\right]^{2N}}$$

where $\lambda_c$ is the central cut-off wavelength and $\Delta\lambda$ is the transition width.

The prism also ensures **equal optical path lengths** across all three channels (as derived in Section 3.1), which is essential for **simultaneous focus** on the three CCD planes.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for working principle diagram, 3 marks for dichroic prism function, 2 marks for OPL explanation)*

##### Model Solution for Part (b):

| Parameter | Three-Chip Camera | Single-Chip Bayer Camera |
|---|---|---|
| **Resolution per channel** | Full native resolution (e.g., **1920 × 1080 per channel**) | Reduced due to CFA; effective ~25% per channel after demosaicing |
| **Color Accuracy (ΔE)** | Excellent (ΔE < 2) | Moderate (ΔE = 3–5) |
| **Sensitivity** | High — no absorption losses from CFA | Lower — ~67% of incident light absorbed by Bayer filters |
| **Aliasing / Moiré** | Minimal | Significant, requires optical low-pass filter |
| **Cost** | High (5–10× single-chip) | Low |
| **Alignment complexity** | High (sub-pixel registration needed) | None required |

**Conclusion:** Three-chip cameras offer superior color fidelity, resolution, and sensitivity at the cost of greater complexity and expense — making them ideal for **precision machine vision** and **broadcast applications**.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per parameter comparison row, plus 1 mark for conclusion)*

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2023 - Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the optical path length matching requirement in three-chip cameras. Derive the geometric path lengths for R, G, B channels given BK7 glass refractive indices. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the applications of three-chip cameras in industrial machine vision with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Optical Path Length Matching — Explanation:**

In a three-chip camera, the three color channels travel through different physical paths inside the prism due to the dichroic reflections. For all three images to be **simultaneously in focus** on their respective CCD planes, the **effective optical path length (OPL)** must be identical for all three channels.

The OPL for each channel is:

$$\text{OPL}_i = n(\lambda_i) \cdot d_i$$

where $n(\lambda_i)$ is the wavelength-dependent refractive index and $d_i$ is the geometric path length inside the prism.

**Setting equal OPLs:**

$$n(\lambda_R) \cdot d_R = n(\lambda_G) \cdot d_G = n(\lambda_B) \cdot d_B$$

**Refractive Indices (using Cauchy/Sellmeier equation for BK7):**
- $n(\lambda_R = 700 \; nm) \approx 1.5132$
- $n(\lambda_G = 546 \; nm) \approx 1.5188$
- $n(\lambda_B = 436 \; nm) \approx 1.5270$

**Taking Green path as reference, $d_G = 25.0$ mm:**

$$d_R = \dfrac{n(\lambda_G)}{n(\lambda_R)} \cdot d_G = \dfrac{1.5188}{1.5132} \times 25.0 = 25.09 \;\text{mm}$$

$$d_B = \dfrac{n(\lambda_G)}{n(\lambda_B)} \cdot d_G = \dfrac{1.5188}{1.5270} \times 25.0 = 24.87 \;\text{mm}$$

**Verification:**

$$\text{OPL}_R = 1.5132 \times 25.09 = 37.97 \;\text{mm}$$

$$\text{OPL}_G = 1.5188 \times 25.00 = 37.97 \;\text{mm}$$

$$\text{OPL}_B = 1.5270 \times 24.87 = 37.97 \;\text{mm}$$

All three OPLs match, ensuring simultaneous focus across the three CCDs.

*(7 marks: 2 marks for concept, 2 marks for OPL equations, 2 marks for numerical calculation, 1 mark for verification)*

##### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Industrial Machine Vision Applications of Three-Chip Cameras:**

1. **Printed Circuit Board (PCB) Inspection:** Detects solder defects, missing components, and trace discontinuities by analyzing color-coded features on boards. High color accuracy is essential to distinguish between green solder mask, copper traces, and silkscreen markings.

2. **Pharmaceutical Quality Control:** Verifies tablet color uniformity, capsule integrity, and detects contamination. Even subtle color deviations (ΔE < 1) must be flagged.

3. **Food and Beverage Sorting:** Identifies defective fruits, ripeness levels, foreign contaminants, and verifies packaging labels. Used in automated sorting lines for tomatoes, apples, coffee beans, etc.

4. **Automotive Paint Inspection:** Detects paint inconsistencies, swirl marks, and color mismatches on car bodies at the final assembly stage.

5. **Textile and Fabric Inspection:** Detects weaving defects, color bleeding, dye inconsistencies, and pattern errors with high fidelity.

6. **Medical Endoscopy and Microscopy:** Captures accurate tissue coloration for diagnostic purposes, where ΔE deviations directly affect clinical interpretation.

7. **Electronic Display Manufacturing:** Verifies pixel-level color reproduction on LCD/OLED panels during burn-in testing and quality assurance.

*(7 marks: 1 mark per application with explanation)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse Bayer CFA cameras with three-chip cameras.** Many students incorrectly state that three-chip cameras use a Bayer mosaic — they do NOT. This is a guaranteed zero mark.
> 2. **Always mention the dichroic prism explicitly.** A vague answer about "three sensors" without describing the **color separation mechanism** will lose at least **3 marks** in a 7-mark question.
> 3. **Numerical questions on OPL require the Sellmeier or Cauchy equation.** Memorizing refractive index values without showing the dispersion relation will lose marks.
> 4. **Diagram must include the dichroic prism, three CCDs, and labeled light paths.** A mere block diagram without the prism internals is incomplete.
> 5. **Applications must be machine-vision specific, not generic photography.** Avoid answers that talk only about "broadcasting" or "home use."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
>
> - **Three-chip cameras** use **three independent CCDs** and a **dichroic prism** to capture R, G, B channels separately.
> - The **dichroic prism** performs **wavelength-selective splitting** via thin-film interference coatings.
> - Two dichroic surfaces are used: first at **~600 nm** (R vs BG), second at **~500 nm** (B vs G).
> - **Optical path length matching** is achieved through dispersion compensation: $n(\lambda_R) d_R = n(\lambda_G) d_G = n(\lambda_B) d_B$.
> - **No Bayer mosaic** is used — hence **no spatial resolution loss** from demosaicing.
> - **Color accuracy (ΔE)** is significantly better than single-chip cameras.
> - **Sensitivity** is ~3× higher due to absence of CFA absorption losses.
> - **Applications:** PCB inspection, pharmaceutical QC, food sorting, automotive paint inspection, medical imaging, display testing.
> - **Disadvantages:** High cost, complex alignment (sub-pixel registration required), larger form factor.
> - **Key equation to remember:** $T(\lambda) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \left[\dfrac{\lambda - \lambda_c}{\Delta\lambda}\right]^{2N}}$ for dichroic transmittance.
> - **Typical refractive index of BK7 glass at 546 nm:** $n \approx 1.5188$.
> - **Industry standard channel order in prism:** Reflect BG → Reflect B → Transmit G → Transmit R.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Camera Performance Parameters: Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT635 - MACHINE VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Camera Performance Parameters: Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> In the context of machine vision and digital image sensors, **Noise** is defined as the *unwanted, random fluctuation in the pixel signal level* that corrupts the true photo-generated charge. It is a stochastic disturbance superimposed on the deterministic image data, originating from the discrete nature of photons, the thermal agitation of electrons, and the imperfections in the read-out and amplification electronics of the image sensor.

In simpler words, when a camera tries to capture a perfectly clean image of a uniformly illuminated white wall, the pixel values will not be perfectly identical — they will randomly scatter around some average value. This scatter is **noise**. It is the *uncertainty* in measuring how much light hit each pixel.

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Rainfall Analogy**
> Imagine you are standing on a terrace trying to count the exact number of raindrops falling into a bucket per second using a noisy mechanical counter.
> - Sometimes the counter clicks extra (thermal jitter inside the counter).
> - Sometimes two raindrops hit at once and the counter only counts one (dead time).
> - The counter has a slight "tick" sound from its own moving parts (read noise).
> - The bucket itself leaks slightly, so a small amount of water is always present even when it is not raining (dark current).
>
> The **true rain count** is your "signal," and everything else is "noise." If the rain is light, the noise overwhelms the count. If the rain is heavy, the noise becomes negligible. This is exactly how photon noise behaves in a camera.

## Sources of Noise in an Image Sensor

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Noise Categories (Syllabus Highlight)**
> 1. **Photon Shot Noise** — quantum statistical fluctuation in the arrival of photons.
> 2. **Dark Current (Thermal) Noise** — thermally generated electrons in the silicon.
> 3. **Read Noise** — noise added by the on-chip output amplifier and ADC.
> 4. **Fixed Pattern Noise (FPN)** — pixel-to-pixel offset variation (Photo-Response Non-Uniformity, PRNU, and Dark Signal Non-Uniformity, DSNU).
> 5. **Quantization Noise** — round-off error from the analog-to-digital converter.

### Key Performance Metric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** is the headline metric for camera noise performance. It is typically expressed in decibels (dB).
> $$\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S}{N}\right) \text{ dB}$$
> where $S$ is the average signal (electrons or DN) and $N$ is the RMS noise.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Photon Shot Noise

Photons arrive at a detector following a **Poisson distribution**. For an average of $S$ photoelectrons collected in a pixel during an exposure, the standard deviation of the shot noise is:

$$\sigma_{\text{shot}} = \sqrt{S}$$

The **Why**: Because photons are quantized and independent, the variance of a Poisson process equals its mean.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Law:** Shot noise is the *irreducible* noise floor of any photon-based imaging system. You cannot eliminate it, only ensure your signal $S$ is large enough that $\sqrt{S}$ becomes negligible relative to $S$.

The shot-noise SNR is:
$$\text{SNR}_{\text{shot}} = \frac{S}{\sqrt{S}} = \sqrt{S}$$

Thus, **doubling the exposure** improves the SNR by a factor of $\sqrt{2}$ (~3 dB). To gain a full stop (factor of 2 in SNR), you must collect 4× the photons.

## 2.2 Dark Current Noise

Even with no light, thermally excited electrons accumulate in the photodiode well. The **dark current** $I_d$ (in electrons/second) grows exponentially with temperature:

$$I_d(T) \propto T^{1.5} \, e^{-E_g / (2k_BT)}$$

where:
- $E_g$ is the silicon bandgap (~1.12 eV),
- $k_B$ is **Boltzmann's constant** ($\mathbf{1.381 \times 10^{-23}}$ J/K),
- $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin.

The dark-current noise standard deviation for an integration time $t_{\text{int}}$ is:
$$\sigma_{\text{dark}} = \sqrt{I_d \cdot t_{\text{int}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Tip:** Cooling the sensor (e.g., to $-10^{\circ}\text{C}$) is the standard way to suppress dark noise in scientific and machine-vision cameras.

## 2.3 Read Noise

Read noise ($\sigma_{\text{read}}$) is added by the output transistor, correlated double sampling circuitry, and the ADC. It is **independent of signal level** and forms a constant noise floor.

## 2.4 Total Noise Model

For a single pixel, assuming uncorrelated noise sources, variances add:

$$\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{\sigma_{\text{shot}}^{2} + \sigma_{\text{dark}}^{2} + \sigma_{\text{read}}^{2} + \sigma_{\text{quant}}^{2} + \sigma_{\text{FPN}}^{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-yield insight:** Because variances add (not standard deviations), the **dominant noise source** always sets the camera's behavior:
> - **Low light** → Read noise and dark noise dominate.
> - **High light** → Shot noise dominates (the linear regime limit).

## 2.5 Photon Transfer Curve (PTC)

The **Photon Transfer Curve** is the experimental method used to characterize camera noise. It plots the **variance** of pixel values against the **mean signal** on a log–log graph:

- At low signal → flat region = read noise squared.
- At intermediate signal → slope = 1 (shot noise limited, since $\sigma^2 = S$).
- At high signal → slope = 2 (Fixed Pattern Noise dominated, since $\text{FPN} \propto S$).

This curve is the single most important diagnostic in machine-vision camera calibration.

## 2.6 Noise Equivalent Exposure & Dynamic Range

- **Noise Equivalent Exposure (NEE):** The signal level at which SNR = 1 (i.e., $S = \sigma_{\text{total}}$). Below this, the pixel cannot distinguish signal from noise.
- **Dynamic Range (DR):** Ratio of the full-well capacity $S_{\max}$ to the noise floor:
$$\text{DR} = \frac{S_{\max}}{\sigma_{\text{total,min}}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{S_{\max}}{\sigma_{\text{read}}}\right) \text{ dB}$$

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Shot noise std. dev. | $\sigma_{\text{shot}} = \sqrt{S}$ | electrons (e⁻) |
| 2 | Shot-noise SNR | $\text{SNR}_{\text{shot}} = \sqrt{S}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | SNR in dB | $\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(S/N)$ | dB |
| 4 | Dark noise std. dev. | $\sigma_{\text{dark}} = \sqrt{I_d \cdot t_{\text{int}}}$ | e⁻ |
| 5 | Total noise | $\sigma_{\text{total}}^{2} = \sigma_{\text{shot}}^{2} + \sigma_{\text{dark}}^{2} + \sigma_{\text{read}}^{2} + \ldots$ | e⁻ |
| 6 | Dynamic Range | $\text{DR} = S_{\max} / \sigma_{\text{read}}$ | linear ratio or dB |
| 7 | Conversion Gain | $K = q/C$ | V/e⁻ (q is electron charge) |
| 8 | Dark current vs T | $I_d \propto T^{1.5} e^{-E_g/(2k_BT)}$ | Arrhenius form |
| 9 | Boltz. constant | $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ | J/K |
| 10 | PTC slope regimes | 0 (read), 1 (shot), 2 (FPN) | log–log plot |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE APPLIED:** All absolute-value bars inside formulas have been replaced with explicit expressions to preserve the markdown table structure.

## 2.8 Real-World Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in machine vision:**
> - In **industrial inspection**, misclassification of a defective part as "good" is directly driven by low SNR — noisy pixels obscure cracks and scratches.
> - In **autonomous vehicles and robotics**, low-light scenes (night, tunnels) push cameras into the read-noise-limited regime, requiring low-read-noise sensors (sCMOS, BSI).
> - In **medical imaging** (X-ray, endoscopy), photon budget is limited by radiation dose; SNR optimization is a clinical necessity.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Photon Shot Noise Has the Form $\sqrt{S}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting point:** Photon arrival is a Poisson process.

**Step 1 — Definition of Poisson distribution.**
The probability of collecting exactly $k$ photoelectrons in a pixel during exposure time $t_{\text{int}}$ is:

$$
P(k) = \frac{\mu^{k} e^{-\mu}}{k!}
$$

where $\mu$ is the mean (and also the variance) of the distribution.

**Step 2 — Variance of a Poisson variable.**
By the definition of a Poisson process:

$$
\sigma^{2} = \mu
$$

**Step 3 — Identify $\mu$ with the signal $S$.**
The mean number of collected electrons is the signal $S$:

$$
S = \mu
$$

**Step 4 — Standard deviation.**
Take the square root of the variance:

$$
\sigma_{\text{shot}} = \sqrt{\sigma^{2}} = \sqrt{\mu} = \sqrt{S}
$$

**Step 5 — Shot-noise SNR.**

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{shot}} = \frac{S}{\sigma_{\text{shot}}} = \frac{S}{\sqrt{S}} = \sqrt{S}
$$

**Conclusion:** The SNR of a shot-noise-limited camera grows as the square-root of the signal. This is a **fundamental physical limit** that no amount of engineering can overcome.

## 3.2 Derivation: Dark Current Doubling Temperature

> [!NOTE]
> The dark current is dominated by Shockley–Read–Hall generation in the depletion region. Its temperature dependence is modeled as:

$$
I_d(T) = C \cdot T^{1.5} \cdot e^{-E_g / (2 k_B T)}
$$

where $C$ is a process-dependent constant.

**Step 1 — Take the ratio at two temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$.**

$$
\frac{I_d(T_2)}{I_d(T_1)} = \left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{1.5} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_2} - \frac{1}{T_1}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 2 — For the small temperature changes typical in cooling (a few °C), the $T^{1.5}$ prefactor is negligible compared to the exponential term.**

$$
\frac{I_d(T_2)}{I_d(T_1)} \approx \exp\!\left[\frac{E_g}{2 k_B}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Use silicon's $E_g = 1.12$ eV. Converting to Joules:**

$$
E_g = 1.12 \times 1.602\times 10^{-19} = 1.794 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the "doubling temperature" — the change in $T$ required to double $I_d$.**

Setting the ratio to 2 and solving for $\Delta T$ yields approximately **6 to 8 °C** for typical silicon imagers.

**Engineering takeaway:** Cooling the sensor by 20 °C reduces dark current by a factor of 8–16×.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Noise Dominance Map

| Illumination Regime | Dominant Noise | SNR Behavior | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Very low light ($S \ll \sigma_{\text{read}}^{2}$) | Read noise | $\text{SNR} \approx S / \sigma_{\text{read}}$ (linear in $S$) | Use sCMOS/EMCCD; bin pixels; cool sensor |
| Low light ($S \sim \sigma_{\text{read}}^{2}$) | Read + Shot | Transition regime | Increase exposure; use larger pixel |
| Mid light (shot-limited) | Shot noise | $\text{SNR} = \sqrt{S}$ (sqrt growth) | Cannot be improved; only more photons |
| High light | Fixed Pattern Noise (PRNU) | $\text{SNR} \propto S^{1/2}$ falling relative to FPN | Apply flat-field correction |
| Saturation | Clipping / Blooming | Signal lost | Use ND filter; shorten exposure |

## 3.4 Worked Example (Numerical, KTU-style)

**Problem:** A machine-vision camera has full-well capacity $S_{\max} = 30{,}000$ e⁻, read noise $\sigma_{\text{read}} = 5$ e⁻, and negligible dark noise. Calculate:

**(a)** The dynamic range in dB.

**(b)** The shot-noise SNR (in dB) at half-full-well.

**(c)** The total noise when the signal $S = 10{,}000$ e⁻.

---

**Solution:**

**(a)** Dynamic range:

$$
\text{DR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{30{,}000}{5}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(6000)
$$

$$
\text{DR} = 20 \times 3.778 = 75.57 \text{ dB}
$$

**(b)** Shot-noise SNR at $S = 15{,}000$ e⁻:

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{shot}} = \sqrt{15{,}000} = 122.47
$$

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(122.47) = 20 \times 2.088 = 41.76 \text{ dB}
$$

**(c)** Total noise at $S = 10{,}000$ e⁻:

$$
\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{\sigma_{\text{shot}}^{2} + \sigma_{\text{read}}^{2}} = \sqrt{10{,}000 + 25} = \sqrt{10{,}025}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{total}} \approx 100.12 \text{ e⁻}
$$

**Interpretation:** At 10,000 e⁻, read noise contributes only 0.025% of the total variance — the camera is firmly shot-noise-limited.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Noise Source Hierarchy in an Image Sensor (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Photon Flux Incident on Pixel] --> B[Photodiode Charge Collection]
    B --> C[Charge Transfer / Pixel Well]
    C --> D[Output Amplifier Stage]
    D --> E[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    E --> F[Digital Pixel Value]

    N1[Photon Shot Noise - Poisson] -.-> B
    N2[Dark Current Shot Noise - Thermal] -.-> C
    N3[Read Noise - Amplifier] -.-> D
    N4[Quantization Noise - ADC] -.-> E
    N5[Fixed Pattern Noise - PRNU / DSNU] -.-> F

    G[Total Noise Variance Sum] --> H[Camera Noise Performance Metric]
    N1 --> G
    N2 --> G
    N3 --> G
    N4 --> G
    N5 --> G
    G --> H

    style A fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#2d5a27,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#7a1f1f,color:#ffffff
    style N1 fill:#5a4a1f,color:#ffffff
    style N2 fill:#5a4a1f,color:#ffffff
    style N3 fill:#5a4a1f,color:#ffffff
    style N4 fill:#5a4a1f,color:#ffffff
    style N5 fill:#5a4a1f,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The solid arrows show the *signal path* (photon → electrons → voltage → bits). The dashed arrows show the *noise injection points*. Each stage injects its own stochastic disturbance, and they all add in variance at the end.

## 4.2 Photon Transfer Curve — Schematic Behavior

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph A[Low Signal Region]
        A1[Read Noise Dominant - Flat Slope 0 on Log-Log]
    end
    subgraph B[Mid Signal Region]
        B1[Shot Noise Dominant - Slope 1]
    end
    subgraph C[High Signal Region]
        C1[FPN Dominant - Slope 2]
    end
    A --> B --> C

    style A1 fill:#3a5a1f,color:#ffffff
    style B1 fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style C1 fill:#5a1f3a,color:#ffffff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** A common mistake is to confuse the *signal* axis with the *noise* axis. Remember: PTC plots **variance vs. mean signal** on log–log axes. The three slopes (0, 1, 2) are diagnostic of the dominant noise type.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: SNR Optimization Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Stage 1 Optical Design - High NA Lens, Anti-Reflection Coatings] --> P2[Stage 2 Sensor Selection - Low Read Noise, Large Pixel, BSI]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 3 Integration Control - Optimal Exposure, Gain Selection]
    P3 --> P4[Stage 4 Sensor Cooling - Thermoelectric Cooler TEC]
    P4 --> P5[Stage 5 On-Sensor Calibration - Correlated Double Sampling CDS]
    P5 --> P6[Stage 6 Off-Chip Correction - Dark Subtraction and Flat Fielding]
    P6 --> P7[Final Image with Maximized SNR]

    style P7 fill:#2d5a27,color:#ffffff
    style P1 fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style P2 fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style P3 fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style P4 fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style P5 fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
    style P6 fill:#1f3a5f,color:#ffffff
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define shot noise in an image sensor and write its standard deviation expression.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Shot noise is the random fluctuation in the number of photoelectrons collected by a pixel due to the discrete, statistical nature of photon arrival. Photon arrival follows a Poisson distribution. For an average signal $S$ photoelectrons, the standard deviation is:
> $$\sigma_{\text{shot}} = \sqrt{S}$$
> This is the fundamental, irreducible noise source in any photon-based imaging system. (1 mark — definition, 1 mark — Poisson basis, 1 mark — formula and significance)

---

**Q2. Distinguish between read noise and dark current noise in a CCD/CMOS sensor.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Read Noise | Dark Current Noise |
|---|---|---|
| Origin | Output amplifier and ADC | Thermal generation in silicon |
| Dependence on exposure time | Independent | Grows as $\sqrt{I_d \cdot t_{\text{int}}}$ |
| Dependence on temperature | Weak | Strong (exponential) |
| Mitigation | Better electronics, CDS | Sensor cooling (TEC) |
| Signal dependence | Constant floor | Accumulates with time |

(1 mark each for any 3 distinguishing criteria)

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1 Coverage]**

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the various sources of noise in a digital image sensor. Derive the expression for the total noise variance in a pixel.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A CMOS sensor has a full-well capacity of 25,000 electrons, read noise of 4 e⁻ RMS, and a dark current of 10 e⁻/s at room temperature. For an exposure of 100 ms with a mean signal of 8,000 photoelectrons, compute:
- (i) Shot noise
- (ii) Dark shot noise
- (iii) Total RMS noise
- (iv) SNR in dB

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Sources of noise (7 marks):**

The model answer must enumerate **all five** noise sources and assign them to physical stages. Examiner's expected structure:

1. **Photon Shot Noise** — generated at the photodiode; Poisson statistics; $\sigma_{\text{shot}} = \sqrt{S}$ (2 marks)
2. **Dark Current Noise** — thermal generation; $\sigma_{\text{dark}} = \sqrt{I_d \cdot t_{\text{int}}}$ (1.5 marks)
3. **Read Noise** — amplifier + ADC; signal-independent (1.5 marks)
4. **Fixed Pattern Noise (FPN)** — pixel mismatch (PRNU/DSNU) (1 mark)
5. **Quantization Noise** — ADC granularity, $\sigma_{\text{quant}} \approx \text{LSB}/\sqrt{12}$ (1 mark)

**Derivation of total noise (must include in the answer):**

Assuming statistically independent noise sources, variances add:

$$
\sigma_{\text{total}}^{2} = \sigma_{\text{shot}}^{2} + \sigma_{\text{dark}}^{2} + \sigma_{\text{read}}^{2} + \sigma_{\text{FPN}}^{2} + \sigma_{\text{quant}}^{2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{\sigma_{\text{shot}}^{2} + \sigma_{\text{dark}}^{2} + \sigma_{\text{read}}^{2} + \sigma_{\text{FPN}}^{2} + \sigma_{\text{quant}}^{2}}
$$

**Justification for variance addition:** Independent zero-mean random variables satisfy $\text{Var}(X+Y) = \text{Var}(X) + \text{Var}(Y)$. (1 mark for the derivation logic)

---

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

Given: $S_{\max} = 25{,}000$ e⁻, $\sigma_{\text{read}} = 4$ e⁻, $I_d = 10$ e⁻/s, $t_{\text{int}} = 0.1$ s, $S = 8{,}000$ e⁻.

**(i) Shot noise:**

$$
\sigma_{\text{shot}} = \sqrt{S} = \sqrt{8{,}000} \approx 89.44 \text{ e⁻}
$$

**(ii) Dark shot noise:**

$$
\sigma_{\text{dark}} = \sqrt{I_d \cdot t_{\text{int}}} = \sqrt{10 \times 0.1} = \sqrt{1} = 1.0 \text{ e⁻}
$$

**(iii) Total RMS noise:**

$$
\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{89.44^{2} + 1.0^{2} + 4^{2}}
$$

$$
\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sqrt{8{,}000 + 1 + 16} = \sqrt{8{,}017} \approx 89.54 \text{ e⁻}
$$

**(iv) SNR in dB:**

$$
\text{SNR} = \frac{8{,}000}{89.54} \approx 89.35
$$

$$
\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10}(89.35) \approx 39.02 \text{ dB}
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** The camera is shot-noise-limited — read and dark noise together contribute less than 0.3% of the variance.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is the Photon Transfer Curve (PTC)? Sketch its three characteristic regions on a log–log plot and explain what each region indicates about the dominant noise source.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the engineering strategies to minimize each of the following in a machine-vision camera:
- (i) Photon shot noise
- (ii) Dark current noise
- (iii) Read noise
- (iv) Fixed pattern noise

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Photon Transfer Curve (7 marks):**

The Photon Transfer Curve is a **log–log plot of pixel-signal variance vs. mean pixel signal**, measured experimentally by uniformly illuminating the sensor at multiple exposure levels. (2 marks for definition)

**Three characteristic regions (3 marks):**

| Region | Mean Signal | Slope on log–log | Dominant Noise |
|---|---|---|---|
| I — Floor | Very low | 0 (flat) | Read noise ($\sigma_{\text{read}}^{2}$) |
| II — Linear | Mid | 1 | Shot noise ($\sigma^{2} = S$) |
| III — Knee | High | 2 | Fixed Pattern Noise (PRNU) |

The transition between regions gives the **knee signal** (full-well / read-noise trade-off) and the **conversion gain** from the slope-1 region. (1 mark for engineering significance)

**Sketch must show:** x-axis = mean signal, y-axis = variance, three labelled regions with their slopes. (1 mark)

---

**(b) Noise mitigation strategies (7 marks — 1.75 marks each):**

**(i) Photon Shot Noise** — *Irreducible*. Cannot be removed, only mitigated by collecting more photons:
- Increase exposure time (within motion-blur limits)
- Use a larger aperture / lower f-number
- Use a sensor with larger pixel pitch (higher full well)
- Use pixel binning (sacrifices resolution for SNR)

**(ii) Dark Current Noise** — *Strongly temperature dependent*:
- Thermoelectric cooling (TEC) to $-10$°C or below
- Use **Multi-Pinned Phase (MPP)** mode in CCDs
- Minimize integration time
- Use **dark-frame subtraction** in software

**(iii) Read Noise** — *Electronics-limited*:
- Use **Correlated Double Sampling (CDS)** to suppress kTC noise
- Choose sensors with low read-noise specs (sCMOS, BSI CMOS)
- Operate at higher gain only when shot noise is the limit
- Use cooled scientific-grade cameras for ultra-low read noise (<1 e⁻)

**(iv) Fixed Pattern Noise** — *Pixel mismatch*:
- **Flat-field correction** using a uniform white reference
- **Dark-frame subtraction** for DSNU
- **Per-pixel gain calibration** for PRNU
- On-chip CDS and column-parallel ADCs to reduce column FPN

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing variance addition with standard deviation addition.** Noise sources add in *quadrature* (variance), NOT linearly. Writing $\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3$ loses full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert SNR to dB** when the question asks for dB. The factor is **20 log**, not 10 log, when the ratio is in amplitude/linear units.
> 3. **Mixing up PRNU and DSNU.** PRNU = Photo-Response Non-Uniformity (signal-dependent), DSNU = Dark Signal Non-Uniformity (offset-based). They appear in different PTC regions.
> 4. **Ignoring units.** Always carry the unit "electrons (e⁻)" in noise calculations; KTU examiners award partial credit for unit consistency.
> 5. **Treating dark current as deterministic.** Dark current itself is the *mean*; the *noise* from it is its square root, just like shot noise.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist**
>
> - **Shot noise** $\sigma = \sqrt{S}$ — Poisson, irreducible, fundamental.
> - **Dark noise** $\sigma = \sqrt{I_d \cdot t_{\text{int}}}$ — thermal, doubles every 6–8 °C.
> - **Read noise** — amplifier/ADC, constant floor, dominant in low light.
> - **FPN** has two flavors: **DSNU** (offset, dark) and **PRNU** (gain, illumination).
> - **Quantization noise** = LSB / $\sqrt{12}$ for a uniform ADC.
> - **Total noise** = quadrature sum of independent sources (variance addition).
> - **PTC** has 3 slopes: 0 (read), 1 (shot), 2 (FPN) on a log–log variance vs. mean plot.
> - **SNR (dB)** uses 20 log; **DR (dB)** uses 20 log of $S_{\max}/\sigma_{\text{read}}$.
> - **Dynamic range** = full well / read noise floor.
> - **Cooling** suppresses dark noise; **CDS** suppresses read noise; **flat-fielding** suppresses FPN; **more photons** is the only cure for shot noise.
> - **Conversion gain** $K = q/C$ converts electrons to volts; essential for variance-to-electrons conversion in PTC analysis.
> - In the **shot-noise-limited regime**, doubling the photons improves SNR by only $\sqrt{2}$ (a 3 dB, half-stop gain).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Range
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT635 - MACHINE VISION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Range

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Machine Vision** and digital imaging, **Dynamic Range (DR)** is formally defined as the ratio between the **maximum** and **minimum** measurable signal intensities (such as luminance, irradiance, or photo-electron counts) that an imaging system can capture, store, or display in a single acquisition, while maintaining acceptable signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and without saturation or being lost in the noise floor.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$DR = \frac{L_{\max}}{L_{\min}}$$

where $L_{\max}$ is the highest intensity that can be recorded without clipping/saturation, and $L_{\min}$ is the lowest intensity that can be distinguished from the noise floor (typically defined as the signal level where SNR = 1 or SNR = 3 dB).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In Module 1 (Fundamentals of Machine Vision), Dynamic Range is a foundational sensor specification. Examiners frequently test its relationship with **bit depth**, **noise floor**, and **High Dynamic Range (HDR) imaging** techniques.

## 1.2 Units of Measurement

Dynamic Range can be expressed in three standard forms used in KTU board examinations:

1. **Linear Ratio (unitless):** $\frac{L_{\max}}{L_{\min}}$ (e.g., 1000:1)
2. **Decibels (dB):** $DR_{dB} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{L_{\max}}{L_{\min}}\right)$
3. **Stops / F-stops (Photographic):** $DR_{stops} = \log_2\left(\frac{L_{\max}}{L_{\min}}\right)$ — Each stop represents a doubling of light.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Ear and Whisper" Intuition

Imagine you are standing in a noisy market trying to listen to a friend **whisper** a secret, while a **jet engine roars** 5 meters away.

- The **whisper** represents the darkest shadow in a scene (very low signal).
- The **Jet engine roar** represents the bright sun reflection on a metallic surface (very high signal).
- A machine vision sensor's **dynamic range** is exactly like your ear's ability to hear BOTH the whisper and the jet engine **simultaneously, without either one drowning out the other**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Machine Vision:** Industrial inspection often involves scenes with both very dark (e.g., shadowed crevices in a metallic weld) and very bright (e.g., specular highlights from polished steel) regions. A sensor with insufficient dynamic range will either **saturate** the bright regions (turning them pure white, losing detail) or **crush the shadows** into pure black (losing detail). Dynamic Range determines whether a single image can preserve BOTH.

## 1.4 Standard Reference Values

| Imaging System | Approximate Dynamic Range |
|---|---|
| **Human Eye** (simultaneous) | ~1000:1 to 4000:1 (≈ 10–12 stops) |
| Standard 8-bit camera | 256:1 (8 stops) |
| 12-bit industrial camera | 4096:1 (12 stops) |
| 16-bit scientific CCD | 65536:1 (16 stops) |
| HDR Imaging Systems | > 100,000:1 (> 17 stops) |
| Typical Smartphone Sensor | ~1000:1 to 3000:1 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — The Three Pillars of Dynamic Range

The dynamic range of a machine vision system is governed by three fundamental boundaries:

### Pillar 1: The Saturation Ceiling (Upper Limit) — $L_{\max}$
- Determined by the **Full Well Capacity (FWC)** of the photodiode — the maximum number of photo-electrons a pixel well can hold before it overflows.
- In digital terms, limited by the **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) saturation** point (e.g., for an 8-bit ADC, $L_{\max} = 2^8 - 1 = 255$).
- When exceeded → **pixel saturation** → loss of detail in highlights (clipping to pure white).

### Pillar 2: The Noise Floor (Lower Limit) — $L_{\min}$
- Determined by the **total system noise** $\sigma_{total}$, which comprises:
  - **Photon Shot Noise:** $\sigma_{photon} = \sqrt{N_{e^-}}$ (Poisson-distributed)
  - **Dark Current Noise:** thermally generated electrons
  - **Read Noise:** noise from the readout electronics (amplifier, ADC quantization)
  - **Quantization Noise:** inherent to the ADC
- A pixel is considered detectable only if its signal exceeds the noise floor (typically SNR ≥ 1 or 3).

### Pillar 3: The Bit Depth Bridge — $B$ bits
- The number of discrete grayscale levels: $N_{levels} = 2^B$
- Bit depth sets the **theoretical maximum** dynamic range an ideal (noise-free) digitizer can represent: $DR_{ideal} = 2^B - 1$.

## 2.2 Why Bit Depth Alone is Misleading — A KTU Favourite Trap

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Misconception:** "A 12-bit camera always has a dynamic range of 4096:1."
> 
> **REALITY:** Bit depth is the *theoretical upper bound* of digitization. The **effective** dynamic range is **always lower** than $2^B$ because of noise floor contamination. In real sensors, $DR_{effective} \approx \frac{FWC}{\sigma_{read}}$, which is often 5–15 dB less than the theoretical bit-depth value.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear Dynamic Range | $DR = \frac{L_{\max}}{L_{\min}}$ | unitless ratio |
| 2 | Dynamic Range in Decibels | $DR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{L_{\max}}{L_{\min}}\right)$ | dB |
| 3 | Dynamic Range in Stops | $DR_{stops} = \log_2\left(\frac{L_{\max}}{L_{\min}}\right)$ | stops / f-stops |
| 4 | Bit Depth ↔ Stops | $DR_{stops} = B$ (ideal, noise-free) | stops |
| 5 | Bit Depth ↔ Levels | $N_{levels} = 2^B$ | levels |
| 6 | Photon Shot Noise | $\sigma_{photon} = \sqrt{N_{e^-}}$ | electrons RMS |
| 7 | Effective DR (Sensor) | $DR_{eff} = \frac{FWC}{\sigma_{read}}$ | unitless |
| 8 | SNR at a Pixel | $SNR = \frac{S}{\sigma_{total}}$ | dimensionless (often in dB) |
| 9 | Stops to dB Conversion | $1\ \text{stop} = 6.0206\ \text{dB}$ | constant |
| 10 | HDR Exposure Fusion Ratio | $N_{exposures} = \lceil DR_{scene} - DR_{sensor} \rceil$ | exposures needed |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Table Safety Note:** All absolute-value style bars are rendered using `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table structure.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Dynamic range specification directly dictates the choice of imaging hardware in:

- **Automotive ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems):** Must handle both dark tunnels and bright daylight exits in a single frame → requires HDR sensors (≥ 120 dB).
- **Weld Seam Inspection:** Molten weld pools emit intense light while surrounding metal is dark.
- **Medical Endoscopy:** Dark internal cavities with bright specular reflections from wet tissue.
- **Satellite / Remote Sensing:** Extreme contrast between sun-lit surfaces and deep shadows.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From Linear Ratio to Decibels

**Given:** A machine vision CCD sensor has a Full Well Capacity (FWC) of $30{,}000\ e^-$ and a total read noise $\sigma_{read} = 12\ e^-$.

**Find:** (a) The linear dynamic range, (b) The dynamic range in dB, (c) The dynamic range in photographic stops.

**Step 1 — Identify the bounding values.**
The maximum detectable signal is the FWC before saturation:
$$L_{\max} = FWC = 30{,}000\ e^-$$

The minimum detectable signal is conventionally set at the read noise floor (the level at which the signal is statistically indistinguishable from noise):
$$L_{\min} = \sigma_{read} = 12\ e^-$$

**Step 2 — Compute the linear dynamic range (unitless ratio).**
$$DR = \frac{L_{\max}}{L_{\min}} = \frac{30{,}000}{12}$$

$$\boxed{DR = 2{,}500 : 1}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to decibels.**
Using the power-equivalent decibel form (since intensity is proportional to power per unit area):
$$DR_{dB} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\left(\frac{30{,}000}{12}\right)$$

$$DR_{dB} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}(2500)$$

$$DR_{dB} = 20 \times 3.3979$$

$$\boxed{DR_{dB} \approx 67.96\ \text{dB}}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to photographic stops.**
$$DR_{stops} = \log_2\left(\frac{30{,}000}{12}\right) = \log_2(2500)$$

Using the change-of-base identity $\log_2(x) = \frac{\log_{10}(x)}{\log_{10}(2)}$:
$$DR_{stops} = \frac{\log_{10}(2500)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{3.3979}{0.3010}$$

$$\boxed{DR_{stops} \approx 11.29\ \text{stops}}$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check using the stops ↔ dB identity.**
$$DR_{dB} = 11.29 \times 6.0206 \approx 67.97\ \text{dB} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship Between Bit Depth and Dynamic Range

**Theorem:** For an ideal noise-free digitizer with bit depth $B$, the maximum representable dynamic range is $2^B - 1$.

**Proof (Constructive):**

Consider a $B$-bit Analog-to-Digital Converter. The output is an integer code $c \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 2^B - 1\}$.

The minimum representable non-zero signal corresponds to 1 LSB (Least Significant Bit):
$$L_{\min} = 1\ \text{LSB}$$

The maximum representable signal corresponds to the full-scale code:
$$L_{\max} = (2^B - 1) \cdot \text{LSB}$$

Therefore:
$$DR_{ideal} = \frac{L_{\max}}{L_{\min}} = \frac{(2^B - 1) \cdot \text{LSB}}{1 \cdot \text{LSB}}$$

$$\boxed{DR_{ideal} = 2^B - 1}$$

In photographic stops:
$$DR_{stops} = \log_2(2^B - 1) \approx B \quad \text{(for large B)}$$

**Engineering Implication:** A 10-bit camera (1024 levels) can ideally encode 10 stops of dynamic range, IF its noise floor is below 1 LSB. In practice, the noise floor is usually several LSBs, so effective DR is less.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Required Number of Exposures for HDR Fusion

**Given:** A scene with $DR_{scene} = 200{,}000:1$ must be captured using a sensor with $DR_{sensor} = 1000:1$ (10-bit, typical).

**Find:** The minimum number of exposures $N$ needed for a multi-exposure HDR fusion.

**Step 1 — Express both ranges in stops.**
$$DR_{scene,\ stops} = \log_2(200{,}000) = \frac{\log_{10}(200{,}000)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{5.301}{0.3010} \approx 17.61\ \text{stops}$$

$$DR_{sensor,\ stops} = \log_2(1000) = \frac{3.0}{0.3010} \approx 9.97\ \text{stops}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the additional dynamic range needed.**
$$\Delta DR_{stops} = DR_{scene} - DR_{sensor} = 17.61 - 9.97 = 7.64\ \text{stops}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the number of exposures.**
Each successive exposure typically shifts the dynamic range window by 1 stop (2× light multiplier). The number of exposures is:
$$N = \lceil \Delta DR_{stops} \rceil + 1 = \lceil 7.64 \rceil + 1$$

$$\boxed{N = 9\ \text{exposures}}$$

**Step 4 — Verification in linear domain.**
Total linear coverage = $1000 \times 2^8 = 256{,}000$ (using 8 additional stops of 2× multiplication). Since $256{,}000 > 200{,}000$, the coverage is sufficient. $\checkmark$

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Low DR System (8-bit) | High DR System (16-bit / HDR) |
|---|---|---|
| $L_{\max}/L_{\min}$ | 256:1 | 65,536:1 or higher |
| $DR_{dB}$ | 48 dB | 96 dB |
| $DR_{stops}$ | 8 stops | 16+ stops |
| Shadow Detail | Poor (crushed) | Excellent |
| Highlight Detail | Prone to clipping | Preserved |
| File Size / Throughput | Low | High (often needs compression) |
| Typical Use Case | Webcams, basic QC | Scientific, medical, automotive |
| Cost | Low | High |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Three Boundaries of Dynamic Range

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incident Light on Pixel] --> B[Photodiode Charge Accumulation]
    B --> C{Signal > Full Well Capacity?}
    C -- Yes --> D[SATURATION: Pixel clipped to MAX value]
    C -- No --> E[Signal within dynamic window]
    E --> F{Signal > Noise Floor?}
    F -- No --> G[SUB-THRESHOLD: Signal lost in noise]
    F -- Yes --> H[Valid Detectable Signal]
    D --> I[Effective Dynamic Range Window]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Analog-to-Digital Conversion]
    J --> K[Digital Image Output]
    
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style F fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style H fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#4169E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style J fill:#9370DB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style K fill:#2E8B57,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Multi-Exposure HDR Fusion Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Scene with High Dynamic Range] --> A1[Capture Exposure 1: Short]
    A0 --> A2[Capture Exposure 2: Medium]
    A0 --> A3[Capture Exposure 3: Long]
    A0 --> A4[Capture Exposure N: Variable]
    
    A1 --> B1[Image L1]
    A2 --> B2[Image L2]
    A3 --> B3[Image L3]
    A4 --> B4[Image LN]
    
    B1 --> C[Alignment and Ghost Removal]
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    B4 --> C
    
    C --> D[Radiance Map Recovery]
    D --> E[Tone Mapping Operator]
    E --> F[Display Referred HDR Image]
    
    style A0 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style A1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style A2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style A3 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style A4 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style D fill:#6A5ACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style E fill:#9370DB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style F fill:#228B22,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Dynamic Range in the Machine Vision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Scene Radiance] --> P2[Optics and Lens]
    P2 --> P3[Sensor Pixel Array]
    P3 --> P4[Analog Front End]
    P4 --> P5[ADC]
    P5 --> P6[Digital Processing]
    P6 --> P7[Output Image]
    
    P3 -.->|Defines| Q1[Full Well Capacity]
    P4 -.->|Defines| Q2[Read Noise]
    P5 -.->|Defines| Q3[Bit Depth B]
    
    Q1 --> R[Effective Dynamic Range]
    Q2 --> R
    Q3 --> R
    
    R --> S{DR_scene > DR_sensor?}
    S -- Yes --> T[Need HDR Technique]
    S -- No --> U[Single Exposure Sufficient]
    
    style P1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P3 fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P5 fill:#4682B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style Q1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Q2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Q3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R fill:#4169E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style S fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style T fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style U fill:#32CD32,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**Define Dynamic Range in a machine vision system. Express it in three different units.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Dynamic Range (DR) is the ratio of the maximum to the minimum light intensity that an imaging sensor can capture in a single exposure, where the maximum is bounded by pixel saturation (Full Well Capacity) and the minimum is bounded by the sensor's noise floor. It is expressed as:

1. **Linear Ratio:** $DR = \frac{L_{\max}}{L_{\min}}$ (e.g., 1000:1)
2. **Decibels:** $DR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{L_{\max}}{L_{\min}}\right)$
3. **Photographic Stops:** $DR_{stops} = \log_2\left(\frac{L_{\max}}{L_{\min}}\right)$

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for any two units with formulas, 1 mark for completeness.)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]**
**Differentiate between the theoretical dynamic range (from bit depth) and the effective dynamic range of a real sensor. Which is greater, and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Theoretical DR | Effective DR |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $2^B - 1$ | $FWC / \sigma_{read}$ |
| Bounded by | Bit depth $B$ | Noise floor $\sigma_{read}$ |
| Assumes | Noise-free digitizer | Real noise sources present |

**Theoretical DR is always ≥ Effective DR** (1 mark). This is because a real sensor's effective dynamic range is limited by **read noise, dark current, and quantization noise**, which raise the minimum detectable signal above 1 LSB. The bit depth only describes the ADC's resolution; the analog front-end (sensor + amplifier) determines how many of those bits are *useful*. *(1 mark for reasoning, 1 mark for noise sources.)*

---

## Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — Path 1: Computation + Theory

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**A 12-bit machine vision camera has a Full Well Capacity of $50{,}000\ e^-$ and a total read noise of $8\ e^-$.**

**(a)** Calculate the linear dynamic range, express it in decibels, and convert it to photographic stops. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of High Dynamic Range (HDR) imaging. Under what condition is HDR essential in machine vision? Mention two real-world industrial applications where HDR cameras are mandatory. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Step-by-step computation:**

Step 1: Identify the boundaries.
- $L_{\max} = FWC = 50{,}000\ e^-$
- $L_{\min} = \sigma_{read} = 8\ e^-$

Step 2: Linear ratio (2 marks).
$$DR = \frac{50{,}000}{8} = 6{,}250 : 1$$

Step 3: Decibel form (2 marks).
$$DR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(6250) = 20 \times 3.7959 = 75.92\ \text{dB}$$

Step 4: Photographic stops (3 marks).
$$DR_{stops} = \log_2(6250) = \frac{\log_{10}(6250)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{3.7959}{0.3010} = 12.61\ \text{stops}$$

**Part (b) — HDR Concept and Application (7 marks):**

**Definition (3 marks):** High Dynamic Range (HDR) imaging is a set of techniques — including multi-exposure fusion, tone mapping, and specialized logarithmic or split-pixel sensors — used to capture, store, and display a scene whose luminance range **exceeds the dynamic range of a single conventional sensor exposure**. The goal is to preserve detail in both extreme shadows and extreme highlights within one composite image.

**Condition for Necessity (2 marks):** HDR is essential when the **scene dynamic range ($DR_{scene}$) exceeds the sensor dynamic range ($DR_{sensor}$)** — i.e., when $DR_{scene} > DR_{sensor}$. The number of exposures required is approximately $N = \lceil \log_2(DR_{scene}/DR_{sensor}) \rceil + 1$.

**Two Industrial Applications (2 marks):**
1. **Automotive Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS):** Simultaneously handle dark tunnel interiors and bright daylight exits; the European NCAP protocols mandate ≥ 120 dB dynamic range.
2. **Electronic Component Soldering Inspection:** Distinguish glowing molten solder joints from dark PCB substrate in reflow oven monitoring.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Path 2: Theory-Focused Alternative

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a neat labelled sketch, describe the operational principle of a Charge-Coupled Device (CCD) sensor and explain how its **Full Well Capacity (FWC)** and **Read Noise** jointly determine the effective dynamic range. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A scene has a luminance range from $0.5\ \text{cd/m}^2$ (deep shadow) to $250{,}000\ \text{cd/m}^2$ (direct sunlight reflection). A standard 8-bit camera is used. Determine:
   - (i) The scene dynamic range in stops. 
   - (ii) The sensor dynamic range in stops. 
   - (iii) Whether HDR fusion is required, and if so, the minimum number of exposures needed (assuming each exposure adds 1 stop of coverage). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — CCD Operational Principle and DR (7 marks):**

**Operational Principle (4 marks):** A CCD (Charge-Coupled Device) is a semiconductor image sensor where each pixel consists of a **MOS capacitor (photogate)** that accumulates photo-generated electrons in a potential well during exposure. The accumulated charge is proportional to the incident light intensity integrated over the exposure time. After exposure, the charge packets are **sequentially transferred** from pixel to pixel across the chip (charge coupling) to a single output amplifier at the chip edge, where they are converted to a voltage and then digitized by an ADC.

**Role of FWC and Read Noise in DR (3 marks):**
- The **Full Well Capacity (FWC)** is the maximum number of electrons the pixel well can hold before overflowing into adjacent pixels (blooming). It sets $L_{\max}$.
- The **Read Noise** $\sigma_{read}$ is the RMS noise contributed by the output amplifier and reset transistor during charge-to-voltage conversion. It sets $L_{\min}$ (typically defined as the signal level for SNR = 1).
- Therefore: $\displaystyle DR_{eff} = \frac{FWC}{\sigma_{read}}$, and in stops: $DR_{stops} = \log_2(FWC / \sigma_{read})$.

**Part (b) — Numerical Analysis (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (i): Scene dynamic range in stops (2 marks).**
$$DR_{scene,\ stops} = \log_2\left(\frac{250{,}000}{0.5}\right) = \log_2(500{,}000)$$
$$= \frac{\log_{10}(500{,}000)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{5.6990}{0.3010} \approx 18.93\ \text{stops}$$

**Step 2 (ii): Sensor dynamic range in stops (2 marks).**
For an ideal 8-bit ADC (theoretical max):
$$DR_{sensor,\ stops} = \log_2(2^8 - 1) = \log_2(255) \approx 7.99\ \text{stops} \approx 8\ \text{stops}$$

**Step 3 (iii): HDR requirement and exposure count (3 marks).**
Compare: $DR_{scene} = 18.93\ \text{stops} > DR_{sensor} = 8\ \text{stops}$ → **HDR fusion IS required.** (1 mark)

Additional dynamic range needed:
$$\Delta DR = 18.93 - 8 = 10.93\ \text{stops}$$

Number of exposures (each adds 1 stop):
$$N = \lceil 10.93 \rceil + 1 = 11 + 1 = 12\ \text{exposures}$$

$$\boxed{N_{min} = 12\ \text{exposures}}$$

*(Accept N = 12, since 12 exposures give $2^{11} = 2048\times$ the linear range of one exposure, i.e., 19 stops of total coverage, exceeding the required 18.93.)* ✓

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "+1" in exposure count:** Many students compute $N = \lceil \Delta DR \rceil$ and answer 11. The base exposure itself counts — the correct formula is $N = \lceil \Delta DR \rceil + 1$. (Lose 1 mark.)
> 2. **Using $2^B$ instead of $2^B - 1$:** For small $B$, this is acceptable, but examiners at KTU often expect $2^B - 1$ for full marks. (Lose 0.5 mark.)
