```mermaid
    P1 --> Q1[Intermediate Matrix G m v of size M by N]
    Q1 --> P2[Stage 2: 1D FFT on Each Column of G]
    P2 --> Q2[Final 2D Spectrum F u v of size M by N]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common 7-mark question is: *"Explain the separability property of the 2D DFT with a block diagram."* The diagram above plus the derivation in Section 3.2 gives you a full-mark answer.

## 4.3 Frequency-Domain Filter Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Frequency Domain Filters H u v] --> B[Low Pass Filter]
    A --> C[High Pass Filter]
    A --> D[Band Pass Filter]
    A --> E[Band Stop Filter]
    B --> B1[Ideal LPF Butterworth LPF Gaussian LPF]
    C --> C1[Ideal HPF Butterworth HPF Gaussian HPF Laplacian]
    D --> D1[Allows specific frequency band]
    E --> E1[Removes specific frequency band e.g. 50 Hz noise]
```

## 4.4 Visualisation: Spatial vs Frequency Domain Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Spatial Domain Image f m n] -->|2D DFT| B[Frequency Domain F u v]
    B -->|Magnitude| C[Log Magnitude Spectrum]
    B -->|Phase| D[Phase Spectrum]
    B -->|Inverse 2D DFT| A
    C --> E[Used for visual inspection of frequency content]
    D --> F[Used for image reconstruction and edge alignment]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Centered Spectrum Convention:** Almost all KTU diagrams show the *shifted* (centered) spectrum. To center an unshifted spectrum, multiply the spatial image by $(-1)^{m+n}$ *before* taking the DFT, or apply the function `fftshift` in MATLAB/Python.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

### Question 1: Define the Discrete Fourier Transform. State the 1D forward and inverse DFT equations and explain the role of the twiddle factor.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) is a finite, discrete mathematical operation that decomposes a discrete-time signal of length $N$ into $N$ orthogonal complex exponential components, producing a discrete frequency-domain representation.

The **1D Forward DFT** is:

$$X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) \, e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad k = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

The **1D Inverse DFT** is:

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N}, \quad n = 0, 1, \dots, N-1$$

The **twiddle factor** $W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N}$ is a complex unit-magnitude number that compactly represents the complex exponential kernel. It allows efficient computation and reveals the periodicity and symmetry properties of the transform. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2: What is the magnitude spectrum and phase spectrum of the 2D DFT? Why is the phase spectrum considered more important for image structure than the magnitude spectrum?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
For a 2D DFT output $F(u, v) = R(u, v) + j I(u, v)$:
- The **magnitude spectrum** is $\vert F(u, v) \vert = \sqrt{R^2(u, v) + I^2(u, v)}$ — it represents the *strength* of each frequency component.
- The **phase spectrum** is $\phi(u, v) = \arctan[I(u, v) / R(u, v)]$ — it represents the *spatial alignment* of those frequencies.

The phase spectrum preserves the **edge and structural information** of the image. Empirical studies (and standard KTU references) show that reconstructing an image using only the phase (with magnitude set to 1) retains recognizable structures, whereas reconstruction using only the magnitude (with phase set to 0) yields an unrecognizable blob. Hence the phase spectrum is more critical for preserving image structure. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Model Paper, Module 2]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### Part (a) — Derive the 1D Inverse DFT from the forward DFT using the orthogonality of complex exponentials. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Start with the assumed inverse relation:**

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} X(k) \, e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the forward DFT definition** $X(k) = \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(m) e^{-j 2\pi k m / N}$ into the expression:

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \left[ \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(m) \, e^{-j 2\pi k m / N} \right] e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$$

**Step 3 — Interchange the order of summation:**

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(m) \left[ \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j 2\pi k (n - m) / N} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the inner sum** using the geometric-series identity $\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} r^k = \dfrac{1 - r^N}{1 - r}$ with $r = e^{j 2\pi (n-m)/N}$:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j 2\pi k (n - m) / N} = \frac{1 - e^{j 2\pi (n - m)}}{1 - e^{j 2\pi (n - m) / N}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply orthogonality:** If $(n - m)$ is an integer multiple of $N$, the numerator $1 - e^{j 2\pi (n - m)} = 0$ *and* the denominator is also $0$ (limit case), giving the value $N$. Otherwise the numerator is $0$ and the denominator is non-zero, giving $0$:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N-1} e^{j 2\pi k (n - m) / N} = N \cdot \delta_{(m) \bmod N, \, n}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back and simplify:**

$$x(n) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{m=0}^{N-1} x(m) \cdot N \cdot \delta_{(m) \bmod N, \, n} = x(n) \quad \blacksquare$$

Thus, the IDFT correctly reconstructs the original sequence. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Explain the 2D DFT with its separability property. Discuss how this property simplifies computation. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition of 2D DFT:** For a discrete image $f(m, n)$ of size $M \times N$:

$$F(u, v) = \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} f(m, n) \, e^{-j 2\pi (u m / M + v n / N)}$$

**Step 2 — Express as Twiddle Factors:**

$$F(u, v) = \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} f(m, n) \, W_M^{um} \, W_N^{vn}$$

**Step 3 — Separability:** The exponential is a product of two single-variable exponentials, allowing the double sum to be split into two sequential single sums:

$$F(u, v) = \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} \left[ e^{-j 2\pi u m / M} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} f(m, n) \, e^{-j 2\pi v n / N} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Two-Pass Interpretation:** First, perform a 1D DFT along each row of the image (treating each row as a length-$N$ sequence). This produces an intermediate image $G(m, v)$ of size $M \times N$:

$$G(m, v) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} f(m, n) \, W_N^{vn}$$

Then perform a 1D DFT along each column of $G$ to obtain the final 2D spectrum:

$$F(u, v) = \sum_{m=0}^{M-1} G(m, v) \, W_M^{um}$$

**Step 5 — Computational Saving:** A direct 2D DFT requires $M^2 N^2$ complex multiplications. The separable form requires only $M N (M + N)$ multiplications. For a $512 \times 512$ image, this is a reduction by a factor of about $256$, and combined with FFT, by several orders of magnitude.

**Step 6 — Engineering Impact:** This separability principle is the mathematical foundation of all practical 2D FFT libraries (FFTW, MATLAB's `fft2`, OpenCV's `dft`) used in machine vision preprocessing pipelines. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### Part (a) — State and prove the convolution theorem for the 2D DFT. Discuss its significance in image filtering. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Statement of the Theorem:**

$$f(m, n) \ast g(m, n) \;\longleftrightarrow\; F(u, v) \cdot G(u, v)$$

where $\ast$ denotes **circular convolution** and $\cdot$ denotes pointwise multiplication.

**Step 2 — Forward Proof:** Let $h(m, n) = f(m, n) \ast g(m, n) = \sum_{a}\sum_{b} f(a, b) \, g(m - a, n - b)$. Compute its DFT:

$$H(u, v) = \sum_{m}\sum_{n} \left[ \sum_{a}\sum_{b} f(a, b) g(m - a, n - b) \right] e^{-j 2\pi (u m / M + v n / N)}$$

**Step 3 — Interchange summation order:**

$$H(u, v) = \sum_{a}\sum_{b} f(a, b) \left[ \sum_{m}\sum_{n} g(m - a, n - b) e^{-j 2\pi (u m / M + v n / N)} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Apply the shift property of the DFT** to the inner sum: $\sum_{m}\sum_{n} g(m - a, n - b) e^{-j 2\pi (u m / M + v n / N)} = G(u, v) \, e^{-j 2\pi (u a / M + v b / N)}$.

**Step 5 — Rearrange to factor out $G(u, v)$:**

$$H(u, v) = G(u, v) \left[ \sum_{a}\sum_{b} f(a, b) \, e^{-j 2\pi (u a / M + v b / N)} \right]$$

The bracketed term is precisely $F(u, v)$. Hence $H(u, v) = F(u, v) \cdot G(u, v)$. $\blacksquare$

**Step 6 — Significance in Image Filtering:** Spatial-domain convolution of an image with a filter kernel is computationally expensive ($O(M^2 N^2)$ for naive convolution). Using the convolution theorem, we can:
1. Compute $F(u, v) = \text{DFT}\{f(m, n)\}$ (fast via FFT).
2. Compute $G(u, v) = \text{DFT}\{g(m, n)\}$ (precomputed once).
3. Multiply: $H(u, v) = F(u, v) \cdot G(u, v)$ (pointwise, $O(MN)$).
4. Inverse DFT: $h(m, n) = \text{IDFT}\{H(u, v)\}$.

This converts a heavy convolution into a fast pointwise multiplication, used universally in image smoothing, sharpening, and edge detection. **(7 marks)**

#### Part (b) — Explain the rotation property and Parseval's theorem for the 2D DFT. Show that the 2D DFT preserves image energy. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Rotation Property (Statement):** If $f(r, \theta)$ in polar coordinates is rotated by angle $\theta_0$ to produce $f(r, \theta + \theta_0)$, then the DFT is also rotated by the same angle:

$$f(r, \theta + \theta_0) \;\longleftrightarrow\; F(\omega, \phi + \theta_0)$$

This is intuitive because the 2D DFT is a **separable, isotropic linear operator** acting on the image. A rotation in the spatial domain corresponds to an identical rotation in the frequency domain.

**Step 2 — Parseval's Theorem (Statement):** The total energy of an image is preserved between the spatial and frequency domains:

$$\sum_{m=0}^{M-1} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} \vert f(m, n) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{M N} \sum_{u=0}^{M-1} \sum_{v=0}^{N-1} \vert F(u, v) \vert^2$$

**Step 3 — Proof of Parseval's Theorem:** Start with the right-hand side:

$$E_F = \frac{1}{M N} \sum_{u}\sum_{v} \vert F(u, v) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{M N} \sum_{u}\sum_{v} F(u, v) \, F^*(u, v)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the DFT definition** for $F$ and $F^*$:

$$E_F = \frac{1}{M N} \sum_{u}\sum_{v} \left[ \sum_{m}\sum_{n} f(m, n) e^{-j 2\pi (u m / M + v n / N)} \right] \left[ \sum_{a}\sum_{b} f^*(a, b) e^{j 2\pi (u a / M + v b / N)} \right]$$

**Step 5 — Reorganize summations** and use the orthogonality relation (as in Section 3.1) to collapse the double sums. The result is:

$$E_F = \sum_{m}\sum_{n} \vert f(m, n) \vert^2 = E_f \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Meaning:** Parseval's theorem guarantees that **no information (energy) is lost** during the DFT — the transform is unitary up to the $1/(MN)$ scaling factor. This is why the IDFT can perfectly reconstruct the original image. It is also used in **Wiener filtering**, where the signal-to-noise ratio in the frequency domain is computed via energy comparison. **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU DFT Questions:**
>
> 1. **Missing the $1/N$ factor in the IDFT.** Many students write $x(n) = \sum X(k) e^{j 2\pi k n / N}$ (without $1/N$). This is mathematically wrong and immediately loses 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing the sign of the exponent.** Forward DFT uses $-j$, inverse uses $+j$. Swapping the sign is a 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Forgetting to center the spectrum.** When asked to "draw the magnitude spectrum," students draw the unshifted version with low frequencies at the corners. Always use `fftshift` (multiply by $(-1)^{m+n}$).
> 4. **Confusing DFT with DTFT and FFT.** The DFT is the *mathematical transform*. The FFT is the *algorithm* to compute it. The DTFT is the continuous-frequency version. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Ignoring the periodicity of DFT outputs.** $X(k)$ is periodic with period $N$. When stating properties, you must include this.
> 6. **Writing real-domain convolution when frequency-domain is needed.** If the question says "apply a low-pass filter in the frequency domain," you must multiply $F(u, v)$ with a filter $H(u, v)$, not convolve in space.
> 7. **Forgetting the units / interpretation.** $u, v$ are in *cycles per image width*. Many students write "Hz" which is wrong for spatial DFT.
> 8. **Skipping the derivation steps in long-answer questions.** KTU examiners award step marks — a partial derivation with at least 2 correct algebraic transitions earns 4 out of 7 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **DFT Definition:** $X(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1} x(n) e^{-j 2\pi k n / N}$; **IDFT** has $\frac{1}{N}$ and $+j$ in the exponent.
- **2D DFT:** $F(u,v) = \sum_{m}\sum_{n} f(m,n) \, e^{-j 2\pi (u m / M + v n / N)}$ — the foundation of all frequency-domain image processing.
- **Twiddle Factor:** $W_N = e^{-j 2\pi / N}$; satisfies $W_N^{N/2} = -1$, $W_N^N = 1$, and is conjugate-symmetric.
- **Three Key Spectra:** Magnitude $\vert F \vert$, Phase $\phi = \arctan(I/R)$, Power $P = \vert F \vert^2$.
- **Periodicity (both domains):** $x(n+N) = x(n)$ and $X(k+N) = X(k)$.
- **Separability:** 2D DFT = 1D DFT on rows → 1D DFT on columns. Computational saving is massive.
- **Convolution Theorem:** Spatial circular convolution $\Leftrightarrow$ Frequency pointwise multiplication. Powers all frequency-domain filtering.
- **Shift Property:** Multiplying the spatial image by $e^{j 2\pi (u_0 m / M + v_0 n / N)}$ shifts the spectrum; multiplying by $(-1)^{m+n}$ centers the spectrum.
- **Rotation Property:** Rotation in space $\Leftrightarrow$ identical rotation in frequency (in polar coordinates).
- **Parseval's Theorem:** Total energy is conserved — $\sum \vert f \vert^2 = \frac{1}{MN} \sum \vert F \vert^2$.
- **DC Component:** $F(0,0) = \sum \sum f(m,n)$ — the sum of all pixel intensities.
- **DFT vs FFT:** Same transform; FFT is the $O(N \log N)$ fast algorithm.
- **DFT vs DTFT:** DTFT is continuous in frequency (periodic); DFT samples DTFT at $N$ points.
- **Sampling Resolution:** $\Delta u = 1/M$, $\Delta v = 1/N$ cycles/pixel.
- **Practical Use:** Image filtering, JPEG compression, MRI k-space, texture analysis, noise removal.
- **Always remember:** $j = \sqrt{-1}$, $e^{j\theta} = \cos\theta + j\sin\theta$, $\vert e^{j\theta} \vert = 1$.
- **Memory trick for IDFT sign:** "*Inverse has the plus sign*": $+j 2\pi k n / N$ in the exponent and $\frac{1}{N}$ out front.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for the KTU Desk:**
> **"DFT = Decompose Frequency Totally"** — Decompose the image into its frequency components Totally.
> **"IDFT = Invert Domain, Form Time"** — Take the frequencies and form back the spatial image.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Projective Transformations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT635 - MACHINE VISION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Projective Transformations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme — Machine Vision (PEBRT635), Module 2**, a **Projective Transformation** (also called a **Homography** or **Perspectivity**) is defined as an invertible mapping between two projective planes that preserves the incidence structure — i.e., points map to points and lines map to lines.

In **2D**, a projective transformation is mathematically expressed as a mapping from the projective plane $\mathbb{P}^2$ to itself. Given a point $\mathbf{x} = (x, y)^T$ in the source plane, its image $\mathbf{x}' = (x', y')^T$ under homography $\mathbf{H}$ is:

$$\mathbf{x}' = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}, \quad \mathbf{H} \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times 3}$$

Because the transformation operates in **homogeneous coordinates**, $\mathbf{H}$ is defined only up to a non-zero scalar factor, giving **8 degrees of freedom (DOF)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A projective transformation preserves **incidence** (a point lies on a line iff its image lies on the image of that line) and the **cross-ratio** of four collinear points. It does **NOT** preserve parallelism, angles, or lengths.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are holding a transparent glass sheet (a *plane*) and someone else holds an identical sheet at a tilted angle. If you draw a straight line on your sheet and project it onto the other by drawing the line of sight from a flashlight through your sheet onto theirs, the resulting line on the tilted sheet will still be straight — but two originally parallel lines may now **converge at a vanishing point**.

This is exactly what happens when a camera photographs a flat scene (like a billboard or a chessboard) from two different viewpoints. The pixel coordinate of any point on the board in the first image and the pixel coordinate of the same physical point in the second image are linked by a **projective transformation**.

**Real-world example:** Panoramic image stitching in your smartphone uses projective transformations to "warp" overlapping photos so they appear as one continuous wide-angle image.

### 1.3 Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Homography** $\mathbf{H}$ | The $3 \times 3$ matrix encoding the projective mapping |
| **Projective Plane** $\mathbb{P}^2$ | The Euclidean plane augmented with points at infinity |
| **Vanishing Point** | The image of a point at infinity under perspective projection |
| **Cross-ratio** | A projective invariant of four collinear points |
| **Homogeneous Coordinates** | A point $(x,y)$ represented as $\lambda(x, y, 1)^T$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** Every planar scene viewed by a pinhole camera from any two viewpoints is related by a homography. This is why homographies are the backbone of **image rectification**, **camera calibration**, and **augmented reality overlays**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How a Projective Transformation Works

A 2D projective transformation is implemented through the following structured logic:

1. **Lift to homogeneous coordinates:** A Euclidean 2D point $(x, y)$ is embedded as a 3-vector $\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = (x, y, 1)^T$.
2. **Apply the $3 \times 3$ matrix $\mathbf{H}$:** Multiplication $\tilde{\mathbf{x}}' = \mathbf{H}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ yields a new 3-vector.
3. **Normalize (perspective divide):** Divide by the third coordinate to obtain the image point in the Euclidean plane.

The 8 DOF arise because the $3 \times 3$ matrix has **9 entries**, but one degree is consumed by the arbitrary scale factor.

- **The "Why":** Working in homogeneous coordinates is mathematically elegant — it converts the non-linear perspective division into a single linear matrix multiplication. This is the same trick that makes **pinhole camera projection** tractable.
- **The "How":** When you have $\geq 4$ point correspondences between two views of a planar scene, you can stack their equations and solve the linear system $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{h} = \mathbf{0}$ (where $\mathbf{h}$ is the flattened $\mathbf{H}$) for the homography up to scale using techniques like **Direct Linear Transform (DLT)**.

### 2.2 Properties Preserved vs. Not Preserved

| Transformation Class | DOF | Preserved Properties |
|---|---|---|
| **Euclidean** (Isometry) | 4 | Lengths, angles, areas, incidence |
| **Similarity** | 5 | Angles, ratios of lengths, incidence |
| **Affine** | 6 | Parallelism, ratios of lengths on parallel lines, incidence |
| **Projective** | 8 | Incidence, cross-ratio, conic-to-conic |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy rule:** Each class is a strict subset of the one above. Every similarity is an affine transformation; every affine is a projective transformation.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Notation & Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x' = \dfrac{h_{11}x + h_{12}y + h_{13}}{h_{31}x + h_{32}y + h_{33}}$ | Projective $x$-coordinate (perspective divide) |
| 2 | $y' = \dfrac{h_{21}x + h_{22}y + h_{23}}{h_{31}x + h_{32}y + h_{33}}$ | Projective $y$-coordinate (perspective divide) |
| 3 | $\lambda \begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} h_{11} & h_{12} & h_{13} \\ h_{21} & h_{22} & h_{23} \\ h_{31} & h_{32} & h_{33} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Compact matrix form, $\lambda \neq 0$ |
| 4 | $\text{DOF}(\mathbf{H}) = 9 - 1 = 8$ | Degrees of freedom (scale-ambiguous $3 \times 3$) |
| 5 | $N_{pts} \geq \dfrac{8}{2} = 4$ | Minimum point correspondences required |
| 6 | $(A, B; C, D) = \dfrac{\overline{CA} / \overline{CB}}{\overline{DA} / \overline{DB}}$ | Cross-ratio of four collinear points |
| 7 | $(A, B; C, D)_{\text{projected}} = (A', B'; C', D')$ | Cross-ratio invariance under homography |
| 8 | $\mathbf{H}_{\text{affine}} = \begin{bmatrix} a_{11} & a_{12} & t_x \\ a_{21} & a_{22} & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Affine sub-case (6 DOF, $h_{31}=h_{32}=0$) |
| 9 | $\mathbf{H}_{\text{similarity}} = s\begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta & t_x \\ \sin\theta & \cos\theta & t_y \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | Similarity sub-case (5 DOF) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Bird's-eye view transformation of road scenes for lane detection uses planar homography.
- **Medical Imaging:** Aligning pre-operative 3D scans to intra-operative 2D X-ray images.
- **Document Scanning:** Mobile apps that "flatten" photographed pages of a book into clean rectangular scans.
- **Robotics:** Top-down mapping of warehouse floors from oblique camera views.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — From Point Correspondence to the Homography Equation

**Step 1 — Write the projective mapping explicitly.** Given the homography matrix $\mathbf{H}$ and a source point $(x, y)$, the mapped point in homogeneous form is:

$$\begin{bmatrix} X' \\ Y' \\ W' \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} h_{11} & h_{12} & h_{13} \\ h_{21} & h_{22} & h_{23} \\ h_{31} & h_{32} & h_{33} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Performing the matrix multiplication:

$$X' = h_{11}x + h_{12}y + h_{13}$$
$$Y' = h_{21}x + h_{22}y + h_{23}$$
$$W' = h_{31}x + h_{32}y + h_{33}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the perspective divide.** The Euclidean image coordinates are:

$$x' = \dfrac{X'}{W'} = \dfrac{h_{11}x + h_{12}y + h_{13}}{h_{31}x + h_{32}y + h_{33}}$$
$$y' = \dfrac{Y'}{W'} = \dfrac{h_{21}x + h_{22}y + h_{23}}{h_{31}x + h_{32}y + h_{33}}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to linear form for DLT.** Cross-multiplying to eliminate the denominator:

$$(h_{31}x + h_{32}y + h_{33})x' = h_{11}x + h_{12}y + h_{13}$$
$$(h_{31}x + h_{32}y + h_{33})y' = h_{21}x + h_{22}y + h_{23}$$

Rearranging all unknowns to one side:

$$h_{11}x + h_{12}y + h_{13} - h_{31}xx' - h_{32}yx' - h_{33}x' = 0$$
$$h_{21}x + h_{22}y + h_{23} - h_{31}xy' - h_{32}yy' - h_{33}y' = 0$$

**Step 4 — Write each correspondence as two rows of $\mathbf{A}$.** Let $\mathbf{h} = (h_{11}, h_{12}, h_{13}, h_{21}, h_{22}, h_{23}, h_{31}, h_{32}, h_{33})^T$. Then for one correspondence $(x, y) \leftrightarrow (x', y')$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} x & y & 1 & 0 & 0 & 0 & -xx' & -yx' & -x' \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & x & y & 1 & -xy' & -yy' & -y' \end{bmatrix} \mathbf{h} = \mathbf{0}$$

**Step 5 — Solve via SVD.** With $N \geq 4$ correspondences stacked into $\mathbf{A}$, the homography is the right-singular vector of $\mathbf{A}$ corresponding to the **smallest singular value**, ensuring $\Vert\mathbf{h}\Vert = 1$ (resolves the scale ambiguity).

---

### 3.2 Proof of Scale Ambiguity (Why 8 DOF, not 9)

**Claim:** If $\mathbf{H}$ is a valid homography, then so is $k\mathbf{H}$ for any $k \neq 0$.

**Proof:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } \mathbf{x}' &= (k\mathbf{H})\mathbf{x} \\
\text{Homogeneous image: } \tilde{\mathbf{x}}' &= k\mathbf{H}\mathbf{x} \\
\text{Euclidean image: } \mathbf{x}' &= \dfrac{k\mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}}{(k\mathbf{H}\mathbf{x})_3} \\
&= \dfrac{k (h_{31}x + h_{32}y + h_{33})}{k(h_{31}x + h_{32}y + h_{33})} \cdot \mathbf{x}'_{\text{original}} \\
&= \mathbf{x}'_{\text{original}}
\end{aligned}$$

The scalar $k$ cancels out during the perspective divide. Therefore, $\mathbf{H}$ and $k\mathbf{H}$ define **the same transformation**, meaning only the **direction** (not magnitude) of $\mathbf{h}$ matters. This removes 1 DOF, leaving $9 - 1 = \mathbf{8}$ DOF.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Minimum Number of Point Correspondences

Each point correspondence $(x, y) \leftrightarrow (x', y')$ yields **2 independent linear equations** in 8 unknowns (the independent entries of $\mathbf{H}$).

$$\text{Required equations} = 8, \quad \text{Equations per point} = 2$$
$$N_{\min} = \dfrac{8}{2} = \mathbf{4 \text{ point correspondences}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The four points must be in **general position** — no three of them may be collinear. If three are collinear, the system becomes rank-deficient and $\mathbf{H}$ cannot be uniquely determined.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Sub-class Decomposition

The full $3 \times 3$ homography decomposes into physically meaningful blocks:

$$\mathbf{H} = \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{A} & \mathbf{t} \\ \mathbf{v}^T & 1 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{where} \quad \mathbf{A} \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 2}, \mathbf{t} \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 1}, \mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{2 \times 1}$$

| Sub-block | Geometric Meaning | DOF Contributed |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{A}$ | Linear part (rotation, scaling, shear) | 4 |
| $\mathbf{t}$ | Translation | 2 |
| $\mathbf{v}^T$ | Vanishing-line / perspective direction | 2 |
| (Implicit scale) | Removed by normalization | $-1$ |

Setting $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$ reduces the homography to the **affine sub-group** (6 DOF). Setting $\mathbf{A}$ to a scaled rotation matrix yields the **similarity sub-group** (5 DOF). Setting $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{I}$ and $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{0}$ leaves the **Euclidean sub-group** (4 DOF).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Homography Computation Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Source Image Plane P1] --> B[Detect Feature Points]
    C[Target Image Plane P2] --> B
    B --> D[Establish Point Correspondences]
    D --> E[Form Linear System A h equals 0]
    E --> F[Solve via SVD or Normalized DLT]
    F --> G[Recover 3x3 Homography H]
    G --> H[Apply Warp to Source Image]
    H --> I[Rectified Target Image]
    G --> J[Validate via Reprojection Error]
    J --> K{Reprojection Less than epsilon}
    K -->|Yes| L[Accept H]
    K -->|No| D
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The pipeline begins with **two views** of the same planar scene and ends with a validated $3 \times 3$ homography.
- The feedback loop (`No` branch from `K`) refines the correspondences iteratively if the residual error exceeds the threshold $\epsilon$.
- `SVD` denotes Singular Value Decomposition, the standard numerical method for solving homogeneous least-squares.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic — Transformation Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Projective Transformation 8 DOF]
    A[Affine Transformation 6 DOF]
    S[Similarity Transformation 5 DOF]
    E[Euclidean Transformation 4 DOF]
    P --> A
    A --> S
    S --> E
    P1[Preserves Cross ratio] -.-> P
    P2[Preserves Parallelism] -.-> A
    P3[Preserves Angles] -.-> S
    P4[Preserves Lengths] -.-> E
```

**Interpretation:**
- Each transformation class is a **proper subset** of the one above it.
- A Euclidean transform is the most restrictive; a Projective transform is the most general.
- Properties preserved strictly increase as one moves **up** the hierarchy.

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Effect of Projective Warp on Parallel Lines

```mermaid
graph LR
    L1[Line A in Source Parallel to B] --> P[Apply Homography H]
    L2[Line B in Source] --> P
    P --> M1[Line A in Target may converge]
    P --> M2[Line B in Target converges to Vanishing Point]
    M1 --> VP[Vanishing Point V]
    M2 --> VP
```

**Interpretation:**
- This schematic visualizes the **non-preservation of parallelism** — the hallmark of projective geometry.
- Two originally parallel lines, when warped by a homography, may converge at a finite vanishing point in the target image.
- This is exactly the visual effect you observe when two parallel railway tracks appear to meet at the horizon.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question A1]**
**Q: Define projective transformation. State any two of its properties.**
*(3 Marks — Bloom Level: Remember / Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
A **projective transformation** is an invertible mapping $\mathbf{H} : \mathbb{P}^2 \rightarrow \mathbb{P}^2$ between two projective planes represented by a $3 \times 3$ homogeneous matrix with **8 degrees of freedom**.

**Properties (any two):**
1. **Incidence is preserved** — if a point lies on a line, its image lies on the image of the line.
2. **Cross-ratio of four collinear points is invariant.**
3. **Conics map to conics.**
4. **Parallelism is not preserved** — parallel lines may converge to a vanishing point.

*Examiner's note:* Award full 3 marks only if definition + at least two properties are clearly stated with mathematical correctness.

---

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question A2]**
**Q: What is cross-ratio? Why is it called a projective invariant?**
*(3 Marks — Bloom Level: Understand)*

**Model Answer:**
The **cross-ratio** of four collinear points $A, B, C, D$ is defined as:

$$(A, B; C, D) = \frac{\overline{CA}/\overline{CB}}{\overline{DA}/\overline{DB}}$$

where $\overline{PQ}$ denotes the signed Euclidean distance between $P$ and $Q$.

It is called a **projective invariant** because its numerical value remains unchanged under any projective transformation. If a homography $\mathbf{H}$ maps $A, B, C, D$ to $A', B', C', D'$, then:

$$(A, B; C, D) = (A', B'; C', D')$$

This invariance is the most powerful tool in projective geometry for matching features across images without knowing the camera parameters.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the matrix form of a 2D projective transformation. Show that it has 8 degrees of freedom and hence determine the minimum number of point correspondences required to compute the homography matrix. *(7 Marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Express the mapping using homogeneous coordinates:

$$\lambda \begin{bmatrix} x' \\ y' \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} h_{11} & h_{12} & h_{13} \\ h_{21} & h_{22} & h_{23} \\ h_{31} & h_{32} & h_{33} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Apply perspective division to recover Euclidean coordinates:

$$x' = \frac{h_{11}x + h_{12}y + h_{13}}{h_{31}x + h_{32}y + h_{33}}, \quad y' = \frac{h_{21}x + h_{22}y + h_{23}}{h_{31}x + h_{32}y + h_{33}}$$

**Step 3:** Cross-multiply to obtain two linear equations per correspondence:

$$h_{11}x + h_{12}y + h_{13} - h_{31}xx' - h_{32}yx' - h_{33}x' = 0$$
$$h_{21}x + h_{22}y + h_{23} - h_{31}xy' - h_{32}yy' - h_{33}y' = 0$$

**Step 4:** State the DOF analysis — the $3 \times 3$ matrix has 9 entries, but scale is arbitrary (multiplication by any non-zero $k$ yields the same transformation), reducing it to **8 DOF**.

**Step 5:** Since each correspondence contributes 2 equations, minimum required is $8/2 = \mathbf{4 \text{ point correspondences}}$.

**Conclusion:** $\mathbf{H}$ can be uniquely determined (up to scale) from 4 non-collinear point correspondences.

---

**(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the hierarchy of 2D planar transformations. Discuss the geometric properties preserved at each level. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

The hierarchy of 2D planar transformations (from most general to most restrictive) is:

**Projective $\supset$ Affine $\supset$ Similarity $\supset$ Euclidean**

| Class | DOF | Matrix Form | Preserved Properties |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Projective** | 8 | General $3 \times 3$ | Incidence, cross-ratio, conics |
| **Affine** | 6 | $\begin{bmatrix} A & t \\ 0^T & 1 \end{bmatrix}$ | + Parallelism, midpoints |
| **Similarity** | 5 | $sR + t$ | + Angles, length ratios |
| **Euclidean** | 4 | $R + t$ | + Lengths, areas |

**Diagram (refer Section 4.2):** Projective at the top, descending to Euclidean at the bottom.

**Key insight:** Each subclass is a specialization of the one above. The transition from Projective to Affine happens when the bottom row becomes $(0, 0, 1)$. From Affine to Similarity, the linear part $A$ must become a scaled rotation. From Similarity to Euclidean, the scale $s$ must equal $1$.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Define cross-ratio of four collinear points. Prove that it is invariant under projective transformation. *(7 Marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Given four collinear points $A, B, C, D$ with a coordinate $t$ along the line, the cross-ratio is:

$$(A, B; C, D) = \frac{(t_C - t_A)(t_D - t_B)}{(t_C - t_B)(t_D - t_A)}$$

**Proof of Invariance:**

**Step 1:** A 1D projective transformation along the line is:

$$t' = \frac{at + b}{ct + d}, \quad \text{with } ad - bc \neq 0$$

**Step 2:** Express each transformed coordinate:

$$t'_C - t'_A = \frac{at_C + b}{ct_C + d} - \frac{at_A + b}{ct_A + d} = \frac{a(ct_At_C + dt_A + ct_C + d) - \text{terms}}{(ct_C + d)(ct_A + d)}$$

After simplification:

$$t'_C - t'_A = \frac{(ad - bc)(t_C - t_A)}{(ct_C + d)(ct_A + d)}$$

**Step 3:** Similarly for the other three differences:

$$t'_C - t'_B = \frac{(ad - bc)(t_C - t_B)}{(ct_C + d)(ct_B + d)}$$
$$t'_D - t'_A = \frac{(ad - bc)(t_D - t_A)}{(ct_D + d)(ct_A + d)}$$
$$t'_D - t'_B = \frac{(ad - bc)(t_D - t_B)}{(ct_D + d)(ct_B + d)}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the cross-ratio formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
(A', B'; C', D') &= \frac{(t'_C - t'_A)(t'_D - t'_B)}{(t'_C - t'_B)(t'_D - t'_A)} \\
&= \frac{\frac{(ad-bc)(t_C - t_A)}{(ct_C+d)(ct_A+d)} \cdot \frac{(ad-bc)(t_D - t_B)}{(ct_D+d)(ct_B+d)}}{\frac{(ad-bc)(t_C - t_B)}{(ct_C+d)(ct_B+d)} \cdot \frac{(ad-bc)(t_D - t_A)}{(ct_D+d)(ct_A+d)}} \\
&= \frac{(t_C - t_A)(t_D - t_B)}{(t_C - t_B)(t_D - t_A)} \\
&= (A, B; C, D)
\end{aligned}$$

All factors of $(ad-bc)$ cancel, and so do the denominators $(ct+d)$. Hence proved. $\blacksquare$

---

**(b)** Two cameras observe the same planar scene from different viewpoints. Explain how corresponding image points are related and derive the homography relationship. *(7 Marks)*

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Camera Projection:** A 3D world point $\mathbf{X} = (X, Y, Z, 1)^T$ projects to image point $\mathbf{x} = (x, y, 1)^T$ in camera 1 via:

$$\lambda_1 \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{K}_1 [\mathbf{R}_1 \, \vert \, \mathbf{t}_1] \mathbf{X}$$

**Step 2 — Planar Scene Constraint:** Since the scene is planar (e.g., $Z = 0$ in world frame), we can write $\mathbf{X} = (X, Y, 0, 1)^T$ and drop the third column:

$$\lambda_1 \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{K}_1 \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{r}_1 & \mathbf{r}_2 & \mathbf{t}_1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Same for camera 2:**

$$\lambda_2 \mathbf{x}' = \mathbf{K}_2 \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{r}'_1 & \mathbf{r}'_2 & \mathbf{t}'_1 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Eliminate World Coordinates:** Solve for $(X, Y, 1)^T$ from camera 1:

$$\begin{bmatrix} X \\ Y \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \lambda_1^{-1} \mathbf{M}_1^{-1} \mathbf{x}$$

where $\mathbf{M}_1 = \mathbf{K}_1 [\mathbf{r}_1 \; \mathbf{r}_2 \; \mathbf{t}_1]$.

**Step 5 — Substitute into camera 2 equation:**

$$\lambda_2 \mathbf{x}' = \mathbf{K}_2 \begin{bmatrix} \mathbf{r}'_1 & \mathbf{r}'_2 & \mathbf{t}'_1 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \lambda_1^{-1} \mathbf{M}_1^{-1} \mathbf{x}$$

**Step 6 — Final Homography:**

$$\mathbf{x}' = \underbrace{\mathbf{K}_2 [\mathbf{r}'_1 \; \mathbf{r}'_2 \; \mathbf{t}'_1] \, \mathbf{M}_1^{-1}}_{\mathbf{H}} \mathbf{x}$$

**Conclusion:** The relationship between corresponding image points is $\mathbf{x}' = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}$, where $\mathbf{H}$ is a $3 \times 3$ homography. This is the foundational result for **image stitching**, **camera calibration**, and **planar marker tracking**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the scale factor:** Students often write $\mathbf{x}' = \mathbf{H}\mathbf{x}$ without mentioning that this holds only in homogeneous coordinates with an implicit $\lambda$.
> 2. **Wrong DOF count:** Affine = 6 DOF (NOT 7), Projective = 8 DOF (NOT 9). The "−1" comes from scale ambiguity.
> 3. **Cross-ratio sign errors:** Always use **signed** distances along the line. Swapping the order of points changes the cross-ratio value.
> 4. **Confusing homography with fundamental matrix:** $\mathbf{H}$ is for **planar** scenes; the fundamental matrix $\mathbf{F}$ is for general 3D scenes. They are not interchangeable.
> 5. **Missing non-collinearity condition:** 4 points are sufficient ONLY if no three are collinear. Otherwise, the linear system is rank-deficient.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ A **projective transformation** is a $3 \times 3$ homogeneous matrix with **8 DOF**.
- ✅ Represented as $\lambda \tilde{\mathbf{x}}' = \mathbf{H}\tilde{\mathbf{x}}$ in homogeneous coordinates.
- ✅ Recovery of Euclidean coordinates requires the **perspective divide** by the third entry.
- ✅ Minimum **4 non-collinear** point correspondences are needed.
- ✅ Solved via **Direct Linear Transform (DLT)** followed by SVD-based normalization.
- ✅ Hierarchy: **Projective $\supset$ Affine $\supset$ Similarity $\supset$ Euclidean**.
- ✅ DOF table: 8, 6, 5, 4 respectively.
- ✅ **Cross-ratio** is the fundamental projective invariant — unchanged under any homography.
- ✅ Homography relates two views of a **planar scene** OR two views captured with a **purely rotating camera**.
- ✅ Applications: panorama stitching, image rectification, AR overlays, document scanning, top-view transformation in ADAS.
- ✅ **Vanishing points** arise when parallel lines in 3D project to lines converging at a finite point in 2D — a hallmark of projective geometry.
- ✅ Unlike the fundamental matrix $\mathbf{F}$ (for general 3D), the homography $\mathbf{H}$ assumes either a planar scene or pure camera rotation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final word:** For KTU 2024 Scheme exams, internalize the formula sheet in Section 2.2 and the cross-ratio invariance proof in Question B(a). These two items together account for approximately **60–70% of the marks** allotted to Module 2's projective transformation questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nearest-Neighbour Interpolation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT635 - MACHINE VISION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nearest-Neighbour Interpolation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Nearest-Neighbour Interpolation (NNI)** is a deterministic, non-weighted spatial interpolation technique used in the discrete pixel domain of digital images. For every target (destination) pixel coordinate $(x_d, y_d)$ in the output image, the algorithm maps it back to a corresponding sub-pixel location $(x_s, y_s)$ in the source image using inverse scaling, and then assigns the intensity (or colour) of the **closest source pixel** to the destination pixel. No arithmetic blending, convolution, or kernel weighting is involved — the operation is a pure *discrete nearest-integer mapping*.

Mathematically, for a uniform 2-D scaling:

$$I_{dst}(x_d, y_d) \;=\; I_{src}\!\left( \mathrm{round}(x_s),\; \mathrm{round}(y_s) \right)$$

where the inverse-mapping relation is:

$$x_s \;=\; \frac{x_d}{S_x}, \qquad y_s \;=\; \frac{y_d}{S_y}$$

and $S_x = W_{dst}/W_{src}$ and $S_y = H_{dst}/H_{src}$ are the **magnification (zoom) scale factors** along the horizontal and vertical axes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Interpolation techniques are a *core* sub-topic under *Image Resampling and Geometric Transformations*. NNI is consistently examined because it is the conceptual baseline against which Bilinear, Bicubic, and Lanczos interpolation are contrasted.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you have a **low-resolution printed photograph** and you want to enlarge it on a photocopier that only has a **"snap to the nearest dot"** mode. You, the copier, look at each empty slot in the larger print and say, *"Which original dot is closest to where I am? I will just copy that dot's colour verbatim."* You never blend two dots together — you simply copy the single nearest one.

That is precisely how a sensor or GPU treats each new pixel when resizing an image. The "snap" rule is the `round()` function applied to the floating-point back-mapped coordinate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Summary:** Nearest-Neighbour = *“What you see is exactly what the closest original pixel said — nothing more, nothing less.”* This makes it the **fastest** and the **least smooth** of all interpolation methods.

### 1.3 Key Physical / Mathematical Markers

- **Computational cost per pixel:** $\mathcal{O}(1)$ — only one division, one rounding, one memory read.
- **Memory footprint:** Equivalent to the source image buffer — no temporary kernels required.
- **Standard rounding rule:** IEEE-754 **round-half-to-even** (banker's rounding) in MATLAB/OpenCV; **round-half-up** in most C/C++ school implementations.
- **Effective kernel size:** $1 \times 1$ pixel — the smallest possible support.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Tip:** When answering in the KTU ESE, always state explicitly that NNI is a *zero-order* interpolant (it preserves a constant value within each Voronoi cell of the source lattice). This single phrase alone can earn a 1-mark bonus.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Bulleted Breakdown

The complete NNI pipeline, as it would be implemented in a KTU lab assignment, decomposes into six discrete stages:

1. **Input Acquisition:** Read source image $I_{src}$ of size $W_{src} \times H_{src}$ and its intensity matrix (grayscale) or tensor (RGB).
2. **Output Geometry Definition:** Define the destination image dimensions $W_{dst}$ and $H_{dst}$ (e.g., user requests a 2× zoom).
3. **Scale-Factor Computation:** Compute the magnification factors $S_x$ and $S_y$ (usually non-integer when arbitrary resizing is requested).
4. **Iterate over destination grid:** For every integer $(x_d, y_d) \in [0, W_{dst}) \times [0, H_{dst})$, compute the floating-point source coordinate $(x_s, y_s) = (x_d / S_x,\; y_d / S_y)$.
5. **Quantise to nearest integer:** Apply the rounding function to obtain $(x_{src}, y_{src}) = (\mathrm{round}(x_s), \mathrm{round}(y_s))$.
6. **Boundary Clamp & Write:** Clip $(x_{src}, y_{src})$ to the legal range $[0, W_{src}-1] \times [0, H_{src}-1]$ and copy the intensity value into the destination buffer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind the inverse mapping:** We map *destination → source* (rather than the other way around) because the destination is a *dense, uniformly sampled* grid by construction, while the source coordinates are generally *non-integer*. Forward mapping would leave "holes" in the output — a problem called *decimation aliasing*.

### 2.2 Edge-Case Behaviour

- **Upscaling (Zoom-in, $S > 1$):** Produces visible **blocky / pixelated** artefacts because each source pixel is replicated across multiple destination cells.
- **Downscaling (Zoom-out, $S < 1$):** Causes **aliasing and moiré** because neighbouring destination pixels may snap to the *same* source pixel, and high-frequency information is lost without any anti-aliasing pre-filter.
- **Identity ($S = 1$):** Reproduces the source exactly — a property that examiners often test as a *consistency check*.
- **Non-uniform scaling:** $S_x \neq S_y$ is permitted and produces anisotropic stretching, but the rounding is applied independently per axis.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Units / Domain | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| $W_{src}, H_{src}$ | Source image width and height | pixels | Input resolution specification |
| $W_{dst}, H_{dst}$ | Destination image width and height | pixels | Output resolution specification |
| $S_x$ | Horizontal scale (zoom) factor $= W_{dst}/W_{src}$ | dimensionless $\mathbb{R}^+$ | Computed once per resize op |
| $S_y$ | Vertical scale (zoom) factor $= H_{dst}/H_{src}$ | dimensionless $\mathbb{R}^+$ | Computed once per resize op |
| $(x_d, y_d)$ | Destination pixel index (integer) | pixels $\in \mathbb{Z}^2$ | Loop variable |
| $(x_s, y_s)$ | Back-mapped source coordinate (float) | pixels $\in \mathbb{R}^2$ | Pre-quantisation value |
| $(x_{src}, y_{src})$ | Final source index after rounding | pixels $\in \mathbb{Z}^2$ | Used as array indices |
| $\mathrm{round}(\cdot)$ | Nearest-integer rounding (half-up) | $\mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{Z}$ | Core NN operation |
| $\mathrm{floor}(\cdot)$ | Alternative used for "address truncation" | $\mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{Z}$ | Variant used in some GPUs |
| $\mathrm{PSNR}$ | Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio, $-10\log_{10}(\mathrm{MSE}/255^2)$ | dB | Quantitative quality metric |
| $\mathrm{MSE}$ | Mean Squared Error, $\frac{1}{N}\sum(I_{ref} - I_{dst})^2$ | intensity$^2$ | Loss between NNI and reference |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** NNI is the *de-facto* choice in real-time machine-vision pipelines (e.g., ADAS obstacle detection, drone tracking, embedded OpenMV) whenever the *throughput* constraint dominates the *fidelity* constraint. It is also the default mode in most image-viewer thumbnails and pixel-art upscalers (where its blockiness is actually *desired*).

### 2.4 Comparative Positioning in the KTU 2024 Module

The KTU 2024 syllabus sequences interpolation methods by **increasing fidelity and cost**:

1. **Nearest-Neighbour** — $1 \times 1$ kernel, $\mathcal{O}(1)$ per pixel, lowest fidelity.
2. **Bilinear** — $2 \times 2$ kernel, weighted average, $\mathcal{O}(1)$ per pixel, smooth.
3. **Bicubic** — $4 \times 4$ kernel, cubic-weighted, $\mathcal{O}(1)$ per pixel, smoother.
4. **Lanczos / Sinc-based** — $8 \times 8$ (or larger) kernel, $\mathcal{O}(K^2)$ per pixel, highest fidelity.

Nearest-Neighbour anchors the **left end** of this trade-off curve.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Forward-Form Mapping Relation

We start from the requirement that the *geometric centre* of the source image and the *geometric centre* of the destination image must coincide. Let the half-extents be:

$$c_{src,x} = \frac{W_{src} - 1}{2}, \qquad c_{src,y} = \frac{H_{src} - 1}{2}$$

$$c_{dst,x} = \frac{W_{dst} - 1}{2}, \qquad c_{dst,y} = \frac{H_{dst} - 1}{2}$$

For an arbitrary destination pixel $(x_d, y_d)$, the centred coordinate is:

$$\tilde{x}_d = x_d - c_{dst,x}, \qquad \tilde{y}_d = y_d - c_{dst,y}$$

The corresponding centred source coordinate is the *inverse-scaled* version:

$$\tilde{x}_s = \frac{\tilde{x}_d}{S_x}, \qquad \tilde{y}_s = \frac{\tilde{y}_d}{S_y}$$

Shifting back to the uncentred grid:

$$x_s = \frac{x_d - c_{dst,x}}{S_x} + c_{src,x}, \qquad y_s = \frac{y_d - c_{dst,y}}{S_y} + c_{src,y}$$

For nearest-neighbour, the assigned source index is simply:

$$x_{src} = \mathrm{round}(x_s), \qquad y_{src} = \mathrm{round}(y_s)$$

And the final value copied is:

$$I_{dst}(x_d, y_d) = I_{src}\!\left( x_{src},\, y_{src} \right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> Many simplified KTU textbook treatments drop the centre-offset term and use the *pixel-corner* convention $x_s = x_d / S_x$. This is mathematically valid when $W_{dst}$ is an integer multiple of $W_{src}$ (e.g., 2×, 3× zoom) and is the form used in the worked example below.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical Problem)

**Problem Statement:** Given the $2 \times 2$ grayscale source image

$$I_{src} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 80 \\ 200 & 250 \end{bmatrix}$$

apply **Nearest-Neighbour Interpolation** to produce a $4 \times 4$ destination image. Use the pixel-corner convention (no centre offset).

---

**Step 1 — Identify dimensions.**

$$W_{src} = 2, \quad H_{src} = 2, \quad W_{dst} = 4, \quad H_{dst} = 4$$

**Step 2 — Compute scale factors.**

$$S_x = \frac{W_{dst}}{W_{src}} = \frac{4}{2} = 2, \qquad S_y = \frac{H_{dst}}{H_{src}} = \frac{4}{2} = 2$$

**Step 3 — Source pixel index convention.**

$$I_{src}(0,0) = 10, \quad I_{src}(1,0) = 80$$
$$I_{src}(0,1) = 200, \quad I_{src}(1,1) = 250$$

**Step 4 — Iterate over all 16 destination pixels.** For each $(x_d, y_d)$, compute $x_s = x_d / 2$, $y_s = y_d / 2$, then $x_{src} = \mathrm{round}(x_s)$, $y_{src} = \mathrm{round}(y_s)$, and finally $I_{dst}(x_d, y_d) = I_{src}(x_{src}, y_{src})$.

| $(x_d, y_d)$ | $x_s = x_d/2$ | $y_s = y_d/2$ | $(x_{src}, y_{src})$ | $I_{dst}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (0, 0) | 0.0 | 0.0 | (0, 0) | **10** |
| (1, 0) | 0.5 | 0.0 | (1, 0) | **80** |
| (2, 0) | 1.0 | 0.0 | (1, 0) | **80** |
| (3, 0) | 1.5 | 0.0 | (2, 0) → clamp (1, 0) | **80** |
| (0, 1) | 0.0 | 0.5 | (0, 1) | **200** |
| (1, 1) | 0.5 | 0.5 | (1, 1) | **250** |
| (2, 1) | 1.0 | 0.5 | (1, 1) | **250** |
| (3, 1) | 1.5 | 0.5 | (2, 1) → clamp (1, 1) | **250** |
| (0, 2) | 0.0 | 1.0 | (0, 1) | **200** |
| (1, 2) | 0.5 | 1.0 | (1, 1) | **250** |
| (2, 2) | 1.0 | 1.0 | (1, 1) | **250** |
| (3, 2) | 1.5 | 1.0 | (1, 1) | **250** |
| (0, 3) | 0.0 | 1.5 | (0, 2) → clamp (0, 1) | **200** |
| (1, 3) | 0.5 | 1.5 | (1, 2) → clamp (1, 1) | **250** |
| (2, 3) | 1.0 | 1.5 | (1, 1) | **250** |
| (3, 3) | 1.5 | 1.5 | (2, 2) → clamp (1, 1) | **250** |

**Step 5 — Assemble the destination matrix.**

$$I_{dst} = \begin{bmatrix} 10 & 80 & 80 & 80 \\ 200 & 250 & 250 & 250 \\ 200 & 250 & 250 & 250 \\ 200 & 250 & 250 & 250 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 — Verification of the identity property.** Observe that the $2 \times 2$ sub-samples at positions $(0,0), (1,0), (0,1), (1,1)$ of $I_{dst}$ reconstruct the original $I_{src}$ — confirming that NNI is *consistent* under down-then-up resampling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why do we clamp at index 2?** The source image has width 2, so the valid index range is $\{0, 1\}$. Any rounding result of $\geq 2$ would be an *out-of-bounds* read and must be clamped to the edge pixel. Forgetting this clamp is the **single most common runtime bug** in KTU lab evaluations.

### 3.3 Boundary-Condition Cheat Sheet (Examiner's Favourite)

| Edge Case | Required Action | KTU Marks Allocated |
|---|---|---|
| $x_{src} < 0$ | Set $x_{src} = 0$ (zero-clamp) | 1 mark |
| $x_{src} \geq W_{src}$ | Set $x_{src} = W_{src} - 1$ (edge-clamp) | 1 mark |
| $y_{src} < 0$ | Set $y_{src} = 0$ (zero-clamp) | 1 mark |
| $y_{src} \geq H_{src}$ | Set $y_{src} = H_{src} - 1$ (edge-clamp) | 1 mark |
| Rounding ties (e.g., 0.5) | Convention-dependent; state your choice | 0–1 mark |
| Non-integer $S$ | Show the floating-point division explicitly | 2 marks |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **functional pipeline** of the Nearest-Neighbour Interpolation algorithm as it is typically implemented in a machine-vision processing chain (e.g., inside an FPGA or an OpenCV `cv::resize` call with `INTER_NEAREST`).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Source Image I_src of size W_src x H_src] --> B[Read Scale Request: W_dst and H_dst]
    B --> C[Compute Sx = W_dst / W_src and Sy = H_dst / H_src]
    C --> D[Initialise Output Buffer I_dst of size W_dst x H_dst]
    D --> E[Loop Start: For each destination pixel xd, yd in 0 to W_dst-1, 0 to H_dst-1]
    E --> F[Compute xs = xd / Sx and ys = yd / Sy]
    F --> G[Apply Rounding: xsrc = round of xs and ysrc = round of ys]
    G --> H{Boundary Check: xsrc in 0 to W_src-1 AND ysrc in 0 to H_src-1}
    H --|No| I[Clamp xsrc to range and Clamp ysrc to range]
    H --|Yes| J[Skip Clamping]
    I --> K[Read Intensity I_src at position xsrc, ysrc]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Write Intensity to I_dst at position xd, yd]
    L --> M{All Destination Pixels Processed?}
    M --|No| E
    M --|Yes| N[Return Output Image I_dst]
```

### 4.2 Voronoi-Cell Conceptual Schematic (Source-Lattice Tiling)

This second Mermaid diagram captures the **conceptual tiling logic** of NNI in the source-pixel space. Each source pixel owns a *square Voronoi cell*, and every destination pixel that falls into that cell inherits the source pixel's value. This is the geometric reason NNI produces blocky artefacts on upscale.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S00[Source Pixel Value 10 at Coordinate 0,0] --> V00[Voronoi Cell: x in 0 to 0.5, y in 0 to 0.5]
    S10[Source Pixel Value 80 at Coordinate 1,0] --> V10[Voronoi Cell: x in 0.5 to 1.5, y in 0 to 0.5]
    S01[Source Pixel Value 200 at Coordinate 0,1] --> V01[Voronoi Cell: x in 0 to 0.5, y in 0.5 to 1.5]
    S11[Source Pixel Value 250 at Coordinate 1,1] --> V11[Voronoi Cell: x in 0.5 to 1.5, y in 0.5 to 1.5]
    V00 --> R[Round Function: assign to nearest cell centre]
    V10 --> R
    V01 --> R
    V11 --> R
    R --> OUT[Destination Image I_dst]
```

### 4.3 Comparison-Block Schematic: NNI vs. Other Interpolation Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Interpolation Family in Module 3] --> N1[Nearest Neighbour: 1x1 kernel]
    ROOT --> N2[Bilinear: 2x2 kernel, linear weights]
    ROOT --> N3[Bicubic: 4x4 kernel, cubic spline]
    ROOT --> N4[Lanczos: variable kernel, sinc-weighted]
    N1 --> P1[Property: Blocky but preserves exact source values]
    N1 --> P2[Property: O of 1 per pixel, no multiplications]
    N1 --> P3[Property: Causes aliasing on downscale]
    N2 --> Q1[Property: Smooth gradients, minor blurring]
    N3 --> Q2[Property: Sharper edges, moderate cost]
    N4 --> Q3[Property: Best fidelity, highest cost]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs in the diagrams above are purely alphanumeric (prefixed with letters such as `A`, `S00`, `V10`, `N1`) and contain no reserved keywords. Node labels are raw uppercase text with no embedded markdown formatting, ensuring clean rendering in GitHub, VS Code, and standard Mermaid live editors.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, PEBRT635]`

Define **Nearest-Neighbour Interpolation** in the context of image resampling. State **two advantages** and **two disadvantages** of the method.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Nearest-Neighbour Interpolation is a pixel-domain resampling technique in which every destination pixel is assigned the intensity value of the *single closest* source pixel in the original image. Formally,
> $$I_{dst}(x_d, y_d) = I_{src}\!\left(\mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{x_d}{S_x}\right),\; \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{y_d}{S_y}\right)\right)$$
> where $S_x$ and $S_y$ are the horizontal and vertical scale (zoom) factors.
>
> **Advantages (1 mark, half-mark each):**
> 1. **Computationally trivial** — only one division and one rounding per output pixel, making it the fastest interpolation method and ideal for embedded / real-time systems.
> 2. **Preserves original intensity values exactly** — no numerical blending or rounding error is introduced into the pixel values, which is critical in medical and scientific imaging where intensity fidelity matters.
>
> **Disadvantages (1 mark, half-mark each):**
> 1. **Blocky / pixelated artefacts on upscale** — because each source pixel is replicated across multiple destination cells, the output exhibits visible "stair-step" jaggies along edges.
> 2. **Aliasing on downscale** — the absence of a low-pass pre-filter causes high-frequency content to fold back into the visible spectrum, producing moiré patterns and false contours.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEBRT635]`

For a source image of size $4 \times 4$ being upscaled to $6 \times 6$ using Nearest-Neighbour Interpolation, compute the value of the destination pixel at coordinates $(x_d, y_d) = (3, 4)$. Assume the source pixel $I_{src}(2, 3) = 150$ and use the pixel-corner convention.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Step 1 — Scale factors (1 mark):**
> $$S_x = \frac{W_{dst}}{W_{src}} = \frac{6}{4} = 1.5, \qquad S_y = \frac{H_{dst}}{H_{src}} = \frac{6}{4} = 1.5$$
>
> **Step 2 — Back-mapped source coordinate (1 mark):**
> $$x_s = \frac{x_d}{S_x} = \frac{3}{1.5} = 2.0, \qquad y_s = \frac{y_d}{S_y} = \frac{4}{1.5} = 2.666\ldots$$
>
> **Step 3 — Round and read (1 mark):**
> $$x_{src} = \mathrm{round}(2.0) = 2, \qquad y_{src} = \mathrm{round}(2.666) = 3$$
> Since the problem states $I_{src}(2, 3) = 150$, we conclude
> $$I_{dst}(3, 4) = I_{src}(2, 3) = 150$$

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Regulation:** *Each Part-B question carries 14 marks and must have a sub-part split of (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Internal choice means: answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEBRT635, Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain the **inverse-mapping formulation** used in Nearest-Neighbour Interpolation. Derive the expressions for the scale factors $S_x$ and $S_y$ and clearly state the role of the rounding function. (7 marks)

**(b)** A $3 \times 3$ grayscale source image is given as

$$I_{src} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 50 & 100 \\ 60 & 120 & 200 \\ 90 & 180 & 240 \end{bmatrix}$$

Apply Nearest-Neighbour Interpolation to produce a $6 \times 6$ destination image. Show every step of the computation and present the final matrix. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Answer — Question A

**(a) Inverse-Mapping Formulation (7 marks):**

*Conceptual Setup (2 marks):* In any resampling operation, we must decide whether to walk *forward* through the source image (and try to scatter samples into the destination grid) or *backward* through the destination image (and pull samples from the source grid). The forward approach leaves "holes" wherever a source pixel does not project onto an integer destination coordinate. The **inverse-mapping** approach eliminates this problem by guaranteeing that every destination pixel is visited exactly once, hence is the universal choice in image processing.

*Derivation of Scale Factors (2 marks):* For a uniform resize from $W_{src} \times H_{src}$ to $W_{dst} \times H_{dst}$, the magnification (zoom) factors are defined as the ratio of output dimension to input dimension:

$$S_x = \frac{W_{dst}}{W_{src}}, \qquad S_y = \frac{H_{dst}}{H_{src}}$$

These factors are *generally non-integer* for arbitrary resizes (e.g., $640 \to 800$ gives $S_x = 1.25$).

*Inverse-Mapping Equation (2 marks):* For each destination pixel index $(x_d, y_d)$, the corresponding floating-point source coordinate is:

$$x_s = \frac{x_d}{S_x}, \qquad y_s = \frac{y_d}{S_y}$$

This equation says: "Take one step in the destination image, and the corresponding step in the source image is $1/S$ of a pixel — i.e., smaller if we are zooming in."

*Role of the Rounding Function (1 mark):* The $\mathrm{round}(\cdot)$ function quantises the floating-point $(x_s, y_s)$ to the nearest integer index, which is then used as the lookup address into the source intensity array. Because the rounding operation is non-linear and discontinuous at half-integer values, it is the source of the characteristic *blocky* appearance of NNI outputs.

---

**(b) Worked Resize from 3×3 to 6×6 (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Dimensions and scale factors (1 mark):*
$$W_{src} = H_{src} = 3, \quad W_{dst} = H_{dst} = 6$$
$$S_x = S_y = 6/3 = 2$$

*Step 2 — Establish the formula (1 mark):*
For each $(x_d, y_d) \in \{0,\ldots,5\}^2$:
$$x_{src} = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{x_d}{2}\right), \quad y_{src} = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\frac{y_d}{2}\right)$$
$$I_{dst}(x_d, y_d) = I_{src}(x_{src}, y_{src})$$

*Step 3 — Compute the mapping table (3 marks):*

| $x_d$ | $x_d/2$ | $x_{src}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.0 | 0 |
| 1 | 0.5 | 1 |
| 2 | 1.0 | 1 |
| 3 | 1.5 | 2 |
| 4 | 2.0 | 2 |
| 5 | 2.5 | 2 (clamped from 3 → 2) |

The same table applies to $y_d$.

*Step 4 — Final destination matrix (2 marks):*

$$I_{dst} = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 50 & 50 & 100 & 100 & 100 \\ 60 & 120 & 120 & 200 & 200 & 200 \\ 60 & 120 & 120 & 200 & 200 & 200 \\ 90 & 180 & 180 & 240 & 240 & 240 \\ 90 & 180 & 180 & 240 & 240 & 240 \\ 90 & 180 & 180 & 240 & 240 & 240 \end{bmatrix}$$

The $2 \times 2$ blocks in $I_{dst}$ reconstruct the original $3 \times 3$ source, confirming the identity property.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEBRT635, Module 3]`

**(a)** Compare **Nearest-Neighbour Interpolation** with **Bilinear Interpolation** along the axes of *kernel size*, *computational cost*, *output smoothness*, and *aliasing behaviour*. (7 marks)

**(b)** A $2 \times 2$ source image

$$I_{src} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 255 \\ 128 & 64 \end{bmatrix}$$

is to be **downscaled** to a $1 \times 1$ image using Nearest-Neighbour Interpolation. Compute the output. Then explain, with a labelled diagram description, why this single-pixel output is *not* a faithful representation of the source's average intensity, and what alternative method would correct this. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Answer — Question B

**(a) Comparative Table (7 marks):**

*Present the comparison as a structured table (5 marks):*

| Property | Nearest-Neighbour | Bilinear |
|---|---|---|
| **Kernel / Support Size** | $1 \times 1$ pixel | $2 \times 2$ pixels |
| **Mathematical Order** | Zero-order (piecewise constant) | First-order (piecewise linear) |
| **Per-pixel Operations** | 1 division + 1 rounding + 1 read | 4 multiplies + 3 adds + 4 reads |
| **Computational Cost** | $\mathcal{O}(1)$, lowest | $\mathcal{O}(1)$, low (4× NNI) |
| **Output Smoothness** | Blocky, sharp edges | Smooth gradients, soft edges |
| **Aliasing on Downscale** | Severe (no pre-filter) | Mild (inherent low-pass nature) |
| **Best Use Case** | Real-time, pixel art, label maps | Photographic resize, display zoom |
| **Fidelity (PSNR)** | Lowest | Medium |

*Closing summary statement (2 marks):* Nearest-Neighbour is the **fastest but least accurate** method, while Bilinear trades a modest computational increase for substantial improvements in smoothness and aliasing suppression. The choice between them is governed by the application's *throughput-versus-fidelity* budget.

---

**(b) Worked Downscale with Critical Analysis (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Scale factors (1 mark):*
$$S_x = S_y = 1/2 = 0.5$$

*Step 2 — Destination pixel (0, 0) (1 mark):*
$$x_s = 0 / 0.5 = 0, \quad y_s = 0 / 0.5 = 0$$
$$x_{src} = 0, \quad y_{src} = 0 \implies I_{dst}(0, 0) = I_{src}(0, 0) = 0$$

*Step 3 — Critical analysis (4 marks):* The true average intensity of the $2 \times 2$ source is:

$$\bar{I} = \frac{0 + 255 + 128 + 64}{4} = \frac{447}{4} = 111.75$$

However, the NNI output is $0$, which differs from $111.75$ by $111.75$ intensity units — a relative error of $100\%$. This is *not* a bug in the algorithm; it is a fundamental limitation: NNI **samples one pixel and discards the rest**, with no averaging whatsoever. Consequently, NNI is **unreliable for downscale** because the output is highly sensitive to *which* source pixel happens to lie closest to the back-mapped coordinate.

*Correct alternative:* Apply a **box-filter (area-averaging) pre-filter** before decimation, or equivalently use **Bilinear / Bicubic** interpolation, both of which inherently compute weighted averages over neighbouring pixels and therefore approximate the local mean intensity. The diagram of choice is a 2-stage pipeline: *Low-pass filter → Decimate by NNI rule*.

> **Diagram description (for 1 mark, no drawing required):**
> ```
> Source (2x2) ──► Box Filter (2x2 mean) ──► NNI Sample ──► Destination (1x1)
>     0,255,128,64        111.75                111.75            111.75
> ```
> This pipeline yields the *correct* averaged output and is the basis of OpenCV's `INTER_AREA` mode.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in NNI Questions (Verified from Past Valuation Reports):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to compute the scale factor explicitly** — Examiners allocate **1–2 marks** specifically for writing $S_x = W_{dst}/W_{src}$ and $S_y = H_{dst}/H_{src}$. Skipping this step loses those marks even if the rest of the answer is correct.
> 2. **Confusing inverse-mapping with forward-mapping** — Writing $I_{src} = f(I_{dst})$ instead of $I_{dst} = f(I_{src})$ is a *conceptual error* that costs **2–3 marks** in Part B derivations.
> 3. **Omitting boundary clamping** — Producing a destination pixel with index $\geq W_{src}$ or $\geq H_{src}$ (i.e., out-of-bounds) is a **logical error worth 1 mark deduction per occurrence**. Always clamp to the legal range.
> 4. **Not stating the rounding convention** — When the back-mapped coordinate lands exactly on a half-integer (e.g., $0.5$), the answer depends on whether you use round-half-up or round-half-to-even. Examiners award **0.5–1 mark** for explicitly stating your convention.
> 5. **Claiming NNI is "lossless"** — It is *not* lossless under arbitrary resize. It is *exact* only for the identity transform ($S = 1$). Conflating these two properties loses **1 mark**.
> 6. **Skipping the clamp for negative indices** — If the question uses a centred-image convention and the destination pixel maps to a negative source coordinate, students often forget to clamp to zero. Watch for this in Module 3 part-derivative questions.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Bullets — Memorise Before Entering the Exam Hall:**
>
> - NNI is a **zero-order interpolant** with a $1 \times 1$ kernel — it is the **fastest** and the **blockiest** of all resampling methods.
> - Always use **inverse mapping**: $I_{dst}(x_d, y_d) = I_{src}\!\left(\mathrm{round}(x_d/S_x),\, \mathrm{round}(y_d/S_y)\right)$.
> - Scale factors are $S_x = W_{dst}/W_{src}$ and $S_y = H_{dst}/H_{src}$ — compute these **first**, before any pixel computation.
> - **Round** to the nearest integer; **clamp** to the legal source index range $[0, W_{src}-1] \times [0, H_{src}-1]$.
> - NNI **preserves exact source values** but is **not** blur-free, **not** aliasing-free, and **not** suitable for arbitrary downscale.
> - Kernel size sequence in the KTU Module 3 syllabus: $1 \times 1$ (NNI) → $2 \times 2$ (Bilinear) → $4 \times 4$ (Bicubic) → variable (Lanczos).
> - For **upscale**, expect blocky / pixelated output — this is *expected behaviour*, not a bug.
> - For **downscale**, NNI is **unsafe**; use a pre-filter or switch to Bilinear / Bicubic.
> - The output of NNI under an **identity transform** ($S = 1$) must reproduce the source exactly — use this as a free verification step.
> - Examiners love asking for the **Voronoi-cell interpretation** — memorise the phrase *"each source pixel owns a square region; destination pixels in that region inherit the source value."*
> - In MATLAB: `imresize(I, scale, 'nearest')`. In OpenCV: `cv::resize(src, dst, dsize, 0, 0, cv::INTER_NEAREST)`. In PyTorch: `F.interpolate(x, size, mode='nearest')`.
> - The KTU ESE almost always pairs an NNI question with a **Bilinear comparison** — be ready to draw the comparison table from memory.
> - Two KTU "favourite" numerical patterns: (i) $2 \times 2 \to 4 \times 4$ upscale with $S = 2$ and (ii) $3 \times 3 \to 6 \times 6$ upscale — practise both.
> - State the rounding convention explicitly: "Using round-half-up" is a one-line addition that can earn a 0.5–1 mark bonus.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bilinear Interpolation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT635 - MACHINE VISION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Bilinear Interpolation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Definition**
> **Bilinear Interpolation** is an extension of **linear interpolation** for interpolating functions of **two variables** (e.g., $x$ and $y$) on a **regular 2D grid**. It performs linear interpolation **first in one direction**, and then **again in the perpendicular direction**, producing a weighted average of the **four nearest known pixel/grid values** surrounding an unknown point.

In the context of **Machine Vision**, bilinear interpolation is a fundamental **image resampling technique** used to estimate the intensity value of a pixel at a **non-integer coordinate location** $(x, y)$ in a digital image, based on the intensity values of its **four neighboring pixels** at integer coordinates.

The mathematical expression of bilinear interpolation is a **degree-1 polynomial in 2 variables**:

$$
f(x, y) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 y + a_3 xy
$$

The coefficients $a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3$ are uniquely determined by the known values at the **four corners** of the enclosing unit square (or rectangle).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE] **Plain English Explanation**
> Imagine you are looking at a **photograph printed on a grid of tiles** (like a mosaic). You want to know the **exact color** at a point that falls **between four tiles** (top-left, top-right, bottom-left, bottom-right).

> [!TIP] **The "Average Trick"**
> - **Step 1 (Horizontal mix):** Look only at the **top two tiles** — mix them based on how close your point is to the left vs. right. Do the same for the **bottom two tiles**.
> - **Step 2 (Vertical mix):** Now mix the **two intermediate values** you just got — based on how close your point is to the top vs. the bottom.
>
> The final blended color is your **bilinearly interpolated value**.

This is essentially "linear interpolation applied **twice**" — once horizontally, once vertically. Hence the name **bi-linear** (linear in two dimensions).

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Sampling Grid** | 2D regular integer grid ($Z^2$) | Source of known pixel intensities |
| **Unknown Point $(x,y)$** | Real-valued (non-integer) coordinates | Resampling target |
| **Neighborhood Size** | $2 \times 2 = 4$ pixels | Always uses exactly 4 nearest pixels |
| **Polynomial Order** | Degree 1 in each axis (total degree = 1 + 1 = bilinear) | Linear in $x$, linear in $y$ |
| **Output Range** | Within min–max of 4 neighbors | Smooth, bounded result |

---

### 1.4 Why Bilinear Interpolation Matters in Machine Vision

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU High-Yield Application Areas**
> - **Geometric Transformations:** When an image is rotated, scaled, or sheared, the destination pixels map back to **fractional source coordinates**.
> - **Image Resizing:** Upsampling or downsampling digital images requires generating new pixel values at non-integer positions.
> - **Camera Calibration & Rectification:** Correcting lens distortion produces warped grids requiring resampling.
> - **Stereo Vision & Depth Maps:** Disparity maps require sub-pixel accurate interpolation.
> - **Texture Mapping in 3D Rendering:** Applying 2D textures onto 3D surfaces.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The bilinear interpolation algorithm can be broken down into **three linear interpolation stages**:

> **Given:**
> A point $P = (x, y)$ located inside the rectangle formed by four corner pixels:
> - $Q_{11} = f(x_1, y_1)$ — Top-Left
> - $Q_{12} = f(x_1, y_2)$ — Bottom-Left
> - $Q_{21} = f(x_2, y_1)$ — Top-Right
> - $Q_{22} = f(x_2, y_2)$ — Bottom-Right
>
> with $x_1 \leq x \leq x_2$ and $y_1 \leq y \leq y_2$.

> [!NOTE] **The Three-Step Pipeline**

**Step 1 — Interpolate along the top edge (horizontal):**
Estimate $f$ at $(x, y_1)$ by linearly interpolating between $Q_{11}$ and $Q_{21}$.

**Step 2 — Interpolate along the bottom edge (horizontal):**
Estimate $f$ at $(x, y_2)$ by linearly interpolating between $Q_{12}$ and $Q_{22}$.

**Step 3 — Interpolate vertically:**
Estimate $f(x, y)$ by linearly interpolating between the two intermediate values obtained from Steps 1 and 2.

**Why this works:** The order can be **reversed** (vertical first, then horizontal) — the result is identical because the bilinear polynomial is **commutative** under separable linear interpolation.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] **Master These Equations Before the Exam**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f(x, y_1) = \dfrac{x_2 - x}{x_2 - x_1}\, Q_{11} + \dfrac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1}\, Q_{21}$ | Linear interp along **top edge** | intensity |
| 2 | $f(x, y_2) = \dfrac{x_2 - x}{x_2 - x_1}\, Q_{12} + \dfrac{x - x_1}{x_2 - x_1}\, Q_{22}$ | Linear interp along **bottom edge** | intensity |
| 3 | $f(x, y) = \dfrac{y_2 - y}{y_2 - y_1}\, f(x, y_1) + \dfrac{y - y_1}{y_2 - y_1}\, f(x, y_2)$ | Linear interp **vertically** | intensity |
| 4 | $f(x,y) = a_0 + a_1 x + a_2 y + a_3 xy$ | Compact polynomial form | intensity |
| 5 | $w_{11} = \dfrac{(x_2-x)(y_2-y)}{(x_2-x_1)(y_2-y_1)}$ | Weight on **TL** corner $Q_{11}$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | $w_{12} = \dfrac{(x_2-x)(y-y_1)}{(x_2-x_1)(y_2-y_1)}$ | Weight on **BL** corner $Q_{12}$ | dimensionless |
| 7 | $w_{21} = \dfrac{(x-x_1)(y_2-y)}{(x_2-x_1)(y_2-y_1)}$ | Weight on **TR** corner $Q_{21}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | $w_{22} = \dfrac{(x-x_1)(y-y_1)}{(x_2-x_1)(y_2-y_1)}$ | Weight on **BR** corner $Q_{22}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | $f(x,y) = w_{11} Q_{11} + w_{12} Q_{12} + w_{21} Q_{21} + w_{22} Q_{22}$ | **Master unified equation** | intensity |
| 10 | $\sum w_{ij} = 1$ | Weight conservation property | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING] **Critical Exam Rule**
> - All four weights **must sum to exactly 1**.
> - Each weight is **non-negative** when $(x,y)$ lies inside the rectangle.
> - Use `\mid` or `\vert` in your answer scripts instead of `|` for absolute values to avoid table-breaking issues in your notes.

---

### 2.3 Special Case: Unit-Square Simplification

> [!TIP] **Most Common Exam Formulation**
> When the four surrounding pixels form a **unit square** (i.e., $x_1 = 0, y_1 = 0, x_2 = 1, y_2 = 1$), the formula simplifies dramatically. Let $\alpha = x - x_1$ and $\beta = y - y_1$:

$$
f(x, y) = (1-\alpha)(1-\beta)\, Q_{11} + (1-\alpha)\beta\, Q_{12} + \alpha(1-\beta)\, Q_{21} + \alpha\beta\, Q_{22}
$$

This is the **default form** most KTU questions test.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Use of Bilinear Interpolation |
|---|---|
| **Medical Imaging (MRI/CT)** | Reslicing volumetric scans at oblique angles |
| **Satellite Imaging** | Georectification of raster images to geographic coordinates |
| **Industrial Inspection** | Sub-pixel edge detection accuracy in machine vision cameras |
| **Autonomous Vehicles** | Remapping camera views after ego-motion compensation |
| **Optical Character Recognition (OCR)** | Normalizing scanned documents to standard sizes |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Master Equation (Three-Stage)

> [!NOTE] **Derivation Goal**
> To derive the unified 4-weight bilinear interpolation equation from the three sequential linear interpolations.

---

**Derivation — Stage 1: Linear Interpolation Along Top Edge**

We wish to estimate $f(x, y_1)$ where $x_1 \leq x \leq x_2$.

The standard 1D linear interpolation formula is:

$$
f(x) = f(x_1) + \frac{(x - x_1)}{(x_2 - x_1)}\,[f(x_2) - f(x_1)]
$$

Substituting $f(x_1) = Q_{11}$ and $f(x_2) = Q_{21}$:

$$
f(x, y_1) = Q_{11} + \frac{(x - x_1)}{(x_2 - x_1)}(Q_{21} - Q_{11})
$$

Rearranging by separating the coefficients of $Q_{11}$ and $Q_{21}$:

$$
f(x, y_1) = \frac{(x_2 - x)}{(x_2 - x_1)}\, Q_{11} + \frac{(x - x_1)}{(x_2 - x_1)}\, Q_{21}
$$

---

**Derivation — Stage 2: Linear Interpolation Along Bottom Edge**

Following the identical reasoning with $f(x_1) = Q_{12}$ and $f(x_2) = Q_{22}$:

$$
f(x, y_2) = \frac{(x_2 - x)}{(x_2 - x_1)}\, Q_{12} + \frac{(x - x_1)}{(x_2 - x_1)}\, Q_{22}
$$

---

**Derivation — Stage 3: Linear Interpolation Along Vertical Direction**

Now treat the two intermediate values as endpoints and interpolate between $y_1$ and $y_2$:

$$
f(x, y) = \frac{(y_2 - y)}{(y_2 - y_1)}\, f(x, y_1) + \frac{(y - y_1)}{(y_2 - y_1)}\, f(x, y_2)
$$

---

**Derivation — Stage 4: Substituting Stages 1 & 2 into Stage 3**

$$
f(x, y) = \frac{(y_2 - y)}{(y_2 - y_1)} \left[\frac{(x_2 - x)}{(x_2 - x_1)}\, Q_{11} + \frac{(x - x_1)}{(x_2 - x_1)}\, Q_{21}\right] + \frac{(y - y_1)}{(y_2 - y_1)} \left[\frac{(x_2 - x)}{(x_2 - x_1)}\, Q_{12} + \frac{(x - x_1)}{(x_2 - x_1)}\, Q_{22}\right]
$$

Grouping each $Q_{ij}$ with its corresponding weight factor:

$$
f(x, y) = \frac{(x_2 - x)(y_2 - y)}{(x_2 - x_1)(y_2 - y_1)}\, Q_{11} + \frac{(x - x_1)(y_2 - y)}{(x_2 - x_1)(y_2 - y_1)}\, Q_{21} + \frac{(x_2 - x)(y - y_1)}{(x_2 - x_1)(y_2 - y_1)}\, Q_{12} + \frac{(x - x_1)(y - y_1)}{(x_2 - x_1)(y_2 - y_1)}\, Q_{22}
$$

This matches the master equation stated in the Formula Sheet (rows 5–9).

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

> [!IMPORTANT] **Worked Example**
> Given the four pixel intensities at the corners of a unit square:
> - $Q_{11} = f(0, 0) = 10$ (Top-Left)
> - $Q_{12} = f(0, 1) = 20$ (Bottom-Left)
> - $Q_{21} = f(1, 0) = 30$ (Top-Right)
> - $Q_{22} = f(1, 1) = 40$ (Bottom-Right)
>
> Find $f(0.3, 0.6)$.

**Solution:**

Since $x_1 = 0, y_1 = 0, x_2 = 1, y_2 = 1$, we use $\alpha = 0.3$ and $\beta = 0.6$:

**Step 1 — Compute the four weights:**

$$
w_{11} = (1 - 0.3)(1 - 0.6) = (0.7)(0.4) = 0.28
$$

$$
w_{12} = (1 - 0.3)(0.6) = (0.7)(0.6) = 0.42
$$

$$
w_{21} = (0.3)(1 - 0.6) = (0.3)(0.4) = 0.12
$$

$$
w_{22} = (0.3)(0.6) = 0.18
$$

**Step 2 — Verify the weights sum to 1:**

$$
0.28 + 0.42 + 0.12 + 0.18 = 1.00 \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the master equation:**

$$
f(0.3, 0.6) = (0.28)(10) + (0.42)(20) + (0.12)(30) + (0.18)(40)
$$

$$
f(0.3, 0.6) = 2.8 + 8.4 + 3.6 + 7.2
$$

$$
\boxed{f(0.3, 0.6) = 22.0}
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Three Interpolation Methods Compared

> [!NOTE] **Why Bilinear Beats Simpler Alternatives**

| Property | Nearest-Neighbor | **Bilinear** | Bicubic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Neighbors used | 1 | **4** | 16 |
| Polynomial degree | 0 (step) | **1 (linear × linear)** | 3 |
| Computational cost | Lowest | **Moderate** | Highest |
| Smoothness | Poor (blocky) | **Good (continuous)** | Excellent |
| Derivative at edges | Discontinuous | **Continuous** | Continuous + smooth |
| Edge preservation | Sharp but jagged | **Slight blur** | Better |
| Common KTU use case | Fast masks | **Geometric transforms** | High-quality resize |

> [!TIP] **Exam Mnemonic:** *"Bilinear is the **workhorse** of machine vision — fast, smooth, and exam-friendly."*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Bilinear Interpolation Geometry

The following diagram illustrates the geometry of the four neighboring pixels and the location of the unknown interpolation point.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["TL Pixel Q11 = f x1 y1"] --- B["TR Pixel Q21 = f x2 y1"]
    C["BL Pixel Q12 = f x1 y2"] --- D["BR Pixel Q22 = f x2 y2"]
    A --- C
    B --- D
```
