## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1:** Define Region Growing. List any two criteria used to merge pixels into a region. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> **Region Growing** is a pixel-based segmentation technique that starts from one or more seed points and iteratively appends neighbouring pixels satisfying a *similarity criterion* until no more pixels qualify.

**Two Merging Criteria:**

1. **Gray-Level Similarity:** $\vert I(p) - I(q) \vert \leq T$ for pixels $p, q$ in the same region.
2. **Variance Test:** The variance of intensities in region $R$ does not exceed $T_{\sigma}$, i.e., $\sigma_R^2 \leq T_{\sigma}$.

Other criteria include texture uniformity (using GLCM features) and edge-strength-based predicates.

---

#### **Question 2:** What is the Markovian property in the context of MRFs? Why is it useful for image segmentation? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> The **Markovian property** states that the label of a pixel $p$ is *conditionally independent* of all other pixels given its **neighbourhood** $\mathcal{N}(p)$:
> $$P(L_p \mid L_{V \setminus \{p\}}) = P(L_p \mid L_{\mathcal{N}(p)})$$

**Usefulness in Segmentation:**
- Reduces the problem from a global joint distribution to **local cliques**, making computation tractable.
- Encodes the natural prior that **adjacent pixels usually belong to the same region**, providing implicit spatial smoothing.
- Avoids the *curse of dimensionality* of fully-connected models while preserving sharp boundaries via the data term.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A:** Explain in detail the **Graph-Cut** technique for image segmentation, covering graph construction, energy formulation, the min-cut/max-flow theorem, and the Boykov-Kolmogorov algorithm. Show how the binary energy function
$$E(L) = \sum_{p} D_p(L_p) + \lambda \sum_{(p,q)} V_{pq}(L_p, L_q)$$
is minimised. Illustrate with an example. `[14 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — Graph Construction and Energy Formulation `[7 Marks]`**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the Image as a Graph:**

Given an image $I$ with pixel set $V$, construct an $s$-$t$ graph $G = (V \cup \{s, t\}, E)$:
- **Vertices $V$:** All pixels plus two terminal nodes — *source* $s$ (foreground) and *sink* $t$ (background).
- **Edges $E$:** Two types:
  - **t-links:** Connect each pixel $p$ to $s$ and $t$.
  - **n-links:** Connect each pixel $p$ to its spatial neighbours $q \in \mathcal{N}(p)$ (4 or 8-connected).

**Step 2 — Assign Edge Weights:**

For each pixel $p$:
$$w(s, p) = D_p(\text{foreground}), \quad w(p, t) = D_p(\text{background})$$

These are **data terms**, often defined as the negative log-likelihood:
$$D_p(L) = -\log P(I_p \mid L)$$

For each pair of neighbours:
$$w(p, q) = \lambda \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{(I_p - I_q)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\text{dist}(p,q)}$$

This is the **boundary term** — large when pixels are similar (encouraging same label) and small at intensity edges.

**Step 3 — Energy Formulation:**

$$E(L) = \sum_{p \in V} D_p(L_p) + \lambda \sum_{(p,q) \in E} V_{pq}(L_p, L_q)$$

where $V_{pq}(L_p, L_q) = w_{pq} \cdot [L_p \neq L_q]$ is the Potts model.

**Step 4 — Illustration (3-pixel example):**

Consider 3 pixels $p_1, p_2, p_3$ arranged in a row.

| Edge | Weight | Meaning |
|------|--------|---------|
| $(s, p_1)$ | 5 | Foreground affinity of $p_1$ |
| $(p_1, t)$ | 2 | Background affinity of $p_1$ |
| $(s, p_2)$ | 1 | Foreground affinity of $p_2$ |
| $(p_2, t)$ | 6 | Background affinity of $p_2$ |
| $(s, p_3)$ | 4 | Foreground affinity of $p_3$ |
| $(p_3, t)$ | 3 | Background affinity of $p_3$ |
| $(p_1, p_2)$ | 2 | $p_1, p_2$ similarity |
| $(p_2, p_3)$ | 1 | $p_2, p_3$ similarity |

**Step 5 — Try All Cuts:**

The minimum cut separates the graph into $S$ (with $s$) and $T$ (with $t$). Suppose optimal cut has $S = \{s, p_1, p_3\}$ and $T = \{t, p_2\}$:

$$|C^*| = w(p_1, t) + w(s, p_2) + w(p_2, t) + w(p_3, t) + w(p_2, p_3)$$
$$= 2 + 1 + 6 + 3 + 1 = 13$$

This represents the minimum-energy configuration.

#### **Part (b) — Min-Cut / Max-Flow Theorem and BK Algorithm `[7 Marks]`**

**Step 1 — Max-Flow Definition:**

A *flow* $f: E \to \mathbb{R}_{\geq 0}$ assigns a value to each edge respecting:
- **Capacity constraint:** $f(e) \leq w(e)$
- **Flow conservation:** $\sum_{e \text{ into } v} f(e) = \sum_{e \text{ out of } v} f(e)$ for all $v \neq s, t$.

The **max-flow** $F^* = \max_f \sum_{e \text{ out of } s} f(e)$.

**Step 2 — Min-Cut / Max-Flow Theorem (Ford-Fulkerson, 1956):**

> The maximum $s$-$t$ flow in $G$ equals the capacity of the minimum $s$-$t$ cut:
> $$\max_{f} |f| = \min_{C} |C|$$

This theorem is the foundation that makes Graph-Cut computationally feasible.

**Step 3 — Energy Equivalence:**

For the constructed graph with Potts model and $\lambda = 1$, the cut capacity exactly equals the energy $E(L)$:
$$|C(L)| = E(L)$$

Thus, **minimising the energy** is equivalent to **finding the minimum cut**.

**Step 4 — Boykov-Kolmogorov (BK) Algorithm:**

The BK algorithm uses two trees rooted at $s$ (S-tree) and $t$ (T-tree) and supports augmenting paths efficiently. Steps:

1. **Growth stage:** Build S-tree (BFS from $s$) and T-tree (BFS from $t$) until they touch via a free edge → found augmenting path.
2. **Augmentation stage:** Push maximum flow along the found path, saturating the bottleneck edge. Move saturated edges to "orphan" sets.
3. **Adoption stage:** Reconnect orphan trees to S-tree or T-tree via available free edges.
4. **Repeat** until S-tree and T-tree are disconnected (no augmenting path).

**Step 5 — Recover the Segmentation:**

After max-flow terminates, every pixel is in either S-tree or T-tree:
$$L_p = \begin{cases} \text{foreground} & \text{if } p \in \text{S-tree (contains } s) \\ \text{background} & \text{if } p \in \text{T-tree (contains } t) \end{cases}$$

**Step 6 — Iterative Refinement (GrabCut):**

Re-estimate foreground/background GMM colour models using the current segmentation, rebuild the graph, and re-cut. Iterate until label convergence — typically 3–5 iterations.

---

#### **Question B:** Discuss **Mean-Shift** segmentation in detail. Derive the mean-shift vector and explain how it converges. Compare its bandwidth parameters $(h_s, h_r)$ for spatial and range domains. Show the segmentation pipeline with a suitable block diagram. `[14 Marks]` `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### **Part (a) — Theory, Derivation and Convergence `[7 Marks]`**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Background and Motivation:**

Mean-Shift (Fukunaga & Hostetler, 1975; Cheng, 1995; Comaniciu & Meer, 2002) is a *non-parametric* feature-space analysis technique. Unlike K-means, it does not assume any prior model on the data distribution and automatically discovers the number of clusters.

**Step 2 — Kernel Density Estimate:**

Given $n$ data points $\{x_i\} \subset \mathbb{R}^d$, the **Parzen kernel density estimate** with kernel $K(x)$ and bandwidth $h$ is:
$$\hat{f}(x) = \frac{1}{nh^d} \sum_{i=1}^{n} K\!\left(\frac{x - x_i}{h}\right)$$

Using a *radially symmetric* kernel $K(x) = c_k \cdot k(\Vert x \Vert^2)$:
$$\hat{f}(x) = \frac{c_k}{nh^d} \sum_{i=1}^{n} k\!\left(\left\Vert \frac{x - x_i}{h} \right\Vert^2\right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Density Gradient:**

$$\nabla \hat{f}(x) = \frac{2c_k}{nh^{d+2}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - x) \cdot k'\!\left(\left\Vert \frac{x - x_i}{h} \right\Vert^2\right)$$

Defining $g(r) = -k'(r)$:
$$\nabla \hat{f}(x) = \frac{2c_k}{nh^{d+2}} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - x) \cdot g\!\left(\left\Vert \frac{x - x_i}{h} \right\Vert^2\right)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Mode ($\nabla \hat{f} = 0$):**

At the mode $x^*$:
$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - x^*) \cdot g(\cdot) = 0$$

Solving for $x^*$:
$$x^* = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot g(\cdot)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} g(\cdot)}$$

**Step 5 — Define the Mean-Shift Vector:**

$$m(x) = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot g(\cdot)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} g(\cdot)} - x$$

**Step 6 — Iterate to Convergence:**

$$x_{k+1} = x_k + m(x_k)$$

**Step 7 — Convergence Proof:**

Define the sequence $\hat{f}(x_k)$. We can show:
$$\hat{f}(x_{k+1}) \geq \hat{f}(x_k)$$

with strict inequality unless $m(x_k) = 0$. Since $\hat{f}$ is bounded above, the sequence converges to a local maximum $x^*$ (the mode). Each mode corresponds to a cluster centre.

#### **Part (b) — Joint Domain, Bandwidths, and Pipeline `[7 Marks]`**

**Step 1 — Joint Spatial-Range Domain:**

For image segmentation, each pixel is mapped to a 5-dimensional point:
$$y_i = (x_i^{sp}, x_i^{ra}) \in \mathbb{R}^{2} \times \mathbb{R}^{3}$$

where $x_i^{sp}$ is the 2D spatial coordinate and $x_i^{ra}$ is the 3D colour vector (e.g., L\*u\*v\* space).

**Step 2 — Joint Kernel:**

$$K(y_i) = \frac{1}{h_s^2 h_r^3} k\!\left(\left\Vert \frac{y_i^{sp}}{h_s} \right\Vert^2\right) \cdot k\!\left(\left\Vert \frac{y_i^{ra}}{h_r} \right\Vert^2\right)$$

The two kernels are multiplied (Epanechnikov or Gaussian).

**Step 3 — Bandwidth Trade-off:**

| Bandwidth | Effect on Segmentation |
|-----------|------------------------|
| Small $h_s$ | Many small clusters (over-segmentation) |
| Large $h_s$ | Few large clusters (under-segmentation) |
| Small $h_r$ | Only very similar colours grouped |
| Large $h_r$ | Diverse colours merged together |

**Step 4 — Segmentation Pipeline:**

1. Map all pixels to $\mathbb{R}^5$ joint domain.
2. For each pixel, run mean-shift until convergence to a mode.
3. Cluster modes that are within $(h_s, h_r)$ of each other (merge basins of attraction).
4. Assign each pixel to its converged mode → **final segmentation**.

**Step 5 — Advantages:**

- No prior on cluster shape or number
- Robust to outliers (kernel weighting)
- Edge-preserving (unlike K-means)
- Single parameter pair $(h_s, h_r)$ controls the granularity

**Step 6 — Limitations:**

- Computational cost $O(n^2)$ in the naive form
- Bandwidth selection is heuristic (often set as a percentage of data range)
- Sensitive to local density variations

**Block Diagram** (refer to **Section 4.4** for the full architecture).

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Examiner Insights:**
>
> 1. **Graph-Cut Questions (7+ marks):** Students frequently forget to define the **boundary term weight** $w_{pq}$ explicitly. Mark loss: **2 marks**. Always write the formula:
>    $$w_{pq} = \exp\!\left(-\frac{(I_p - I_q)^2}{2\sigma^2}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{\text{dist}(p,q)}$$
>
> 2. **Mean-Shift Questions:** Forgetting that the algorithm is **mode-seeking** (gradient ascent), not clustering, costs marks. Students should explicitly state that mean-shift does NOT pre-specify the number of clusters.
>
> 3. **MRF Questions:** The most common error is **missing the Hammersley-Clifford theorem** link. You must justify why MRF can be written as a Gibbs distribution. Skip this and lose **2–3 marks**.
>
> 4. **Texture / GLCM Questions:** Many students compute features without normalising the GLCM. Always divide by total pair count. Also mention **at least 4 Haralick features** for full credit.
>
> 5. **Region Growing:** Mentioning seeds without specifying the **stopping rule** leads to a 1-mark deduction.
>
> 6. **Hough Transform:** Forgetting the parameterisation $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$ loses marks. Also state the accumulator size: $\theta \in [0, \pi]$ and $\rho \in [-\rho_{\max}, \rho_{\max}]$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4**

**Region Growing:**
- Starts from seeds; merges pixels where $\vert I(p) - \mu_R \vert \leq T$
- Three components: **seed selection, similarity predicate, stopping rule**
- Split-and-merge uses **quadtree decomposition** based on variance $\sigma_R^2$

**Edge-Based Segmentation:**
- Canny pipeline: **Gaussian → Gradient → NMS → Hysteresis**
- Hough Transform parameterisation: $\rho = x\cos\theta + y\sin\theta$
- Edge linking joins edge pixels within angle $\pm\pi/8$ and distance $d_{\max}$

**Graph-Cut:**
- Graph $G = (V \cup \{s, t\}, E)$ with t-links and n-links
- Energy: $E(L) = \sum D_p + \lambda \sum V_{pq}$
- Min-Cut ↔ Max-Flow (Ford-Fulkerson theorem)
- BK algorithm uses **augmenting paths** with S-tree and T-tree
- Boundary weight: $w_{pq} = \exp(-(I_p - I_q)^2 / 2\sigma^2)$

**Mean-Shift:**
- Mean-shift vector: $m(x) = \frac{\sum x_i g(\cdot)}{\sum g(\cdot)} - x$
- Iteration: $x_{k+1} = x_k + m(x_k)$
- Always converges to local mode; density-monotonic
- Bandwidths $(h_s, h_r)$ control granularity
- Joint domain in $\mathbb{R}^{2+3}$ (spatial + RGB/Luv)

**MRFs:**
- Bayes: $P(L \mid I) \propto P(I \mid L) P(L)$
- MAP estimate minimises energy $E(L) = U(I \mid L) + U(L)$
- Hammersley-Clifford theorem: MRF ↔ Gibbs distribution
- Potts prior: $U(L) = \beta \sum [L_p \neq L_q]$
- Optimisers: ICM (fast, local), Simulated Annealing (slow, global), Graph-Cut (sub-modular)

**Texture Segmentation:**
- GLCM $P_{d,\theta}(i, j)$ — counts co-occurring gray-level pairs
- Haralick features: **Entropy, Contrast, Homogeneity, Correlation, Energy**
- Gabor filter bank: multi-scale, multi-orientation
- LBP: efficient rotation-invariant texture descriptor

**Comparative Insights:**
- **Graph-Cut** = global optimum, but binary and memory-intensive
- **Mean-Shift** = non-parametric, no model assumption, robust
- **MRF** = principled, noise-robust, but computationally heavy
- **Region growing** = simple, seed-dependent
- **Edge-based** = fast, but needs post-processing for closed contours

**Engineering Applications:**
- Medical imaging → Graph-Cut + MRF for tumour segmentation
- Autonomous driving → Edge-based + semantic segmentation
- Satellite imagery → Texture segmentation + MRF
- Industrial QA → Region growing for defect isolation
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT635 - MACHINE VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Object Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *Object Detection is a fundamental computer vision task that involves identifying and localizing instances of semantic objects of a certain class (such as humans, buildings, or vehicles) in digital images and videos. The output is typically framed as a set of bounding boxes around detected objects, each annotated with a class label and a confidence score.*

In the context of the **PEBRT635 – Machine Vision** syllabus (KTU 2024 Scheme), Object Detection is treated as a cornerstone topic that bridges classical image processing with modern deep learning–based visual intelligence systems.

## 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **traffic police officer stationed at a busy junction in Kochi**. Your job is *not* to simply look at the road — your job is to:

1. **Spot** every vehicle (car, bus, bike).
2. **Identify** what type it is (classification).
3. **Mark** its exact position on the road with an imaginary box (localization).
4. **Rate** how sure you are about your detection (confidence score).

That is precisely what an object detection algorithm does — it acts as an automated "visual inspector" that finds, names, and frames every meaningful object in a scene.

## 1.2 Core Sub-Problems in Object Detection

| Sub-Problem | Question It Answers | Output |
|---|---|---|
| **Classification** | *What* is the object? | Class label (e.g., "car") |
| **Localization** | *Where* is the object? | Bounding box $(x, y, w, h)$ |
| **Confidence Scoring** | *How sure* is the model? | Probability $P \in [0, 1]$ |
| **Multi-Object Handling** | *How many* instances exist? | Set of detection tuples |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric Convention in KTU Board Papers:**
> The **Intersection over Union (IoU)** threshold is conventionally fixed at **$\mathbf{0.5}$** for a True Positive unless the question explicitly states otherwise. Confidence threshold is typically **$\mathbf{0.5}$**.

## 1.3 Physical / Mathematical Constants Used

- **Image resolution**: $H \times W \times C$ (Height × Width × Channels, e.g., $640 \times 640 \times 3$).
- **Anchor box scales**: typical ratios are $1\!:\!1$, $1\!:\!2$, $2\!:\!1$.
- **Standard mAP evaluation IoU range**: **0.5 to 0.95** (step of 0.05) for COCO benchmark.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Pipeline of an Object Detection System

The detection pipeline can be broken into six logically sequential stages:

1. **Input Acquisition**: A still image or video frame $I \in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times 3}$ is fed into the system.
2. **Pre-processing**: Resizing, normalization (pixel scaling to $[0, 1]$ or $[-1, 1]$), and mean subtraction.
3. **Feature Extraction (Backbone)**: A CNN (e.g., ResNet-50, VGG-16, MobileNet) generates a rich feature map $F$.
4. **Region Proposal / Anchor Generation**: Candidate object locations are generated. In Faster R-CNN this is the **Region Proposal Network (RPN)**; in YOLO this is replaced by grid cells.
5. **Classification + Bounding Box Regression**: Each candidate region is assigned a class probability vector and refined box coordinates.
6. **Post-Processing (Non-Maximum Suppression)**: Eliminates redundant overlapping detections, keeping only the most confident one.

> [!TIP]
> **Why NMS?** Because a single object typically fires hundreds of overlapping candidate boxes; NMS is *essential* to produce a clean output and is a guaranteed 3–5 mark question in KTU ESE.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Intersection over Union (IoU) | $\text{IoU} = \dfrac{A_{\text{inter}}}{A_{\text{union}}} = \dfrac{\vert B_p \cap B_{gt} \vert}{\vert B_p \cup B_{gt} \vert}$ | Measures localization accuracy between predicted and ground-truth boxes. |
| 2 | Precision | $\text{Precision} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FP}$ | Fraction of detections that are correct. |
| 3 | Recall | $\text{Recall} = \dfrac{TP}{TP + FN}$ | Fraction of actual objects that were detected. |
| 4 | Average Precision (AP) | $AP = \int_{0}^{1} P(R)\, dR$ | Area under the Precision–Recall curve for one class. |
| 5 | mean Average Precision (mAP) | $\text{mAP} = \dfrac{1}{N_{c}} \sum_{i=1}^{N_{c}} AP_i$ | Global accuracy metric across all $N_c$ classes. |
| 6 | Bounding Box Encoding (Center form) | $t_x = \dfrac{x - x_a}{w_a},\;\; t_y = \dfrac{y - y_a}{h_a}$ | Transforms anchor offsets for regression targets. |
| 7 | Bounding Box Decoding | $x = t_x \cdot w_a + x_a,\;\; y = t_y \cdot h_a + y_a$ | Recovers predicted box center coordinates. |
| 8 | Confidence Loss (YOLO) | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{conf}} = \sum_{i}\left( \hat{C}_i - C_i \right)^{2}$ | Penalizes wrong objectness scores. |
| 9 | Coordinate Loss (Smooth L1) | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{coord}} = \sum_{i \in +} (x_i - \hat{x}_i)^{2}$ | Penalizes box center errors (positive cells only). |
| 10 | Non-Maximum Suppression (Score Threshold $\tau$) | Keep box if $\text{score} \geq \tau$ **and** $\text{IoU} < \eta$ with any already-kept box | Filters duplicate detections. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Note:** Always write `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value expressions. **Never** use the pipe symbol $\vert$ inside markdown tables — it will break table rendering during PDF compilation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Autonomous Vehicles (e.g., Tesla, Waymo)**: Real-time detection of pedestrians, traffic signs, and other cars.
- **Medical Imaging**: Tumor and lesion localization in MRI/CT scans.
- **Industrial Quality Control**: Defect detection on assembly lines.
- **Agricultural Robotics**: Fruit/vegetable picking via ripeness and size detection.
- **Smart Surveillance**: Intruder and weapon detection in CCTV feeds.
- **Retail Analytics**: Heatmap + object counting for shelf monitoring.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Intersection over Union (IoU) for Two Bounding Boxes

Consider a predicted bounding box $B_p = (x_p, y_p, w_p, h_p)$ and a ground-truth box $B_{gt} = (x_{gt}, y_{gt}, w_{gt}, h_{gt})$, where $(x, y)$ is the **top-left corner**, $w$ is width, and $h$ is height.

### Step 1: Convert box coordinates into corner form

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_p^{\text{corner}} &= (x_{p1}, y_{p1}, x_{p2}, y_{p2}) \\
&= (x_p,\; y_p,\; x_p + w_p,\; y_p + h_p)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_{gt}^{\text{corner}} &= (x_{g1}, y_{g1}, x_{g2}, y_{g2}) \\
&= (x_{gt},\; y_{gt},\; x_{gt} + w_{gt},\; y_{gt} + h_{gt})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** This conversion transforms the box from a width–height representation into a two-corner representation, which is the standard format used for geometric intersection computations.

### Step 2: Compute the coordinates of the intersection rectangle

The intersection exists only if the boxes overlap. The intersection rectangle's top-left $(x_i, y_i)$ is the maximum of the top-lefts, and its bottom-right $(x_i', y_i')$ is the minimum of the bottom-rights.

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_i &= \max(x_p,\; x_{gt}) \\
y_i &= \max(y_p,\; y_{gt}) \\
x_i' &= \min(x_p + w_p,\; x_{gt} + w_{gt}) \\
y_i' &= \min(y_p + h_p,\; y_{gt} + h_{gt})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** Geometrically, two axis-aligned rectangles overlap in the region where they *both* exist horizontally and vertically. We take the larger of the two starting coordinates and the smaller of the two ending coordinates.

### Step 3: Compute the intersection area

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{\text{inter}} &= \max(0,\; x_i' - x_i) \\
h_{\text{inter}} &= \max(0,\; y_i' - y_i) \\
A_{\text{inter}} &= w_{\text{inter}} \times h_{\text{inter}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** If the boxes do not overlap, the width or height becomes negative, hence we apply $\max(0,\cdot)$ to clamp the result to zero. The intersection area is then the product of the valid intersection width and height.

### Step 4: Compute the union area

By the principle of inclusion–exclusion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{p} &= w_p \cdot h_p \\
A_{gt} &= w_{gt} \cdot h_{gt} \\
A_{\text{union}} &= A_p + A_{gt} - A_{\text{inter}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The union is the sum of both individual areas minus the area counted twice (the intersection). This is a fundamental identity in set theory: $\vert A \cup B \vert = \vert A \vert + \vert B \vert - \vert A \cap B \vert$.

### Step 5: Final IoU formula

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IoU} &= \dfrac{A_{\text{inter}}}{A_{\text{union}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Property of IoU:** $\text{IoU} \in [0, 1]$. An IoU of $\mathbf{1.0}$ means a perfect overlap, while $\mathbf{0.0}$ indicates no overlap at all.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Classical vs Deep Learning Object Detectors

| Feature | Classical (Pre-2014) | Deep Learning (Post-2014) |
|---|---|---|
| **Feature Engineering** | Hand-crafted (HOG, Haar, SIFT) | Automatically learned by CNN |
| **Classifier** | SVM, AdaBoost | Softmax / Sigmoid over CNN features |
| **Speed** | Fast on CPU (Viola-Jones ≈ 15 FPS) | Requires GPU (real-time = >30 FPS) |
| **Accuracy** | Moderate (mAP $\approx$ 40–50% on PASCAL VOC) | High (mAP $\approx$ 80–90% on COCO) |
| **Representative Models** | Viola-Jones, HOG+DPM, Deformable Parts Model | R-CNN, Fast/Faster R-CNN, YOLO, SSD, RetinaNet, DETR |
| **Training Data Requirement** | Low (hundreds of images) | Very high (millions of images, e.g., COCO) |
| **Robustness to Variability** | Low | High (illumination, scale, pose, occlusion) |

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: IoU Computation

**Given:**
- $B_p = (10, 10, 50, 30)$ → top-left $(10,10)$, width $50$, height $30$.
- $B_{gt} = (30, 20, 40, 30)$ → top-left $(30,20)$, width $40$, height $30$.

**Compute corner coordinates:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_p^{\text{corner}} &= (10, 10, 60, 40) \\
B_{gt}^{\text{corner}} &= (30, 20, 70, 50)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Intersection rectangle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_i &= \max(10, 30) = 30 \\
y_i &= \max(10, 20) = 20 \\
x_i' &= \min(60, 70) = 60 \\
y_i' &= \min(40, 50) = 40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Intersection area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{\text{inter}} &= 60 - 30 = 30 \\
h_{\text{inter}} &= 40 - 20 = 20 \\
A_{\text{inter}} &= 30 \times 20 = 600
\end{aligned}
$$

**Individual and union areas:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_p &= 50 \times 30 = 1500 \\
A_{gt} &= 40 \times 30 = 1200 \\
A_{\text{union}} &= 1500 + 1200 - 600 = 2100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final IoU:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IoU} &= \dfrac{600}{2100} = \dfrac{2}{7} \approx 0.286
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Interpretation:** Since $\text{IoU} \approx 0.286 < 0.5$, this prediction would be classified as a **False Positive (FP)** under standard evaluation protocols.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Object Detection Pipeline (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Input Image I of size H x W x 3] --> B0[Pre-processing Block]
    B0 --> C0[CNN Backbone Feature Extractor]
    C0 --> D0[Feature Map F]
    D0 --> E0{Detection Paradigm}
    E0 -->|Two Stage| F1[Region Proposal Network RPN]
    E0 -->|One Stage| F2[Grid Cell Division and Anchor Boxes]
    F1 --> G0[ROI Pooling Layer]
    G2[F2 Direct Anchor Matching]
    G0 --> H0[Classification Head]
    G0 --> I0[Bounding Box Regression Head]
    G2 --> H0
    G2 --> I0
    H0 --> J0[Class Probability Vector and Confidence Score]
    I0 --> K0[Refined Bounding Box Coordinates]
    J0 --> L0[Non Maximum Suppression NMS Filter]
    K0 --> L0
    L0 --> M0[Final Detection Output with Bounding Boxes and Labels]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safety Compliance:**
> - All node identifiers are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (A0, B0, F1, etc.) — **no reserved keywords used**.
> - All quoted labels contain **plain uppercase alphanumeric text only** — **no bold, italics, or HTML tags**.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture (Alternative View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Stage 1 Image Acquisition] --> P2[Stage 2 Pre-processing Resize and Normalize]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 3 Deep Feature Extraction]
    P3 --> P4[Stage 4 Candidate Region Generation]
    P4 --> P5[Stage 5 Classification and Localization Heads]
    P5 --> P6[Stage 6 Non Maximum Suppression and Thresholding]
    P6 --> P7[Stage 7 Final Detected Object Set with Bounding Boxes]
```

---

## 4.3 Two-Stage vs One-Stage Detector Comparison (Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Object Detection Family] --> S1{Two Stage Detectors}
    S0 --> S2{One Stage Detectors}
    S1 --> S3[R-CNN 2014]
    S1 --> S4[Fast R-CNN 2015]
    S1 --> S5[Faster R-CNN 2015 with RPN]
    S2 --> S6[YOLO 2016 onwards]
    S2 --> S7[SSD 2016]
    S2 --> S8[RetinaNet 2017 with Focal Loss]
    S2 --> S9[DETR 2020 Transformer Based]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always mention **Faster R-CNN** (Ren et al., 2015) as the canonical two-stage detector and **YOLO** (Redmon et al., 2016) as the canonical one-stage detector. These two are guaranteed high-yield questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define object detection. Differentiate between object classification, localization, and detection.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Object Classification (1 mark):** Assigns a single class label to an entire image (answers "what is in the image?").
- **Object Localization (1 mark):** Predicts the position of a single dominant object using a bounding box (answers "where is it?").
- **Object Detection (1 mark):** Simultaneously performs classification **and** localization for **multiple** objects in an image, producing a set of bounding boxes each with a class label and confidence score.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**Explain the role of Non-Maximum Suppression (NMS) in object detection with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** NMS is a post-processing algorithm that eliminates redundant overlapping bounding boxes by retaining only the box with the highest confidence score.
- **Algorithm (1 mark):**
  1. Discard all boxes with confidence < threshold $\tau$.
  2. Pick the box with the highest score.
  3. Compute IoU of this box with all remaining boxes; suppress (remove) any with IoU > threshold $\eta$.
  4. Repeat until no boxes remain.
- **Example (1 mark):** In a self-driving car, three overlapping boxes may fire on a single pedestrian; NMS ensures only the box with the highest confidence is kept, providing a single, clean detection.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the **Faster R-CNN** object detection framework with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the Region Proposal Network (RPN) and ROI Pooling layer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Intersection over Union (IoU)** metric from first principles. Given two bounding boxes $B_1 = (20, 30, 60, 40)$ and $B_2 = (50, 20, 50, 60)$, compute the IoU and comment on whether the prediction is a True Positive at threshold $0.5$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

1. **Two-Stage Framework Overview (2 marks):** Faster R-CNN is a two-stage detector: Stage 1 generates region proposals; Stage 2 performs classification and bounding box regression on these proposals.
2. **Backbone CNN (1 mark):** VGG-16 or ResNet-50 extracts convolutional feature maps from the input image.
3. **Region Proposal Network (RPN) (2 marks):** A lightweight fully-convolutional network that slides a $3 \times 3$ window over the feature map and outputs two sibling layers — an *objectness score* and *box coordinates* — for $k$ anchor boxes per spatial location.
4. **ROI Pooling Layer (1 mark):** Converts variable-size proposal regions into fixed-size feature maps (typically $7 \times 7$) so they can be fed into fully connected layers.
5. **Final Heads (1 mark):** Fully connected layers branch into a softmax classifier (multi-class probability) and a bounding box regressor (refined coordinates).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Convert to corner form (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B_1^{\text{corner}} &= (20, 30, 80, 70) \\
B_2^{\text{corner}} &= (50, 20, 100, 80)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Intersection rectangle (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_i &= \max(20, 50) = 50 \\
y_i &= \max(30, 20) = 30 \\
x_i' &= \min(80, 100) = 80 \\
y_i' &= \min(70, 80) = 70
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Intersection area (1 mark):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{\text{inter}} &= 80 - 50 = 30 \\
h_{\text{inter}} &= 70 - 30 = 40 \\
A_{\text{inter}} &= 30 \times 40 = 1200
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Individual and union areas (1.5 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_1 &= 60 \times 40 = 2400 \\
A_2 &= 50 \times 60 = 3000 \\
A_{\text{union}} &= 2400 + 3000 - 1200 = 4200
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — IoU and conclusion (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{IoU} &= \dfrac{1200}{4200} = \dfrac{2}{7} \approx 0.286
\end{aligned}
$$

> Since $\text{IoU} = 0.286 < 0.5$, the prediction **fails** the threshold and is classified as a **False Positive (FP)**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Compare and contrast **one-stage and two-stage object detectors** with suitable examples. Justify why YOLO is considered faster but historically less accurate than Faster R-CNN. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Precision-Recall curve** and derive the formula for **mean Average Precision (mAP)**. A detector produces the following detections for a single class (Ground Truth = 3 objects):

| Detection # | Confidence | TP/FP |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.95 | TP |
| 2 | 0.90 | FP |
| 3 | 0.85 | TP |
| 4 | 0.70 | FP |
| 5 | 0.60 | TP |

Compute the **AP** for this class. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

| Comparison Aspect | One-Stage Detectors (YOLO, SSD) | Two-Stage Detectors (Faster R-CNN) |
|---|---|---|
| **Pipeline** | Single forward pass — direct prediction | Region proposal → classification |
| **Speed** | Real-time (45–155 FPS) | Slower (5–10 FPS) |
| **Accuracy (mAP)** | Moderate, especially for small objects | Higher, especially for small/occluded objects |
| **Localization Quality** | Less precise | More precise due to second-stage refinement |

**Justification (2 marks):**
- YOLO divides the image into an $S \times S$ grid and each cell directly predicts bounding boxes and class probabilities in one shot, hence very fast.
- Faster R-CNN first proposes ~2000 candidate regions via RPN, then performs a second-stage refinement, leading to better localization at the cost of speed.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Sort by confidence (already sorted).** (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Compute cumulative TP and FP at each detection:** (2 marks)

| # | Conf | TP | FP | Cum TP | Cum FP | Precision | Recall |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.95 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1.000 | 0.333 |
| 2 | 0.90 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0.500 | 0.333 |
| 3 | 0.85 | 1 | 0 | 2 | 1 | 0.667 | 0.667 |
| 4 | 0.70 | 0 | 1 | 2 | 2 | 0.500 | 0.667 |
| 5 | 0.60 | 1 | 0 | 3 | 2 | 0.600 | 1.000 |

**Step 3 — Compute AP using the 11-point interpolated method (Pascal VOC style):** (3 marks)

For each unique recall level $r \in \{0, 0.1, 0.2, \ldots, 1.0\}$, precision is the maximum precision at any recall $\geq r$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
AP &= \frac{1}{11} \sum_{r \in \{0.0, 0.1, \ldots, 1.0\}} P_{\text{interp}}(r)
\end{aligned}
$$

The interpolated maximums are: $P(0.0)=1.00$, $P(0.1)=1.00$, $P(0.2)=1.00$, $P(0.3)=1.00$, $P(0.4)=0.667$, $P(0.5)=0.667$, $P(0.6)=0.667$, $P(0.7)=0.667$, $P(0.8)=0.600$, $P(0.9)=0.600$, $P(1.0)=0.600$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
AP &= \frac{1}{11}(1+1+1+1+0.667+0.667+0.667+0.667+0.6+0.6+0.6) \\
&= \frac{1}{11}(8.468) \\
&\approx 0.770
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Final mAP (1 mark):** For a single class, $\text{mAP} = AP \approx \mathbf{77.0\%}$.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing IoU threshold with confidence threshold** — they are *different*. IoU is for box overlap evaluation; confidence is for the model's certainty about a class.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply `max(0, x)`** in IoU intersection step — yields negative areas and wrong IoU.
> 3. **Misinterpreting TP/FP in mAP computation** — a TP requires BOTH correct class AND IoU $\geq$ threshold.
> 4. **Using raw `|` symbol in markdown tables** — breaks PDF compilation; always use `\vert` or `\mid`.
> 5. **Skipping the NMS explanation** when asked about post-processing — at least 1–2 marks are reserved for NMS in nearly every detection question.
> 6. **Stating "R-CNN uses YOLO" or similar nonsense** — examiners instantly flag factual errors. Stick to verified, well-established architectures.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Object Detection** = Classification + Localization + Multi-instance handling.
- ✅ **Bounding Box representation** is usually $(x, y, w, h)$ or $(x_{\min}, y_{\min}, x_{\max}, y_{\max})$.
- ✅ **IoU formula:** $\dfrac{A_{\text{inter}}}{A_{\text{union}}}$, with values in $[0, 1]$; threshold $\mathbf{0.5}$ is standard.
- ✅ **Precision** = TP / (TP + FP); **Recall** = TP / (TP + FN).
- ✅ **AP** = Area under Precision–Recall curve; **mAP** = mean of AP across all classes.
- ✅ **Two-stage detectors** (R-CNN family): High accuracy, slow speed.
- ✅ **One-stage detectors** (YOLO, SSD): Real-time, slightly lower accuracy.
- ✅ **NMS** is mandatory post-processing — eliminates duplicate detections using IoU and confidence thresholds.
- ✅ **Anchor boxes** are pre-defined priors used to predict variable-shaped objects.
- ✅ **Region Proposal Network (RPN)** is the heart of Faster R-CNN — generates ~2000 candidates per image.
- ✅ **YOLO grid division:** Image split into $S \times S$ grid; each cell predicts $B$ boxes and $C$ class probabilities.
- ✅ **Always use `\vert` or `\mid`** for absolute value in markdown tables — never the pipe symbol.
- ✅ **Mention both Faster R-CNN (2015) and YOLO (2016)** as canonical examples in any comparison answer.
- ✅ **Faster R-CNN components** to memorize: Backbone CNN → RPN → ROI Pooling → Classifier + Regressor.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pattern Analysis: Clustering: K-Means, Mixture of Gaussians, Classification: Discriminant Function, Supervised, Un-supervised, Semi-supervised
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBRT635 - MACHINE VISION
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pattern Analysis: Clustering: K-Means, Mixture of Gaussians, Classification: Discriminant Function, Supervised, Un-supervised, Semi-supervised

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Pattern Analysis – Formal Definition

In the context of Machine Vision, **Pattern Analysis** is the computational process of detecting, extracting, and interpreting regularities (patterns) within visual data such as pixels, features, or image descriptors. The objective is to assign meaningful labels, groupings, or decisions to incoming data based on learned or derived statistical/structural properties of the patterns.

$$ \text{Pattern Analysis} = f(\mathcal{X}) \;\longrightarrow\; y $$

where $\mathcal{X} = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_N\}$ is the input feature set (extracted from images) and $y$ represents the corresponding decision, label, or cluster identity.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Module 4 emphasizes two principal pillars of Pattern Analysis — *Clustering* (unsupervised grouping) and *Classification* (supervised/semi-supervised decision-making). Both rely on a **Discriminant Function** that mathematically separates regions in feature space.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Sorting Room" Intuition

Imagine you walk into a warehouse containing 10,000 unsorted mixed fruits. You have **three possible strategies**:

| Strategy | Real-World Equivalent | ML Counterpart |
|----------|----------------------|----------------|
| A librarian hands you a chart saying "this is apple, this is mango" → you sort | **Supervised Classification** | Labels provided |
| No chart, but you group by similarity (color, shape) yourself | **Unsupervised Clustering** | No labels |
| Chart for some fruits, you guess the rest using similarity | **Semi-Supervised Learning** | Few labels |

This mental model is the entire philosophical backbone of Pattern Analysis. The K-Means algorithm is your **"sort by similarity" brain**, Mixture of Gaussians is your **"probabilistic version with uncertainty" brain**, and the Discriminant Function is your **"decision rulebook"**.

---

## 1.3 Clustering – Formal Definition

**Clustering** is the unsupervised partitioning of a dataset $\mathcal{X} = \{x_1, \ldots, x_N\}$ into $K$ disjoint groups (clusters) $\{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_K\}$ such that:

$$ C_i \cap C_j = \emptyset \;\; \forall\, i \neq j, \quad \bigcup_{i=1}^{K} C_i = \mathcal{X} $$

The grouping criterion is typically intra-cluster similarity (compactness) and inter-cluster dissimilarity (separation).

---

## 1.4 K-Means Clustering – Definition

**K-Means** is an iterative, centroid-based partitioning clustering algorithm that minimizes the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)**, also called the *distortion* or *inertia*:

$$ J = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2 $$

where $\mu_i$ is the centroid (mean) of cluster $C_i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** K-Means is *hard clustering* — every data point belongs to **exactly one** cluster (probabilistic assignment = 1 for winner, 0 otherwise). This is its key distinction from **Mixture of Gaussians (soft clustering)**.

---

## 1.5 Mixture of Gaussians (GMM) – Definition

A **Mixture of Gaussians** models the data distribution as a weighted sum of $K$ multivariate Gaussian components:

$$ p(x) = \sum_{i=1}^{K} \pi_i \, \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_i, \Sigma_i) $$

where:
- $\pi_i$ = mixing coefficient (prior weight) of component $i$, with $\sum_i \pi_i = 1$
- $\mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_i, \Sigma_i)$ = multivariate Gaussian PDF with mean $\mu_i$ and covariance $\Sigma_i$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** GMM answers *"How probable is it that this point came from Gaussian cloud #1 vs. #2 vs. #3?"* — yielding **soft (probabilistic) assignments** rather than K-Means' hard binary assignments.

---

## 1.6 Classification – Formal Definition

**Classification** is the supervised task of learning a mapping $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$, where $\mathcal{Y} = \{1, 2, \ldots, M\}$ is a discrete label set, using a labeled training set $\{(x_n, y_n)\}_{n=1}^{N}$.

The mapping is realized through a **Discriminant Function** $g_i(x)$ for each class $i$; the decision rule is:

$$ \hat{y} = \arg\max_{i} \, g_i(x) $$

---

## 1.7 Discriminant Function – Definition

A **Discriminant Function** $g_i(x)$ is a scalar-valued function that quantifies the evidence that observation $x$ belongs to class $\omega_i$. Common choices:

| Type | Form | Decision Boundary |
|------|------|-------------------|
| Linear Discriminant | $g_i(x) = w_i^T x + w_{i0}$ | Hyperplane |
| Quadratic Discriminant | $g_i(x) = x^T W_i x + w_i^T x + w_{i0}$ | Quadric surface |
| Bayes Optimal | $g_i(x) = P(\omega_i \mid x) = P(x \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i)$ | Optimal (min error) |

---

## 1.8 Learning Paradigms – Supervised, Unsupervised, Semi-Supervised

### Supervised Learning
- **Definition:** The training set $\mathcal{D} = \{(x_n, y_n)\}_{n=1}^{N}$ contains input–output pairs (labels).
- **Goal:** Learn $f: \mathcal{X} \rightarrow \mathcal{Y}$.
- **Examples:** SVM, Neural Networks, Decision Trees.
- **Machine Vision Use:** Object recognition with labeled image datasets (e.g., ImageNet).

### Unsupervised Learning
- **Definition:** The training set contains only inputs $\mathcal{D} = \{x_n\}_{n=1}^{N}$ — **no labels**.
- **Goal:** Discover hidden structure, groupings, or density.
- **Examples:** K-Means, GMM, PCA, Autoencoders.
- **Machine Vision Use:** Image segmentation, anomaly detection.

### Semi-Supervised Learning
- **Definition:** A *small* labeled set $\mathcal{D}_L = \{(x_n, y_n)\}$ plus a *large* unlabeled set $\mathcal{D}_U = \{x_n\}$.
- **Goal:** Leverage unlabeled data to improve a classifier beyond pure supervised performance.
- **Examples:** Self-training, Co-training, Graph-based label propagation.
- **Machine Vision Use:** Medical imaging where expert annotation is expensive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favorite:** Examiners frequently ask students to *contrast* the three paradigms in a 3-mark or 7-mark question. Memorize the *presence/absence of labels* as the defining axis.

---

## 1.9 Taxonomy Overview – One-Page Mind Map

> The diagram in **SECTION 4** provides the complete hierarchical taxonomy. Refer to it after reading the deep theory in **SECTION 2**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 K-Means Clustering – Operational Breakdown

K-Means operates via an **Expectation–Maximization-style two-step iteration** (though it is *not* the EM algorithm proper — see GMM section):

### Step-by-Step Logic

1. **Initialization:** Randomly select $K$ initial centroids $\{\mu_1^{(0)}, \mu_2^{(0)}, \ldots, \mu_K^{(0)}\}$ from the data points (or use K-Means++ seeding).
2. **Assignment Step (E-step analogue):** For each data point $x_n$, assign it to the nearest centroid using squared Euclidean distance:
$$ C_i^{(t)} = \left\{ x_n : \Vert x_n - \mu_i^{(t)} \Vert^2 \leq \Vert x_n - \mu_j^{(t)} \Vert^2 \; \forall j \right\} $$
3. **Update Step (M-step analogue):** Recompute each centroid as the mean of all points assigned to it:
$$ \mu_i^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{|C_i^{(t)}|} \sum_{x_n \in C_i^{(t)}} x_n $$
4. **Convergence Check:** Iterate Steps 2–3 until cluster assignments no longer change, or until $J$ drops below a tolerance $\epsilon$.

### The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Assignment step** minimizes intra-cluster distance *given fixed centroids*.
- **Update step** minimizes $J$ *given fixed assignments* — taking the derivative of $J$ w.r.t. $\mu_i$ yields exactly the mean.
- K-Means is therefore a **coordinate descent** on the non-convex objective $J$, and is only guaranteed to find a **local minimum**.

### K-Means++ Initialization (Improvement over Random)

To avoid poor local minima, K-Means++ selects the first centroid uniformly at random, then each subsequent centroid $x$ is chosen with probability proportional to $D(x)^2$, where $D(x)$ is the distance to the nearest already-chosen centroid.

$$ P(x \text{ chosen as } \mu_i) = \frac{D(x)^2}{\sum_{x'} D(x')^2} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** K-Means is the workhorse for **color quantization** in image compression (reducing thousands of colors to $K$ representative colors) and for **image segmentation** by clustering pixel feature vectors (RGB, HSV, or texture descriptors).

---

## 2.2 Mixture of Gaussians (GMM) – Operational Breakdown

GMM is fit using the genuine **Expectation-Maximization (EM) Algorithm**, which finds Maximum Likelihood Estimates (MLE) in the presence of latent (hidden) variables — the cluster identities.

### Latent Variable Formulation

Introduce a latent indicator $z_n \in \{1, \ldots, K\}$ such that $z_n = i$ means $x_n$ was generated by Gaussian component $i$. The joint distribution factors as:

$$ p(x_n, z_n = i) = \pi_i \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_i, \Sigma_i) $$

The **posterior responsibility** that component $i$ takes for point $x_n$ is:

$$ \gamma(z_{ni}) = p(z_n = i \mid x_n) = \frac{\pi_i \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_i, \Sigma_i)}{\sum_{j=1}^{K} \pi_j \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)} $$

### E-Step and M-Step

**E-Step (Expectation):** Compute responsibilities $\gamma(z_{ni})$ using current parameter estimates.

**M-Step (Maximization):** Re-estimate parameters:

$$ \mu_i^{\text{new}} = \frac{\sum_n \gamma(z_{ni}) \, x_n}{\sum_n \gamma(z_{ni})} $$

$$ \Sigma_i^{\text{new}} = \frac{\sum_n \gamma(z_{ni}) \, (x_n - \mu_i^{\text{new}})(x_n - \mu_i^{\text{new}})^T}{\sum_n \gamma(z_{ni})} $$

$$ \pi_i^{\text{new}} = \frac{\sum_n \gamma(z_{ni})}{N} $$

### Convergence

EM monotonically increases the data log-likelihood (or equivalently, the free energy lower bound):

$$ \ell(\theta) = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \ln \left( \sum_{i=1}^{K} \pi_i \, \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_i, \Sigma_i) \right) $$

until convergence to a local maximum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Distinction:** K-Means is the **hard-limit special case** of GMM when covariances $\Sigma_i = \epsilon I$ (isotropic, equal variance) and responsibilities collapse to 0/1 values. This is a frequently asked conceptual question.

---

## 2.3 Discriminant Function – Deep Theory

### Bayes' Decision Theory Foundation

The optimal discriminant (minimizing classification error) is the **posterior probability**:

$$ g_i(x) = P(\omega_i \mid x) = \frac{p(x \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i)}{p(x)} $$

Since $p(x)$ is common to all classes, an equivalent discriminant is:

$$ g_i(x) = p(x \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i) $$

or in log form (numerically stable):

$$ g_i(x) = \ln p(x \mid \omega_i) + \ln P(\omega_i) $$

### Case 1: Gaussian Class-Conditional with Equal Covariance (Linear Discriminant Analysis, LDA)

For $p(x \mid \omega_i) = \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_i, \Sigma)$ (shared $\Sigma$):

$$ g_i(x) = -\frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_i)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu_i) + \ln P(\omega_i) + \text{const.} $$

Expanding the quadratic term, the $x^T \Sigma^{-1} x$ cancels across classes, leaving a **linear** discriminant:

$$ g_i(x) = w_i^T x + w_{i0} $$

where:
$$ w_i = \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i, \quad w_{i0} = -\frac{1}{2} \mu_i^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i + \ln P(\omega_i) $$

The decision boundary between classes $i$ and $j$ is the hyperplane $g_i(x) = g_j(x)$.

### Case 2: Gaussian Class-Conditional with Different Covariances (QDA)

If each class has its own $\Sigma_i$, the discriminant is **quadratic**:

$$ g_i(x) = -\frac{1}{2} \ln \vert \Sigma_i \vert - \frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1} (x - \mu_i) + \ln P(\omega_i) $$

Decision boundaries become quadrics (parabolas, ellipses, hyperbolas).

---

## 2.4 Semi-Supervised Learning – Deep Theory

### Why Semi-Supervised?

In Machine Vision, labels are expensive (expert annotation). Unlabeled images are cheap. SSL exploits the **manifold assumption** (data lies on a low-dimensional manifold) and the **cluster assumption** (same cluster → same label).

### Common SSL Algorithms

| Algorithm | Mechanism |
|-----------|-----------|
| Self-Training | Train on labeled → predict on unlabeled → add high-confidence predictions to training set |
| Co-Training | Two views of data; each trains the other on its confident predictions |
| Label Propagation | Graph-based; labels diffuse along high-density regions |
| Semi-Supervised SVM (S3VM) | Extends SVM margin to unlabeled data |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | K-Means Objective | $J = \sum_i \sum_{x \in C_i} \Vert x - \mu_i \Vert^2$ | WCSS / Inertia |
| 2 | Centroid Update | $\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{x \in C_i} x$ | M-step |
| 3 | GMM Density | $p(x) = \sum_i \pi_i \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_i, \Sigma_i)$ | Soft clustering |
| 4 | Mixing Constraint | $\sum_i \pi_i = 1, \quad \pi_i \geq 0$ | Probability simplex |
| 5 | GMM Responsibility | $\gamma(z_{ni}) = \frac{\pi_i \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_i, \Sigma_i)}{\sum_j \pi_j \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_j, \Sigma_j)}$ | E-step |
| 6 | GMM Mean Update | $\mu_i = \frac{\sum_n \gamma(z_{ni}) x_n}{\sum_n \gamma(z_{ni})}$ | M-step |
| 7 | GMM Covariance Update | $\Sigma_i = \frac{\sum_n \gamma(z_{ni})(x_n - \mu_i)(x_n - \mu_i)^T}{\sum_n \gamma(z_{ni})}$ | M-step |
| 8 | Bayes Discriminant | $g_i(x) = p(x \mid \omega_i) P(\omega_i)$ | Optimal |
| 9 | LDA Weight | $w_i = \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i$ | Linear boundary |
| 10 | LDA Bias | $w_{i0} = -\frac{1}{2} \mu_i^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i + \ln P(\omega_i)$ | With prior |
| 11 | QDA Discriminant | $g_i(x) = -\frac{1}{2} \ln \vert \Sigma_i \vert - \frac{1}{2}(x-\mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1}(x-\mu_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)$ | Quadratic |
| 12 | Decision Rule | $\hat{y} = \arg\max_i g_i(x)$ | Classification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Memorize formulas #1, #3, #5, and #8. They appear in nearly every KTU Pattern Analysis question paper.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **K-Means:** Used in **medical image segmentation** (clustering MRI voxels by intensity), **video compression** (key-frame clustering), and **background subtraction** in surveillance.
- **GMM:** Used in **adaptive background modeling** (e.g., the famous Stauffer–Grimson algorithm for foreground detection), **speaker identification**, and **skin-color modeling** for face detection.
- **LDA:** Used in **face recognition** (Fisherfaces), **retrieval** of similar images.
- **SSL:** Used in **semantic segmentation** where only a few pixels are labeled, and the model propagates labels across visually similar regions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: K-Means Centroid Update Minimizes WCSS

**Goal:** Show that updating $\mu_i$ to the cluster mean minimizes $J$ given fixed assignments.

**Setup:** Let $C_i = \{x_1^{(i)}, x_2^{(i)}, \ldots, x_{N_i}^{(i)}\}$ be the points currently assigned to cluster $i$. The contribution to $J$ from cluster $i$ alone is:

$$ J_i = \sum_{n=1}^{N_i} \Vert x_n^{(i)} - \mu_i \Vert^2 $$

We minimize $J_i$ w.r.t. $\mu_i$. Setting $\nabla_{\mu_i} J_i = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_i &= \sum_{n=1}^{N_i} (x_n^{(i)} - \mu_i)^T (x_n^{(i)} - \mu_i) \\
&= \sum_{n=1}^{N_i} \left[ x_n^{(i)\,T} x_n^{(i)} - 2 \mu_i^T x_n^{(i)} + \mu_i^T \mu_i \right] \\
\nabla_{\mu_i} J_i &= \sum_{n=1}^{N_i} \left[ -2 x_n^{(i)} + 2 \mu_i \right] = 0 \\
\therefore \quad \mu_i &= \frac{1}{N_i} \sum_{n=1}^{N_i} x_n^{(i)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Justification (Text Row):** Differentiating the squared norm w.r.t. $\mu_i$ gives twice the negative of the sum of residuals plus $2 N_i \mu_i$. Setting to zero yields the arithmetic mean. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: GMM M-Step Updates (Parameter Maximization)

**Goal:** Derive the M-step updates for GMM parameters from the expected complete-data log-likelihood.

### Step 1: Complete-Data Log-Likelihood

If we *knew* the latent $z_n$ for every point:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln p(X, Z \mid \theta) &= \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \mathbb{1}(z_n = i) \left[ \ln \pi_i + \ln \mathcal{N}(x_n \mid \mu_i, \Sigma_i) \right] \\
&= \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \mathbb{1}(z_n = i) \left[ \ln \pi_i - \frac{D}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma_i \vert - \frac{1}{2}(x_n - \mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1}(x_n - \mu_i) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2: Take the Expectation w.r.t. Posterior

Replace $\mathbb{1}(z_n = i)$ with its expectation $\gamma(z_{ni})$ (the responsibility):

$$ \mathbb{E}_{Z \mid X, \theta^{\text{old}}}[\ln p(X, Z \mid \theta)] = \sum_{n=1}^{N} \sum_{i=1}^{K} \gamma(z_{ni}) \left[ \ln \pi_i - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma_i \vert - \frac{1}{2}(x_n - \mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1}(x_n - \mu_i) \right] + \text{const.} $$

### Step 3: Differentiate w.r.t. $\mu_i$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\mu_i} \, \mathbb{E}[\cdot] &= -\sum_{n=1}^{N} \gamma(z_{ni}) \, \Sigma_i^{-1}(x_n - \mu_i) = 0 \\
\therefore \quad \Sigma_i^{-1} \sum_n \gamma(z_{ni})(x_n - \mu_i) &= 0 \\
\therefore \quad \mu_i^{\text{new}} &= \frac{\sum_n \gamma(z_{ni}) \, x_n}{\sum_n \gamma(z_{ni})}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Justification:** Multiply both sides by $\Sigma_i$ (invertible), distribute the sum, isolate $\mu_i$. The denominator $N_i^{\text{eff}} = \sum_n \gamma(z_{ni})$ is the **effective number of points** assigned (softly) to component $i$.

### Step 4: Differentiate w.r.t. $\Sigma_i$

Using matrix calculus identity $\nabla_{\Sigma} \ln \vert \Sigma \vert = (\Sigma^T)^{-1}$ and $\nabla_{\Sigma} \text{tr}(\Sigma^{-1} A) = -\Sigma^{-1} A \Sigma^{-1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\nabla_{\Sigma_i} \, \mathbb{E}[\cdot] &= -\frac{1}{2}\sum_n \gamma(z_{ni}) \left[ \Sigma_i^{-1} - \Sigma_i^{-1}(x_n - \mu_i)(x_n - \mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1} \right] = 0 \\
\therefore \quad \Sigma_i^{\text{new}} &= \frac{\sum_n \gamma(z_{ni}) (x_n - \mu_i^{\text{new}})(x_n - \mu_i^{\text{new}})^T}{\sum_n \gamma(z_{ni})}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5: Differentiate w.r.t. $\pi_i$ with Lagrange Multiplier

Maximize $\sum_n \sum_i \gamma(z_{ni}) \ln \pi_i$ subject to $\sum_i \pi_i = 1$ via Lagrange multiplier $\lambda$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{L} &= \sum_n \sum_i \gamma(z_{ni}) \ln \pi_i + \lambda \left(1 - \sum_i \pi_i \right) \\
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \pi_i} &= \frac{\sum_n \gamma(z_{ni})}{\pi_i} - \lambda = 0 \\
\therefore \quad \pi_i &= \frac{\sum_n \gamma(z_{ni})}{\lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing over $i$: $\sum_i \pi_i = \frac{\sum_i \sum_n \gamma(z_{ni})}{\lambda} = \frac{N}{\lambda} = 1$, so $\lambda = N$.

$$
\therefore \quad \pi_i^{\text{new}} = \frac{\sum_n \gamma(z_{ni})}{N}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: LDA Decision Boundary

**Goal:** Show that with shared covariance, the discriminant is linear.

**Setup:** $p(x \mid \omega_i) = \mathcal{N}(x \mid \mu_i, \Sigma)$ with shared $\Sigma$. The log-discriminant is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_i(x) &= \ln p(x \mid \omega_i) + \ln P(\omega_i) \\
&= -\frac{D}{2}\ln(2\pi) - \frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma \vert - \frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_i)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu_i) + \ln P(\omega_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

The terms $-\frac{D}{2}\ln(2\pi)$ and $-\frac{1}{2}\ln \vert \Sigma \vert$ are class-independent constants (can be dropped):

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_i(x) &= -\frac{1}{2}(x - \mu_i)^T \Sigma^{-1} (x - \mu_i) + \ln P(\omega_i) \\
&= -\frac{1}{2}\left[ x^T \Sigma^{-1} x - 2 x^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i + \mu_i^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i \right] + \ln P(\omega_i) \\
&= -\frac{1}{2} x^T \Sigma^{-1} x + x^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i - \frac{1}{2} \mu_i^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i + \ln P(\omega_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

The first term $-\frac{1}{2} x^T \Sigma^{-1} x$ is class-independent (depends only on $x$, not $i$). Dropping it:

$$ g_i(x) = \underbrace{\Sigma^{-1} \mu_i}_{w_i} \,^T x + \underbrace{\left( -\frac{1}{2} \mu_i^T \Sigma^{-1} \mu_i + \ln P(\omega_i) \right)}_{w_{i0}} $$

Hence $g_i(x) = w_i^T x + w_{i0}$ — **linear in $x$**. The decision boundary $g_i(x) = g_j(x)$ is therefore a **hyperplane**. $\blacksquare$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | K-Means | GMM (EM) | LDA | QDA |
|----------|---------|----------|-----|-----|
| Clustering / Classification | Clustering | Clustering | Classification | Classification |
| Hard vs Soft | Hard | Soft (probabilistic) | Hard decision | Hard decision |
| Requires Labels | No | No | Yes | Yes |
| Covariance Assumption | Isotropic (implicit) | Full per component | Shared $\Sigma$ | Per-class $\Sigma_i$ |
| Boundary Shape | Voronoi | Curved (Gaussian) | Linear (hyperplane) | Quadratic |
| Objective | WCSS minimization | Log-likelihood maximization | Posterior maximization | Posterior maximization |
| Convergence Guarantee | Local minimum | Local maximum | Closed-form | Closed-form |
| Computational Cost per Iteration | $\mathcal{O}(NKD)$ | $\mathcal{O}(NKD^3)$ | $\mathcal{O}(D^3)$ one-shot | $\mathcal{O}(KD^3)$ one-shot |
| Initialization Sensitive | Yes (use K-Means++) | Yes (use K-Means first) | No (closed-form) | No (closed-form) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Hierarchical Taxonomy of Pattern Analysis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PA["Pattern Analysis"]
    PA --> CL["Clustering - Unsupervised"]
    PA --> CF["Classification - Supervised"]
    PA --> SSL["Semi-Supervised Learning"]

    CL --> KM["K-Means - Hard Centroid Based"]
    CL --> GMM["Mixture of Gaussians - Soft Probabilistic"]
    CL --> HC["Hierarchical - Agglomerative Divisive"]
    CL --> DBSCAN["Density Based - DBSCAN OPTICS"]

    CF --> DF["Discriminant Function Based"]
    DF --> LIN["Linear - LDA Perceptron SVM"]
    DF --> QUA["Quadratic - QDA"]

    CF --> GEN["Generative Models"]
    GEN --> NB["Naive Bayes"]
    GEN --> GMMCLS["GMM Classifier"]

    CF --> DIS["Discriminative Models"]
    DIS --> KNN["K Nearest Neighbors"]
    DIS --> SVM["Support Vector Machine"]
    DIS --> NN["Neural Networks"]

    SSL --> ST["Self Training"]
    SSL --> CT["Co Training"]
    SSL --> LP["Label Propagation Graph Based"]
    SSL --> S3VM["Semi Supervised SVM"]
```

> **Diagram Note:** This taxonomy mirrors the KTU 2024 syllabus organization for Module 4. K-Means and GMM appear under *Clustering*; Discriminant Functions appear under *Classification*; Supervised/Unsupervised/Semi-Supervised are the three top-level *Learning Paradigms*.

---

## 4.2 K-Means Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0["Start K-Means Algorithm"] --> A1["Input Data X with N points and choose K"]
    A1 --> A2["Initialize K Centroids - K-Means Plus Plus seeding"]
    A2 --> A3{"Iteration Loop Begin"}
    A3 --> A4["Assignment Step: For each x_n assign to nearest centroid"]
    A4 --> A5["Update Step: Recompute each centroid as mean of its cluster"]
    A5 --> A6{"Convergence Check: Assignments unchanged OR J change below epsilon"}
    A6 -->|"No"| A3
    A6 -->|"Yes"| A7["Output Final Centroids and Cluster Assignments"]
    A7 --> A8["End"]
```

---

## 4.3 GMM Expectation-Maximization Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B0["Start GMM EM"] --> B1["Initialize mu, Sigma, pi - often via K-Means"]
    B1 --> B2["E-Step: Compute responsibility gamma for every point and component"]
    B2 --> B3["M-Step: Update mu using responsibility weighted mean"]
    B3 --> B4["M-Step: Update Sigma using responsibility weighted scatter"]
    B4 --> B5["M-Step: Update pi as average responsibility per component"]
    B5 --> B6{"Convergence: log-likelihood change below tolerance"}
    B6 -->|"No"| B2
    B6 -->|"Yes"| B7["Output GMM parameters and soft assignments"]
    B7 --> B8["End"]
```

---

## 4.4 Discriminant Function Decision Process

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F0["Input Feature Vector x"] --> F1["Compute g1 x - class 1 evidence"]
    F0 --> F2["Compute g2 x - class 2 evidence"]
    F0 --> F3["Compute gM x - class M evidence"]
    F1 --> F4["Argmax over all classes"]
    F2 --> F4
    F3 --> F4
    F4 --> F5["Predicted Label y_hat"]
```

---

## 4.5 Learning Paradigm Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0["Pattern Analysis Pipelines"] --> P1["Supervised - Labeled Data Only"]
    P0 --> P2["Unsupervised - No Labels"]
    P0 --> P3["Semi-Supervised - Few Labels Plus Many Unlabeled"]

    P1 --> P1A["Training: minimize loss on labeled set"]
    P1 --> P1B["Inference: predict label for new x"]
    P1A --> P1B

    P2 --> P2A["Discovery: find clusters or density"]
    P2 --> P2B["No prediction of predefined labels"]

    P3 --> P3A["Bootstrap: train classifier on labeled"]
    P3 --> P3B["Expand: label confident unlabeled samples"]
    P3 --> P3C["Retrain: use enlarged training set"]
    P3A --> P3B
    P3B --> P3C
```
